3.content_md:
# Chapter 3 — Alarm Management and Triage

| Chapter Overview
Operational Objectives: Master the complete DWDM and OTN alarm taxonomy. Develop the ability to identify root-cause alarms within alarm storms of hundreds of simultaneous active conditions. Understand alarm correlation, false alarm management, and alarm inhibition discipline.
Field Competencies: Alarm taxonomy application; root-cause identification under pressure; alarm storm correlation; TCA/inhibition configuration.
Real-World Context: In a major trunk cable cut on a 96-channel system, the NMS will generate 400–600 alarms within 30 seconds. The architect who does not have alarm taxonomy internalized will spend 10 minutes reading alarm descriptions. The architect who has it internalized will identify the fiber cut within 60 seconds from the alarm pattern alone. |
|---|

## 3.1 Alarm Taxonomy: Root-Cause vs. Symptom Alarms
Every alarm on a DWDM network is either a **root-cause alarm** (indicating the actual failure) or a **symptom alarm** (indicating a downstream consequence of the root cause). Acting on symptom alarms — trying to fix the downstream effect rather than the originating fault — is the most common triage error in optical networks. The production architect must immediately classify every alarm into one of these categories.
General rules for classification:
- Alarms that appear simultaneously on many channels/ports at the same node are almost always symptomatic of a single upstream failure
- Alarms that appear on a single span in both directions simultaneously suggest a fiber plant failure (root cause)
- Alarms that appear on a single channel but not adjacent channels suggest a transponder or per-channel failure (root cause)
- Alarms at the far end of a span that have corresponding alarms at the near end are symptomatic of the near-end condition
## 3.2 Optical Alarm Hierarchy
Optical layer alarms form a strict hierarchy. Understanding the propagation path of alarms allows the architect to identify which alarm is root-cause and which are downstream effects.

| Alarm | Full Name | Layer | Trigger Condition | Root-Cause or Symptom? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| LOS | Loss of Signal | Physical / OTN section | Received optical power below detector threshold (typically –30 dBm) | Can be root cause (fiber cut, TX failure) or symptom (upstream failure) |
| LOF | Loss of Frame | OTN OTUk | OTUk framing cannot be locked on received signal | Usually symptom of LOS or severe signal degradation |
| OOF | Out of Frame | OTN OTUk | Transient framing loss; precursor to LOF if persistent (>3 ms) | Symptom; transient events may be impulsive events on fiber |
| AIS | Alarm Indication Signal | OTN ODUk | Upstream LOS or LOF causes NE to insert all-ones pattern downstream | Always symptomatic; indicates upstream failure insertion point |
| RDI | Remote Defect Indication | OTN ODUk / OTUk | Far-end NE reports it is receiving a defective signal (inserted in return direction overhead) | Symptom; confirms far end is affected. Root cause is in the near-to-far direction |
| DEG | Signal Degrade | OTN / optical | BER exceeds DEG threshold (typically 10⁻⁶) but signal is not lost | Can be root cause (fiber degradation, component failure) or symptom (OSNR degradation propagating) |
| BDI | Backward Defect Indication | OTN TCM | TCM-layer indication sent in reverse direction confirming defect reception | Always symptom; used for TCM health confirmation signaling |
| LOM | Loss of Multiframe | OTN ODUk | ODUk multiframe alignment lost | Symptom of severe upstream impairment or framing error |

## 3.3 OTN Alarm Set: End-to-End Propagation
Per **ITU-T G.798**, OTN alarms propagate through a defined hierarchy. Understanding this propagation is essential for identifying where in the network the fault has occurred. The key OTN alarms and their propagation behavior:

| Alarm | Layer | Propagation Behavior | Root Cause Indication |
|---|---|---|---|
| OTUk-LOM | OTUk (section) | Local alarm; causes ODUk-AIS insertion downstream | OTUk framing error — fiber section or regenerator failure |
| ODUk-AIS | ODUk (path) | Propagates end-to-end on ODUk path; appears at every downstream NE | Never root cause — symptom of upstream OTUk-LOM or LOS |
| ODUk-OCI | ODUk (path) | Open Connection Indication — upstream NE is not connected to this path | Provisioning mismatch; switch to unconnected port; cross-connect error |
| ODUk-LOCK | ODUk (path) | Signal is being intentionally locked (maintenance signal) | Deliberate operational action — should correspond to active MOP |
| OTUk-BDI | OTUk (section) | Returned from far-end in OTUk overhead; confirms far-end is affected | Root cause is in the toward-far-end direction. Look at near-end TX. |
| TCMi-LTC | ODUk TCM sublayer | Loss of Tandem Connection; TCM sublayer cannot be monitored | TCM-layer discontinuity; possible fiber break in TCM-monitored segment |

## 3.4 Alarm Storms: Identifying Root Cause from 200 Simultaneous Alarms
An **alarm storm** is a large number of alarms generated simultaneously following a single triggering failure. On a 96-channel DWDM system, a single trunk fiber cut can generate 500–800 individual alarm events within 10 seconds. The architect must have a method for reducing this to a single root-cause event within the first 60–90 seconds.
**The Manual Correlation Decision Tree for Alarm Storms:**
- Sort by timestamp. The first alarm(s) to appear are statistically most likely to be root-cause. Symptomatic alarms appear 100–500 ms later as AIS/RDI signals propagate.
- Group by physical location. If all alarms are associated with channels traversing Span 7, the problem is on Span 7 — not at the endpoints.
- Look for LOS at a specific port. An Optical Amplifier LOS on a specific input port, appearing 100 ms before all the per-channel alarms, is your root-cause alarm.
- Check if protection has switched. If APS switched successfully, the root cause is on the working path and can be investigated without urgency. If APS did not switch, the priority shifts to understanding why.
- Count the scope. All channels on a trunk = trunk-level failure (fiber cut, ILA loss). Subset of channels = client-side, WSS, or per-channel issue. Single channel = transponder or per-channel failure.
- Check OSC alarm status. OSC (Optical Supervisory Channel) loss between two specific nodes confirms the fiber span between those nodes is the root cause. OSC is carried separately from traffic channels and is the most reliable span-level diagnostic.

| 💡 Operational Tip: Suppress After You Identify
Once you have identified the root-cause alarm, use alarm correlation / suppression in the NMS to suppress all downstream symptomatic alarms from your console view. Working in a 200-alarm NOC view while you are executing a repair procedure introduces error. Reduce your active alarm view to the root-cause alarm plus any protection/switching status alarms before beginning MOP execution. |
|---|

## 3.5 False Alarms: Causes, Identification, and Suppression Strategy
A **false alarm** (or **nuisance alarm**) is an alarm generated by a real condition that does not represent an actual network fault. Common causes in DWDM networks:
- Protection switch tests not fully coordinated with NMS: A manual protection test that causes APS to switch will generate APS alarms and potentially DEG alarms on the working path. If the test is not properly reflected in the alarm management system as a scheduled maintenance event, Tier 1 NOC will treat these as real faults.
- Temperature-induced optical power variation: Outdoor fiber plants, particularly aerial cables, experience daily thermal cycling that causes fiber attenuation to vary by 0.2–0.5 dB. If TCA thresholds are set too tightly, this generates daily TCAs that are not faults.
- Software-generated alarms during NMS resync: When the NMS reconnects to an NE after a communication interruption, the NE re-reports all active conditions — including conditions that were already known and acknowledged. This creates duplicate alarms that have no operational significance.
- EDFA APC hunting: Gain-controlled EDFAs with tight APC deadbands can oscillate slightly around the gain setpoint, generating minor OPT fluctuations that trigger power-based TCAs.
**Alarm inhibition** is the deliberate suppression of specific alarm types on specific resources during defined time periods. It must be used conservatively — only for confirmed false-alarm sources, only for the minimum time window necessary, and always with an automatic expiration time. Permanent alarm inhibitions that are forgotten are a significant operational hazard: they can mask real failures.

| 📁 Case Study 3.1 — Trunk Fiber Cut: 96-Channel Alarm Storm Triage
Network Context: Metropolitan Area Network, ring topology with 6 nodes (Node A through F). Trunk fiber between Node B and Node C: 45 km aerial ADSS cable. 96 channels loaded (100G DP-QPSK). Full Optical 1+1 protection. NMS is OneControl (multi-vendor).
T+00:00 — Alarm storm begins: 547 alarms appear in NOC console within 8 seconds. NMS correlation engine reduces visible count to 87 correlated alarm groups, but NOC Tier 1 is overwhelmed.
T+00:45 — Architect engaged. Actions:
Step 1: Filter NMS by alarm timestamp. First alarm: "OSC LOS — Node B to Node C, TX direction" at T+00:00.312. Second alarm: "Optical Amplifier Input LOS — Node C, Span B-C input port" at T+00:00.398. All per-channel alarms follow at T+00:00.500 to T+00:00.800.
Step 2: Scope determination — all 96 channels affected. Both OSC and traffic amplifier show simultaneous LOS at Node C input from Node B. This is not a per-channel issue. This is a complete fiber span failure on the B-to-C trunk.
Step 3: Protection status — confirm APS has activated. APS switch logged at T+00:00.485. All 96 channels rerouted via Node A (C-to-A-to-B alternate path). Protection switch time: 47 ms. Within 50 ms SLA ✓.
Step 4: Alarm storm reduction — architect applies maintenance suppression to all symptomatic per-channel alarms on the B-C span. Active alarm view reduced to 3 alarms: OSC LOS (B-C), AMP Input LOS (Node C Span BC), APS active (both directions).
T+02:30 — Root Cause Confirmed: Field crew dispatched. OTDR test from Node B confirms fiber break at kilometer 23.4 of the B-to-C span (aerial cable mechanical failure — wind-induced oscillation fatigue). Repair crew ETA: 4 hours.
T+04:45 — Fiber repaired, splice tested: Splice loss 0.12 dB (within 0.15 dB acceptance criterion). Optical power restored on B-to-C span. APS cleared, channels reverted to working path.
T+05:10 — Verification complete: All 96 channels verified on working path. PM data reviewed — 47 minutes on protect path (recorded as PSD). No UAS accumulated on any channel (protection switch was within 50 ms; UAS counting requires 10 consecutive SES). Zero post-FEC BER events. SLA maintained. Incident closed.
Lessons Learned: (1) OSC LOS is the fastest and most reliable alarm for span-level failures — it appears 80–200 ms before per-channel alarms. Train all NOC tiers to check OSC status first during alarm storms. (2) Proper alarm suppression after root cause identification is not optional — it enables clear-headed execution during the repair phase. (3) Protection switch time measurement (47 ms vs. 50 ms SLA) should always be documented for SLA defense records. |
|---|

| 🟢 Architect Reasoning Block 3.1
Dilemma: "You have 47 active alarms and a 5-minute SLA timer running. What is your sequence?"
Sequence: (1) Immediately check if protection has switched — if yes, service is protected and you have time to diagnose without service impact. SLA timer context changes. (2) Check OSC status across all spans — identifies fiber-level failures in <15 seconds. (3) Sort alarms by timestamp — identify the first 1–3 alarms to appear. (4) Determine geographic scope — are alarms concentrated at one node, one span, or distributed? (5) Identify one root-cause alarm from the first timestamp plus scope analysis. (6) Suppress confirmed symptom alarms. (7) Execute the correct recovery action for the root cause identified. If after 90 seconds you cannot identify a single root-cause alarm, fall back to: restore service via any available path (manual protection switch or reroute) first, then diagnose root cause. Service before forensics — always. |
|---|

### Chapter 3 Summary
Alarm management is a cognitive discipline, not a software function. Production DWDM architects must internalize the alarm hierarchy so thoroughly that alarm pattern recognition becomes near-automatic. The key skills are: correctly classifying root-cause vs. symptom alarms, correlating alarm storms to single root-cause events within 90 seconds, and maintaining operational clarity through alarm suppression during active repair procedures.
#### Architect-Level Review Questions
- You receive simultaneous ODUk-AIS alarms on 12 channels, all originating from the same ROADM add/drop node. The OSC between adjacent nodes is up. What does this alarm pattern indicate, and what is your first diagnostic step?
- A single 100G channel is showing OTUk-BDI (far-end detecting defect) but no LOS at the near-end receiver. Received power is –18 dBm (within range). What physical-layer scenario would produce OTUk-BDI without near-end LOS?
- Your NMS shows a DEG alarm on Channel 23 that appeared 6 days ago and has not cleared. No customer complaints have been received. FEC-CW count is 3× baseline but no UCW events. Is this alarm a priority? What is your reasoning?
- Describe the conditions under which you would apply alarm inhibition on a production resource. What safeguards must be in place? What happens if you forget to remove the inhibition after the maintenance window closes?
- Walk through the exact decision logic you use to determine whether a DEG alarm on an amplified span is caused by (a) connector contamination at the ILA, (b) increasing fiber loss on the span, or (c) EDFA noise figure degradation. What data points distinguish each cause?

3.title: Chapter 4 — CLI Operations and Verification
3.content_md:
# Chapter 4 — CLI Operations and Verification

| Chapter Overview
Operational Objectives: Master CLI command syntax across major DWDM vendor platforms for all operational verification scenarios. Develop the discipline of verify-act-verify. Understand which CLI commands are safe to run on live traffic and which commands require traffic impact assessment before execution.
Field Competencies: Cross-vendor CLI fluency; verification sequence design; CLI-based PM and alarm reading; safety classification of CLI commands.
Real-World Context: NMS systems go offline. Web GUIs time out during critical events. The CLI is the always-available ground truth of the network element. The architect who cannot operate exclusively from CLI during an NMS outage is operationally vulnerable. |
|---|

## 4.1 CLI Philosophy
Three rules govern all CLI operations on production DWDM systems:
- Always verify before acting. Before executing any configuration change, read the current state. Confirm that what you expect to find is what is actually there. Never assume a previous configuration step executed correctly.
- Always verify after acting. After every configuration change, immediately execute the relevant show commands to confirm the change took effect as intended. Do not proceed to the next step until the current step is verified.
- Understand the blast radius. Before executing any CLI command that changes configuration, explicitly think through: what is the worst-case impact if this command has an unintended effect? Can traffic be lost? Will APS switch? Will APC re-converge?
## 4.2 Ciena WaveLogic (SAOS) CLI Reference
Ciena's SAOS (Service Aware Operating System) and WaveLogic coherent modem platforms use a hierarchical CLI structure. Key verification commands:
! === OPTICAL PORT STATUS ===
show port optical [port-name]
  Output: TX power (dBm), RX power (dBm), TX frequency (THz), RX wavelength
  Expected: TX within ±0.5 dBm of target; RX within receiver sensitivity range

! === AMPLIFIER STATUS ===
show amp status [amp-id]
  Output: Pump current (mA), pump power (mW), gain (dB), noise figure (dB),
          output power (dBm), temperature (°C), operational state
  Expected: Pump current below 85% of rated max; gain within ±0.3 dB of setpoint

! === CHANNEL PLAN ===
show channel plan
  Output: All provisioned channels, frequencies, power levels, operational states
  Expected: All channels in "in-service" state; power levels within ±1 dB of target

! === PERFORMANCE MONITORING ===
show pm current [pm-type] [entity]
  Example: show pm current otn-pm port 1/1
  Output: Current 15-minute bin: BBE, ES, SES, UAS, FEC-CW, FEC-UCW
  Expected: FEC-UCW = 0 at all times; FEC-CW within TCA threshold

! === ACTIVE ALARMS ===
show alarms active
  Output: All active alarms with severity, entity, timestamp, description
  Expected: Zero critical alarms outside of known maintenance activities

! === PROTECTION STATUS ===
show protection-group [group-id]
  Output: Active path (working/protect), switch reason, switch count, switch time
  Expected: Working path active unless deliberate switch command issued

! === COHERENT MODEM DIAGNOSTICS ===
show waveserver card [slot] optical-power
  Output: Per-channel TX/RX power, Q-factor, OSNR, BER pre/post-FEC, CD estimate
  Expected: Q-factor above modulation-specific minimum; pre-FEC BER below SD-FEC threshold
## 4.3 Nokia 1830 PSS CLI Reference
The Nokia 1830 Photonic Service Switch uses a provisioning shell with distinct command syntax. All commands must be issued from the appropriate context (system, port, amplifier, etc.).
! === OPTICAL CHANNEL STATUS ===
display optical-channel [channel-id] detail
  Output: Center frequency, power, OSNR, modulation format, FEC mode,
          operational state, admin state
  Expected: Operational state = IS (In Service); admin state = UP

! === AMPLIFIER GAIN AND PUMP STATUS ===
display amplifier [amp-id] detail
  Output: Target gain (dB), actual gain (dB), gain error, pump state,
          pump-1 current (mA), pump-2 current (mA), output power (dBm)
  Expected: Gain error < ±0.2 dB; pumps in NORMAL state

! === PROTECTION SWITCH STATUS ===
display protection-switch [group-id]
  Output: Switch state, active port, requests pending, K1/K2 byte values
  Expected: K1 = 0x00 (No request, working active) under normal conditions
          K1 = 0x20 (SD on working, switch to protect) during DEG event

! === PM DATA ===
display pm-data [entity-type] [entity-id] [interval]
  Example: display pm-data otu 1-A-3-1 15-min current
  Output: All OTN PM counters for current 15-minute interval

! === NODE INVENTORY ===
display equipment [shelf/slot]
  Output: Installed card type, serial number, firmware version, operational status
  Expected: All expected cards PRESENT and IS; no missing or failed cards reported
## 4.4 Infinera TNMS CLI Reference
! === WDM CHANNEL STATUS ===
show wdm-channel [channel-id]
  Output: Channel frequency, OPT, OPR, modulation, FEC type, DSP state,
          pre-FEC BER, post-FEC BER, Q-factor
  Expected: DSP state = LOCKED; post-FEC BER = 0.0

! === EQUIPMENT STATUS ===
show equipment [element-path]
  Output: Module type, operational state, alarm state, temperature, uptime
  Expected: Operational state = OPERATIONAL; no equipment alarms

! === PM DATA RETRIEVAL ===
show pm-data [entity] [granularity]
  Example: show pm-data otu2 node1/shelf1/slot3/port1 15min
  Output: OTN PM counters (BBE, ES, SES, UAS, FEC-CW, FEC-UCW) for period

! === SPAN LOSS CALCULATION ===
show span-loss [span-id]
  Output: Calculated span loss from amplifier gain and power delta;
          OTDR-derived loss if OTDR module present
  Expected: Span loss within 0.5 dB of as-built record
## 4.5 Cisco NCS (Network Convergence System) CLI Reference
Cisco NCS 2000 series uses IOS-XR with optical-specific controller commands under show controllers context.
! === OTS (OPTICAL TRANSPORT SECTION) STATUS ===
show controllers ots [rack/shelf/slot/subslot/port]
  Output: TX power (dBm), RX power (dBm), amplifier gain, pump status,
          OSNR, span loss
  Expected: Powers within ±1 dBm of target; gain stable

! === DWDM CHANNEL STATUS ===
show controllers dwdm [rack/shelf/slot/port] [wavelength]
  Output: TX/RX frequency, TX power, RX power, optical SNR, FEC counters,
          chromatic dispersion
  Expected: FEC uncorrected = 0; CD within DSP compensation range

! === OPTICAL AMPLIFIER STATUS ===
show optical amplifier [controller path]
  Output: Amplifier type, gain mode (constant-gain/constant-power), actual gain,
          target gain, pump power, temperature alarm state
  Expected: Mode = constant-gain; actual gain within 0.3 dB of target

! === OPTICAL INTERFACE DETAIL ===
show optical interface [controller path] detail
  Output: All optical parameters including OSNR, Q-factor, BER, polarization
          state, carrier frequency offset
  Expected: Q-factor above modulation threshold; carrier freq offset < ±1 GHz

! === ALARMS ===
show alarms brief interface [controller path]
  Output: Active alarms on the specified interface with severity and timestamp
  Expected: No critical or major alarms outside maintenance windows

! === OTN PM COUNTERS ===
show controllers otu [controller path] performance-monitoring
  Output: Current 15-minute OTN PM counters: BBE, ES, SES, UAS, FC
  Expected: UAS = 0; FC = 0 (no failed codewords)
## 4.6 Verification Sequences and Safety Rules
Standard verification sequences for common operational events:
### Amplifier Bring-Up Verification Sequence
Step 1: show amp status [id] → Confirm pump states: INACTIVE before enable
Step 2: Enable amplifier per MOP
Step 3: show amp status [id] → Confirm pump state transitions to ACTIVE
Step 4: Wait 60 seconds for APC convergence
Step 5: show amp status [id] → Verify gain within ±0.3 dB of setpoint
Step 6: show port optical [output port] → Verify output power at expected level
Step 7: Show PM data (5-minute wait) → Confirm no FEC-CW increase on channels
Step 8: Notify downstream NE engineer to verify received powers
### Protection Switch Test Verification Sequence
Step 1: Document current state — show protection-group [id] (working active, normal)
Step 2: Notify customer (if required by SLA and maintenance agreement)
Step 3: Issue manual switch command: protection-switch force [group-id] [protect-port]
Step 4: Immediately: show protection-group [id] → Confirm protect path active
Step 5: Measure switch time from command timestamp to protect-active timestamp
Step 6: show pm current [channels] → Verify no UAS accumulated (switch time < 10 SES)
Step 7: Verify traffic on protect path: FEC-UCW = 0; BER nominal
Step 8: Revert: protection-switch clear [group-id]
Step 9: show protection-group [id] → Confirm revert to working path
Step 10: Document switch time and protect-path quality in maintenance record

| ⚠ CLI Commands That Can Impact Live Traffic
The following command categories can affect traffic and require explicit change authorization before execution: (1) Any command that changes amplifier gain by more than 0.5 dB — large gain changes cause APC re-convergence that may transiently affect power levels on all channels. (2) Any command that changes channel power target — APC will adjust output, causing transient power changes on adjacent channels. (3) shutdown or disable on any optical port or amplifier — will drop all traffic on that resource. (4) FEC mode changes — require service interruption to take effect. (5) Protection lockout commands — remove the protection safety net; if working path then fails, service is lost. Always confirm with the approving architect before executing any command in these categories. |
|---|

| 🟢 Architect Reasoning Block 4.1
Dilemma: "It is 2:15 AM. A 400G wavelength has suddenly degraded. What is your CLI sequence?"
Sequence (with reasoning): (1) show alarms active on the NE terminating the affected wavelength — what alarm exists? LOS means signal is gone. DEG means signal is present but degraded. (2) show port optical [rx-port] — check received power. If power is low (more than 3 dBm below expected), loss is between source and this NE. If power is nominal but quality is low, problem is upstream noise or local transceiver. (3) show pm current otn — pull FEC-CW and FEC-UCW. Zero UCW means signal is still FEC-correctable — you have time. Non-zero UCW means service is impacted — protect immediately. (4) show wdm-channel or equivalent — pull Q-factor, OSNR, CD estimate. A CD estimate far outside the expected value suggests fiber route change. Low Q with normal power suggests OSNR problem (amplifier noise). (5) Check adjacent channels — if neighbors are also degraded, problem is span-level (EDFA noise, span loss increase). If isolated to this channel, problem is transponder or per-channel WSS setting. (6) Based on findings: if span-level → check each amplifier on the path hop-by-hop via CLI. If transponder-level → initiate transponder diagnostics, prepare for module swap. Always verify before acting. Always verify after acting. |
|---|

### Chapter 4 Summary
CLI mastery across vendor platforms is a non-negotiable production skill. The verification discipline — before and after every action — prevents the most common category of self-inflicted network outages. Understanding which commands carry traffic impact risk is as important as knowing the syntax. The architect who can operate any major DWDM platform from CLI alone has eliminated their single largest operational vulnerability.
#### Architect-Level Review Questions
- On a Cisco NCS 2006, the show controllers ots 0/0/0/0 command returns "OSNR: 14.3 dB" for a 100G DP-QPSK channel. FEC-UCW in the current 15-minute bin is 47. RX power is –17.2 dBm (within range). What does this data tell you about the problem, and what CLI commands do you run next?
- You need to verify that a protection switch on a Nokia 1830 PSS executed correctly and that traffic is running error-free on the protect path. List the exact CLI commands you would run and what output you expect from each.
- A junior engineer tells you he ran set amp gain 25.0 (an increase from 22.5 dB) on an inline amplifier while channels were live, because "it was just a show command." Explain why this could have caused a service impact, what the APC system's response would have been, and what PM data you would pull to determine if any service was affected.
- You are performing a multi-node CLI audit of amplifier pump currents across a 12-node network. Describe how you would script or structure this audit to collect consistent data from all nodes efficiently, and what you would be looking for in the results.
- The NMS is completely offline due to a software upgrade failure. You have CLI access to all network elements. A NOC alert indicates a channel degradation has been reported by a customer. Walk through your complete CLI-only diagnostic procedure, naming specific commands you would use on your primary vendor platform.

3.title: Chapter 5 — Commissioning and Turn-Up Procedures
3.content_md:
# Chapter 5 — Commissioning and Turn-Up Procedures

| Chapter Overview
Operational Objectives: Master the complete end-to-end process for commissioning a DWDM span and system, from fiber plant pre-check through acceptance testing and handover to operations. Understand what must be measured, documented, and verified at every stage.
Field Competencies: Span characterization; amplifier optimization; transponder provisioning; acceptance test execution; commissioning documentation.
Real-World Context: Poorly commissioned systems fail early and often. The architect who compresses commissioning procedures to meet a schedule deadline creates a maintenance burden that persists for the system's entire operational life. Every parameter that is out-of-spec at commissioning and not corrected is a future outage waiting to happen. |
|---|

## 5.1 Pre-Commissioning Checklist
Before any optical power is applied to the system, the following must be verified and documented:

| Category | Check Item | Method | Pass Criterion |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fiber Plant | OTDR trace of every fiber segment | OTDR from both ends | No reflections > –50 dB; no events > 0.5 dB; end-to-end loss within design budget ± 10% |
| Fiber Plant | Connector inspection at all patch panels | Video inspection probe, IEC 61300-3-35 criteria | Grade A or B per IEC 61300-3-35 on all mated connectors |
| Fiber Plant | Insertion loss measurement per span | Optical Power Meter + calibrated source (G.650.3) | Within ±1 dB of design loss budget |
| Power | DC power voltage at each shelf | Multimeter at PDU output and shelf input | –48 VDC ± 5% (–45.6 to –50.4 VDC) |
| Power | Grounding continuity | Continuity tester from shelf chassis to floor ground bus | <1 Ω resistance |
| Environmental | Site temperature and humidity | Calibrated HVAC monitoring or portable instrument | Temperature: 18–26°C; Humidity: 30–75% RH non-condensing (NEBS GR-63) |
| Equipment | Card seating and cabling | Visual inspection; loopback connectivity test | All cards seated; no bent optical fibers; fiber routing per cable plan |
| Documentation | As-designed fiber route map on site | Verify against physical installation | No discrepancies between design and installed configuration |

## 5.2 Span Characterization Procedure
Span characterization establishes the baseline optical performance of the fiber plant before any active components are introduced. This data becomes the permanent reference against which future degradation is measured.
- OTDR Walk: Perform OTDR traces on all fibers (C-band range, 1550 nm test wavelength recommended for DWDM spans). Document: total insertion loss, number of fusion splices, individual splice losses, connector losses, reflectance levels, and any fiber anomalies (kinks, tight bends, water infiltration signatures). Store raw OTDR trace files.
- CD Measurement: Use an Optical Spectrum Analyzer (OSA) with CD measurement capability or a dedicated CD measurement system. Measure chromatic dispersion coefficient (ps/nm/km) and total accumulated CD (ps/nm) for each fiber. Confirm consistency with ITU-T G.652.D specification (nominally 17 ps/nm/km at 1550 nm). This data is critical for determining DSP compensation requirements for coherent transceivers.
- PMD Measurement: Measure PMD coefficient (ps/√km) using interferometric or Jones Matrix Eigenanalysis method. Calculate maximum mean DGD for the span. For 100G DP-QPSK, total DGD must be below 30 ps (95th percentile). Older fiber (>15 years) may require PMD testing before 100G deployment.
- Loss Budget Verification: Compare measured insertion loss against the optical link budget. Confirm that design margin assumptions are met. Document surplus margin.
## 5.3 Amplifier Optimization
EDFA gain settings must be optimized before channel loading. The target is flat gain across the full C-band with a specified gain tilt (power difference between short and long wavelengths) that compensates for fiber stimulated Raman scattering and EDFA gain shape.
- Gain Setting: Set target gain to exactly compensate for span insertion loss (gain = span loss, within ±0.5 dB). Over-gain causes excessive ASE accumulation. Under-gain reduces OSNR at downstream points.
- Tilt Correction: Use an OSA to measure the power spectral profile at the amplifier output. Adjust tilt compensation (VOA or gain-flattening filter setting) to achieve channel power uniformity of ±1 dB across the full C-band.
- Noise Figure Measurement: Calculate EDFA noise figure using the optical substitution method or signal-to-noise ratio interpolation method. Record for baseline. Typical EDFA noise figure: 5–7 dB for standard amplifiers; 4–5 dB for low-noise (LA) types.
- APC Verification: Confirm APC (Automatic Power Control) is enabled and responding correctly. Change channel count by ±4 channels (if test environment allows) and verify APC adjusts gain to maintain per-channel output power.
## 5.4 OSC Bring-Up and Transponder Provisioning
The **Optical Supervisory Channel (OSC)** operates on a separate wavelength (typically 1510 nm or 1625 nm, outside the DWDM C-band) and carries management traffic between adjacent nodes. OSC must be established and verified before any traffic channels are brought up, as it provides the in-band management path for NE configuration.
OSC bring-up verification: confirm bidirectional connectivity, management plane reachability from NMS, and NE discovery. If OSC fails to come up, do not proceed with traffic channel provisioning — the management plane must be established first.
**Transponder Provisioning Sequence:**
- Assign wavelength (ITU-T G.694.1 grid frequency) per channel plan
- Map client interface to OTN container (ODU mapping per G.709 — e.g., 100GbE into OTU4)
- Enable FEC (confirm SD-FEC for 100G+ coherent; specify HD-FEC only where explicitly required)
- Set modulation format (DP-QPSK for 100G, DP-16QAM for 200G, DP-32QAM for high-capacity)
- Configure OTN overhead: TTI (Trail Trace Identifier) — set with source and expected values for end-to-end path verification
- Enable port and verify DSP lock: DSP must achieve carrier and phase lock before traffic passes
## 5.5 End-to-End Verification and Acceptance Testing
Acceptance testing verifies that the complete commissioned system meets design specifications and is ready for service. The following parameters must be measured and documented:

| Test | Method | Pass Criterion |
|---|---|---|
| OSNR at far-end receiver | OSA measurement at far-end in-line tap or via coherent modem OSNR estimate | ≥ design OSNR + 3 dB margin |
| Per-channel power uniformity | OSA at each ILA output | ±1 dB across all loaded channels at each measurement point |
| Post-FEC BER (all channels) | CLI PM readout: FEC-UCW = 0 over 24-hour test | Zero FEC uncorrected words in any 15-minute bin over 24 hours |
| Pre-FEC BER baseline | CLI PM readout: FEC-CW count | Document as commissioning baseline; must be well below TCA threshold |
| Protection switch test | Manual forced switch; measure switch time; verify protect path performance | Switch time ≤ 50 ms; protect path: post-FEC BER = 0 |
| OTN overhead verification | TTI check: send expected identifier, verify received at far end matches | TTI received = TTI transmitted (no mismatch) |
| Latency measurement | One-way delay measurement using test equipment or PTP timestamps | Within 5% of design latency calculation (fiber length ÷ propagation speed) |
| 72-hour soak period | Continuous PM monitoring: FEC-CW trend, power stability, OSNR stability | No TCAs triggered; no PM parameter trend outside commissioning baseline ±10% |

| 📁 Case Study 5.1 — Turn-Up of a 1,200 km Multi-Span Route
Network Context: New regional backbone, Seattle to Los Angeles, 1,215 km, 12 ILA sites (average span: 93 km), Ciena WaveLogic 5e transponders (400G DP-16QAM), 80-channel load planned, initial activation at 40 channels.
Pre-Commissioning (Day 1–3): OTDR traces completed on all 24 fiber pairs (TX and RX for each direction). Found: ILA-7 input span has splice at km 41.2 with 0.72 dB loss (above 0.5 dB criterion). Connector inspection at ILA-3 patch panel revealed Grade C contamination on 3 connectors. Both issues corrected before proceeding — splice re-done (new loss: 0.18 dB), connectors cleaned and re-inspected (Grade A). Loss budgets verified — all 12 spans within design budget. Longest span (ILA-4 to ILA-5): 97.3 km, measured loss: 22.1 dB (design: 21.5 dB; difference within ±10% tolerance).
OSC Bring-Up (Day 4): OSC enabled at Terminal A (Seattle). Verified hop by hop. ILA-6 OSC board failed to respond. Investigation: incorrect OSC wavelength filter installed (1625 nm board in a 1510 nm system). Board replaced with correct type. OSC connectivity verified end-to-end (Terminal A to Terminal B) by Day 4 afternoon. NMS discovered all 14 NEs.
Amplifier Optimization (Day 4–5): All 12 ILA EDFAs gain-set to match span loss. OSA measurement at each amplifier output confirmed channel power uniformity ±0.8 dB across C-band. ILA-9 showed 1.5 dB tilt — gain-flattening filter adjusted. Noise figure measurements: range 5.4–6.1 dB across all 12 amplifiers (within spec).
Transponder Provisioning (Day 5): 40 channels provisioned at 400G DP-16QAM. Frequency assignment per ITU-T G.694.1 100 GHz grid. OTU4 mapping. SD-FEC enabled. TTI strings configured with circuit ID for each service. DSP lock verified on all 40 channels within 4 minutes of enabling.
End-to-End Verification (Day 5–6): Post-FEC BER: zero UCW events on all 40 channels over 24 hours. Pre-FEC BER (via FEC-CW count): average 1,840 words/15-min (baseline established). OSNR at Terminal B: 23.1–25.4 dB across loaded channels (design target: ≥22 dB with 3 dB margin). Worst-margin channel: Channel 71 at 23.1 dB (meets spec, but flagged for monitoring). Protection switch test: switch time 38 ms (≤50 ms SLA). 72-hour soak: all parameters stable. No TCAs.
Acceptance Sign-Off (Day 8): Full commissioning package delivered: OTDR traces, span loss table, amplifier gain table, noise figure table, channel power table (OSA), OSNR table, protection switch test results, 72-hour PM log, and as-built configuration export from all 14 NEs. System handed to operations with documented baseline and TCA configuration applied.
Lessons Learned: (1) Correcting the splice and connector issues on Days 1–2 prevented what would have been a permanent margin deficit. The 0.72 dB excess splice loss would have reduced the ILA-7 span OSNR contribution by 0.72 dB — on a 12-ILA route, any one span's excess loss directly reduces end-to-end OSNR. (2) The OSC board mismatch (1625 nm vs 1510 nm) is a common site installation error. Always verify OSC board compatibility against site design before Day 1. (3) Channel 71's 23.1 dB OSNR (just 1.1 dB above minimum) should be investigated — document it as a low-margin channel and set tighter TCA thresholds for this channel. |
|---|

### Chapter 5 Summary
Commissioning is the moment when design intent meets physical reality. Every discrepancy found and corrected during commissioning is a prevented future outage. The production architect approaches commissioning as a comprehensive measurement and verification campaign, not as a configuration exercise. The commissioning baseline is the most important document in the system's operational life.
#### Architect-Level Review Questions
- During pre-commissioning fiber testing, you find a span with measured insertion loss 2.1 dB higher than the design budget. The project manager insists you proceed because "the coherent DSP will compensate." Explain your technical and professional response, including what risk the project manager is accepting if you comply.
- You are commissioning a 400G DP-16QAM system on a span with measured PMD coefficient of 0.12 ps/√km over 150 km of installed fiber. Calculate the expected maximum DGD and determine whether this is within the DSP compensation range. What would you recommend if it is not?
- An ILA amplifier commissioning shows noise figure of 8.4 dB on one of the two amplifier modules. The vendor specification is 5–7 dB. The amplifier gain and output power are meeting targets. What is the operational significance of the elevated noise figure, and what action do you take?
- During the 72-hour soak period, you observe Channel 23's FEC-CW count trending from 1,200 at Hour 0 to 3,400 at Hour 48, and the system temperature log shows site temperature rose from 20°C to 27°C over the same period. What is the most likely cause? How do you confirm it? Does this system pass acceptance?
- Design a protection switch test procedure for a 1+1 optical protection group on a newly commissioned system. Include the specific commands you would run, what you would measure, and what criteria must be met for the test to pass.

3.title: Chapter 6 — Systematic Troubleshooting Methodology
3.content_md:
# Chapter 6 — Systematic Troubleshooting Methodology

| Chapter Overview
Operational Objectives: Internalize a rigorous, repeatable troubleshooting methodology for DWDM production environments. Develop the discipline of systematic isolation over intuition-based guessing. Master layer-by-layer isolation, signal tracing, and half-split techniques.
Field Competencies: Framework application under time pressure; layer isolation technique; half-split localization; live fault documentation; 5-Why root-cause analysis.
Real-World Context: Experienced optical engineers often fall into the trap of "I've seen this before" — jumping to a hypothesis based on pattern matching and immediately acting on it. In a production environment, acting on an incorrect hypothesis can worsen the fault, impact additional services, or consume the time needed to find the real root cause. Systematic methodology is not slower than intuition — it is faster on average because it eliminates the backtracking caused by incorrect first assumptions. |
|---|

## 6.1 The DWDM Troubleshooting Framework
The production DWDM troubleshooting framework follows a six-phase loop:
- OBSERVE: Gather all available data without changing anything. Pull PM, alarms, topology, recent change log. Understand the scope and timeline of the problem. Do not touch the network until you have established what you are dealing with.
- ISOLATE: Narrow the problem to a specific layer, span, or component using systematic elimination. Use signal tracing and half-split techniques. The goal is to identify the smallest segment of the network that, if replaced or corrected, would resolve the fault.
- HYPOTHESIZE: Based on the isolated fault location and the symptom pattern, generate a ranked list of hypotheses (most likely to least likely). Each hypothesis must be testable and falsifiable.
- TEST: Execute the minimum possible action that will confirm or refute the top hypothesis without impacting other services. Verify results. If the hypothesis is refuted, move to the next on the list. Document every test action and result.
- RESOLVE: Once root cause is confirmed, execute the resolution action per MOP. Verify that resolution has cleared the fault at every layer (physical, OSNR, OTN PM, end-to-end BER).
- DOCUMENT: Complete the fault log with full timeline, test actions, root cause, resolution, and lessons learned. This documentation becomes institutional knowledge that reduces time-to-resolve for the next occurrence.
## 6.2 Layer-by-Layer Isolation
DWDM networks are a stack of independent layers, each of which can fail independently. The isolation sequence moves from physical to digital, from infrastructure to endpoints:

| Layer | What to Check | Key Tools/Commands | Fault Indicator |
|---|---|---|---|
| Layer 0: Physical Fiber | Span loss, OTDR reflection events, connector condition | OTDR, power meter, visual fault locator | Span loss > baseline + 0.5 dB; new OTDR event; connector degradation |
| Layer 0.5: Optical Amplification | EDFA gain, pump current, noise figure, output power | show amp status; OSA at amp output | Gain off-setpoint > 0.5 dB; pump current at max; noise figure elevated |
| Layer 1: Transponder Optical | TX/RX power, OSNR, CD estimate, DGD estimate, Q-factor | show port optical; show wdm-channel detail | Q-factor below minimum; OSNR below threshold; CD outside DSP range |
| Layer 1.5: DSP / Coherent Modem | DSP lock state, FEC counters, carrier offset, BER | show coherent modem; show pm current | DSP unlocked; FEC-UCW > 0; BER above threshold |
| Layer 2: OTN | OTN framing, TTI match, ODUk mapping, TCM status | show controllers otn; TTI verification | TTI mismatch; ODUk-AIS or OCI; LOF/LOM conditions |
| Layer 3: Client Signal | Client-side interface status (Ethernet, OTN client, SDH) | show interface; client-side loopback tests | Client interface down; client-side errors while OTN is clean |

Always start at Layer 0 and work upward. A Layer 2 OTN framing alarm may appear to be an OTN problem when in reality it is caused by a Layer 0 span loss increase that is degrading OSNR below the FEC threshold. Treating the Layer 2 symptom does not resolve the Layer 0 root cause.
## 6.3 Signal Tracing and Half-Split Technique
**Signal tracing** means following the optical path hop-by-hop from the transmitting transponder to the receiving transponder, measuring optical power and quality at each amplifier stage. This technique identifies exactly where signal quality degrades.
Procedure: Starting from the TX transponder output, note the power level. At the first ILA input, check received power against the expected level (TX output minus span 1 insertion loss). Then check ILA output power against the amplified target. Continue to ILA-2 input, ILA-2 output, and so on until you find the hop where input power is lower than expected (span loss event) or where output power is lower than expected (amplifier issue).
**Half-split technique** is an efficiency optimization for long routes. Rather than starting at one end and working hop-by-hop across 15 spans, select the midpoint of the path (e.g., ILA-7 of 14) and measure signal quality there. If quality is good at the midpoint, the fault is in the second half. If quality is already degraded at the midpoint, the fault is in the first half. Repeat for the identified half, dividing again. This reduces from O(n) span checks to O(log n) — from 14 measurements to 4 measurements to isolate the faulty span on a 14-span route.
## 6.4 Root-Cause Analysis and Post-Incident Review
Once a fault is resolved, the production architect must complete a formal root-cause analysis (RCA). The **5-Why methodology** applied to optical faults:
*Example: 100G wavelength experienced 47 minutes of BER degradation.*
- Why 1: Why did the wavelength degrade? OSNR dropped from 22 dB to 14 dB.
- Why 2: Why did OSNR drop? Span loss on ILA-4 to ILA-5 increased by 4.2 dB.
- Why 3: Why did span loss increase? OTDR showed a new event at km 31.4 — physical fiber bending event.
- Why 4: Why did fiber bend? Cable tension at an aerial crossing point increased due to ice loading on the span.
- Why 5: Why was this not anticipated? The aerial crossing at km 31.4 was not in the at-risk infrastructure list for winter ice loading protocols. The cable sag calculation for that span was at the minimum design margin.
- Corrective Action: Replace ice-loaded cable section; revise at-risk aerial span list to include km 31.4; implement ice load monitoring at this crossing; add pre-winter inspection to maintenance calendar.
The RCA is not complete until the **systemic corrective action** is identified — the change that prevents the same root cause from producing the same fault in the future. Resolving the immediate fault without addressing the systemic cause means the fault will recur.

| 🟢 Architect Reasoning Block 6.1
Dilemma: "A 400G wavelength dropped to pre-FEC BER 1×10⁻⁴ — walk through your full troubleshooting sequence."
Full Sequence: First: pre-FEC BER 1×10⁻⁴ is a serious condition but may still be FEC-correctable with SD-FEC (typical SD-FEC threshold: ~1.2×10⁻²). Verify FEC-UCW first — is this a service-affecting or pre-service-affecting condition? (1) Pull FEC-UCW: if zero, service is intact but degraded; if non-zero, service is affected — activate protection immediately. (2) Pull received optical power (OPR): is it within 1 dBm of baseline? If OPR is low, problem is between source and this receiver (span loss or amplifier). If OPR is normal, problem is OSNR-related (noise, not loss). (3) Pull OSNR: if OSNR is low despite normal OPR, EDFA noise figure has degraded. (4) Pull Q-factor and CD estimate: CD outside range suggests fiber path change (protection switch to wrong fiber?). Very low Q with normal CD suggests polarization or phase noise issue. (5) Check adjacent channels: are they also degraded? If yes, span-level or amplifier issue. If isolated to this channel, transponder or per-channel issue. (6) Check the change log: did anything change in the last 24 hours? (7) Based on findings: if span issue → signal trace from each direction to localize the degraded hop; if amplifier issue → check pump current and gain; if transponder issue → initiate transceiver diagnostics, prepare for swap; if CD issue → verify protection path DCM configuration. Never reset the transceiver as a first action — reset clears the DSP history that may be valuable for diagnosis. |
|---|

### Chapter 6 Summary
Systematic troubleshooting methodology is the professional separation between an engineer who resolves faults and an architect who understands networks. The six-phase framework, combined with layer-by-layer isolation and efficient half-split localization, enables consistent, documented, defensible fault resolution that builds institutional knowledge with every incident.
#### Architect-Level Review Questions
- A 200G wavelength on a 15-span route is showing elevated BER. You have CLI access to all 16 nodes (2 terminal + 14 ILA). Using the half-split technique, describe exactly which nodes you measure at, in what order, and what you are looking for at each measurement to localize the fault to a single span.
- During troubleshooting, you discover that an engineer has already attempted to fix the problem by resetting the far-end transponder before calling you. The reset did not help. What information have you potentially lost due to the premature reset, and how does this affect your troubleshooting approach?
- You have isolated the fault to a degraded ILA on Span 7. The amplifier gain is 2.5 dB below setpoint, and the pump drive current is at maximum. This means the pump laser is failing. The span carries 60 channels with no protection. Describe your decision framework for whether to take emergency action now vs. scheduling a maintenance window.
- Complete a 5-Why analysis for the following scenario: A 100G wavelength failed completely at 03:47 AM. Investigation revealed the fiber was damaged by a contractor performing excavation work near an unprotected underground route. The circuit had no protection. No UAS credit was given to the customer for the 4.5-hour outage.
- What is the minimum information that must be captured in a live fault log during active troubleshooting? Argue why this documentation discipline is technically justified under time pressure, not just administratively required.

3.title: Chapter 7 — Fault Isolation Scenarios and Case Studies
3.content_md:
# Chapter 7 — Fault Isolation Scenarios and Case Studies

| Chapter Overview
Operational Objectives: Apply the troubleshooting methodology to six realistic production fault scenarios. Develop pattern recognition for common DWDM fault signatures. Extract the generalizable lessons from each case that apply to future incidents.
Field Competencies: Real-world fault diagnosis; multi-data-source correlation; root-cause identification from realistic PM and alarm data; resolution execution and verification. |
|---|

| 📁 Case Study 7.1 — Gradual OSNR Degradation Over 4 Weeks
Network Context: Long-haul backbone, 1,100 km, 10 ILA sites, 80 channels (100G DP-QPSK). ILA-6 is at a remote unstaffed hut, accessible by 2-hour road travel. The affected channel: Channel 41 (193.100 THz).
Week 1 PM Data: OSNR: 21.4 dB. FEC-CW (24-hr): 1,820 words. Pre-FEC BER: ~1×10⁻⁸. All nominal.
Week 2: OSNR: 20.8 dB (–0.6 dB). FEC-CW: 2,400 words (+32%). Architect notes trend but no TCA. Opens monitoring ticket.
Week 3: OSNR: 19.9 dB (–1.5 dB from baseline). FEC-CW: 4,800 words (+164%). TCA fires (FEC-CW 24-hr warning). Architect escalates to P2. Channel 41 is specifically affected; adjacent channels 39, 40, 42, 43 show no change. Per-channel fault is confirmed.
Diagnosis — Step by Step:
Step 1: Adjacent channel isolation confirmed — Channel 41 only. Rules out span-level EDFA issue (which would affect all channels).
Step 2: CLI: check received power on Channel 41 at far-end terminal. OPR: –19.2 dBm vs. –18.8 dBm baseline. Only 0.4 dBm reduction — not enough to explain 1.5 dB OSNR drop.
Step 3: OSNR check from OSA at ILA-6 output (remote access via OTDR module): Channel 41 OSNR at ILA-6 output: 33.1 dB. Nominal for that point. OSNR is degrading somewhere after ILA-6.
Step 4: OSA at Terminal B (far-end) patch panel. Channel 41 OSNR: 19.7 dB. Between ILA-6 and Terminal B: OSNR dropped 13.4 dB. Normal for 4-span segment: ~10 dB. Excess: 3.4 dB OSNR degradation in the ILA-6 to Terminal B segment.
Step 5: ILA-7 amp status via CLI: gain and pump current nominal. OSNR contribution calculation: ILA-7 noise figure: 5.7 dB (baseline: 5.6 dB — essentially unchanged). Not the amplifier.
Step 6: Connector inspection scheduled at ILA-7. Field crew dispatched. At ILA-7 input patch panel: Channel 41's LC/APC connector is contaminated — epoxy residue visible under inspection probe (Grade D). Adjacent connectors clean. This is a per-channel contamination affecting only the ILA-7 input port for Channel 41's demultiplexed wavelength tap point (WSS input port).
Root Cause Identified: Contaminated connector at ILA-7 WSS input port for Channel 41, causing 3.4 dB additional insertion loss on that channel only. The per-channel nature of the contamination (at a post-demultiplexed connector) explained why no adjacent channels were affected.
Resolution: Connector cleaned per IEC 61300-3-35 procedure. Re-inspected: Grade A. Re-mated. Channel 41 OSNR immediately recovered to 21.2 dB. FEC-CW returned to 1,950 words/24-hr within 4 hours.
Lessons Learned: (1) A per-channel OSNR issue that does not affect adjacent channels points to a per-channel physical path element: the transponder itself, a per-channel WSS port, or a per-channel patch cord. (2) Connector contamination at WSS demux ports is particularly insidious because it occurs post-mux and affects only individual channels routed through that port. (3) The 4-week trend allowed a planned field dispatch rather than an emergency response — PM trending ROI confirmed. |
|---|

| 📁 Case Study 7.2 — Intermittent Wavelength Drops on a Metro Ring
Network Context: Metro ring, 6 nodes, 120 km circumference, 40 channels (100G DP-QPSK). Customer: financial data center interconnect (latency-sensitive). Circuit: Channel 7, Node A to Node D (half-ring, 60 km). No protection (customer declined protection SLA).
Symptoms: Customer reports momentary traffic interruptions, typically lasting 200–800 ms, occurring 2–5 times per day, always between 06:00 and 10:00 local time and 16:00 and 19:00. NOC sees transient LOS and LOF alarms on Channel 7, self-clearing within seconds. FEC-UCW events during each drop.
Initial PM Data: OSNR: 24.7 dB (strong — not a noise issue). Received power: –16.4 dBm (nominal). Between drop events: all parameters normal. Temperature logs from Node B hut: 18–24°C (within spec). Temperature logs from Node C hut: 19–26°C (within spec).
Environmental Correlation (Key Insight): Alarm timestamps cross-correlated with: weather data (none), power events (none), traffic load changes (none), temperature at nodes (none). Architect requests HVAC cycling records from Node B — HVAC cycle runs every 47 minutes, with 3°C temperature swing. No correlation. Then correlates alarm timestamps with solar position data — drops occur during morning and late-afternoon sun angles. Requests site photographs of fiber routing at Node B — fiber exits the building via a south-facing external cable tray with direct sun exposure during morning and afternoon sun angles.
Root Cause: Thermal cycling of the external cable tray at Node B is causing differential expansion/contraction of the cable tray support brackets. One bracket has a mechanical failure — it has cracked, and the cable tray is moving slightly (1–2 mm deflection) twice daily during peak thermal expansion. The movement is applying transient mechanical stress to an SFP+ module in the external patch panel at Node B, causing momentary optical connection instability. The SFP+ module is a non-standard short-reach type that was used as a temporary test component and was never replaced with the correct long-reach module — the suboptimal mechanical seating is what made it susceptible to the 1–2 mm flex.
Resolution: Replace loose SFP+ with correct long-reach SFP+ module. Replace cracked cable tray bracket. Re-route external fiber to avoid direct solar loading. Drops ceased immediately. No further events in 30-day monitoring period.
Lessons Learned: (1) Time-of-day periodicity in transient faults is a major diagnostic clue — thermal, traffic, or environmental cycling. Cross-correlate with everything. (2) Temporary test components left in production are a persistent risk — implement a formal post-commissioning audit to identify any components marked "temporary." (3) External cable runs require environmental engineering review, not just optical engineering review. |
|---|

| 📁 Case Study 7.3 — Post-Restoration BER Degradation (Incorrect DCM After Re-Route)
Network Context: Long-haul backbone, 900 km, 8 ILA sites. Protection architecture: Optical 1+1, working path via northern route (880 km), protect path via southern route (940 km). Channel 31 (100G DP-QPSK). Working path uses no DCM (full DSP CD compensation). Protect path was originally designed with legacy hardware requiring DCM at ILA-S3.
Scenario: Fiber cut on northern (working) route at 22:37. APS switches Channel 31 to protect (southern) path at 22:37.048 — switch time: 48 ms. Protection successful. Service operational.
Post-Switch PM Data: Channel 31 shows: OSNR: 20.1 dB (nominal for southern route), OPR: –18.9 dBm (nominal). FEC-CW: 8,400 words/15-min (vs. working-path baseline of 1,820 words/15-min). FEC-UCW: 0 (service intact). Q-factor: 8.7 dB (vs. 11.2 dB on working path).
Diagnosis: Quality degraded on protect path despite adequate OSNR and power. Q-factor drop of 2.5 dB with OSNR only 0.6 dB lower than working path. CD estimate from CLI: –3,240 ps/nm. Working path CD estimate was –187 ps/nm. The southern route has accumulated CD of approximately –3,240 ps/nm — which is 940 km × 17 ps/nm/km = approximately 15,980 ps/nm of raw fiber CD, but the CLI estimate reads –3,240 ps/nm, suggesting over-compensation by a DCM module. The DSP is within its compensation range (typically ±50,000 ps/nm for modern coherent modems) but the residual dispersion from an incorrect DCM is causing higher-order penalty.
Investigation: ILA-S3 DCM inventory check reveals a –16,200 ps/nm DCM module (designed for working path legacy requirements) is installed in the southern route protection path. This was a configuration error from a previous network upgrade — the DCM was installed but the protect path was never re-validated after the working path was moved to a coherent platform that does not need DCM.
Root Cause: Incorrect DCM (–16,200 ps/nm) in protect path ILA-S3, installed during a previous upgrade and not removed. Total protect-path CD: +15,980 (fiber) – 16,200 (DCM) = –220 ps/nm net, which is fine. But the issue is not the CD total — it is the higher-order dispersion and dispersion slope compensation error introduced by a physical DCM that is not matched to the fiber's actual dispersion profile, causing a 2.5 dB Q-factor penalty not captured in the simple CD estimate.
Resolution: DCM removed from ILA-S3 after working path fiber restored and APS reverted. Protect path validated with DCM removed: Q-factor improved to 10.8 dB, FEC-CW reduced to 2,100 words/15-min. Protect path now fully verified. As-built records updated.
Lessons Learned: (1) Protect paths must be validated end-to-end independently from working paths — after any network change, both paths must be tested. (2) Protection switch does not mean "service is fine" — post-switch PM verification is mandatory. (3) Physical DCMs in modern coherent networks are a known risk — audit all DCMs in the network for continued necessity. |
|---|

| 📁 Case Study 7.4 — Mass Wavelength Failure at Hub Node (WSS Failure)
Network Context: ROADM hub node, 8-degree ROADM, 96 channels add/drop at this site. All channels from 6 express directions passing through the hub are unaffected. Only channels that specifically add or drop at this hub node fail. 47 channels affected simultaneously.
Alarm Pattern: At T+00:00, 47 simultaneous LOS alarms on channels that terminate at this hub. Express channels through the hub: fully operational. OSC to all adjacent nodes: up. NMS connectivity to hub node: up. Hub node reports: all line-facing amplifiers nominal. WSS card in the add/drop ROADM element reports: "WSS internal laser failure — pump laser offline."
Root Cause: The WSS (Wavelength Selective Switch) at this ROADM hub uses an internal tunable laser for its switching mechanism (MEMS-based WSS with internal reference laser for wavelength calibration and port switching). The internal laser failed, taking the WSS offline. All 47 channels routed to or from this hub via the local WSS lost their switching path.
Resolution: WSS line card is field-replaceable. Spare WSS card was available in the site's spare inventory. Hot-swap performed per MOP: replacement card seated, WSS re-provisioned from NMS (configuration downloaded from NMS database), wavelength calibration sequence ran automatically (12 minutes). All 47 channels re-established. Total impact: 34 minutes.
Lessons Learned: (1) WSS failures cause all channels through the failed WSS simultaneously — the all-vs-none failure scope is a WSS signature. (2) Spare WSS inventory at major hub nodes is a risk reduction investment with quantifiable ROI — 34 minutes with a spare vs. 6+ hours waiting for overnight shipment. (3) The express channels being unaffected was the critical diagnostic — it proved the failure was at the add/drop ROADM level, not the trunk spans. |
|---|

| 📁 Case Study 7.5 — Asymmetric BER: Uni-Directional Degradation
Network Context: Point-to-point, 600 km, 7 ILA sites, Channel 55 (100G DP-QPSK, bidirectional). Node A to Node B direction: all nominal (OSNR 22.1 dB, FEC-CW 1,840 words/day). Node B to Node A direction: OSNR 21.8 dB (close to A-to-B), but FEC-CW 14,200 words/day (vs. 1,840 in the other direction). FEC-UCW: still zero. Q-factor B-to-A: 9.1 dB vs. 11.3 dB A-to-B.
Key Diagnostic Data: Asymmetric BER with symmetric OSNR means the problem is at a specific element that appears in one direction but not the other. The B-to-A signal traverses: Node B TX → ILA-7 → ILA-6 → ILA-5 → ILA-4 → ILA-3 → ILA-2 → ILA-1 → Node A RX. The A-to-B signal traverses the reverse path with different fiber pairs and potentially different ILA modules (ILA sites often have separate A-to-B and B-to-A amplifier chains).
Diagnosis: Signal trace from Node B toward Node A. At ILA-6 input from Node B direction: received power –20.1 dBm vs. –18.4 dBm baseline. Span loss from ILA-7 to ILA-6: 24.3 dB vs. 22.7 dB baseline. Excess loss: 1.6 dB. But OSNR is still adequate. At ILA-6, before investigating further, CLI check of the Node B TX transponder: TX power from Node B on Channel 55 — OPT: +0.8 dBm vs. +3.0 dBm baseline. TX power regression on the far-end (Node B) transponder! The TX laser is degrading: it is producing 2.2 dBm less than specified. Combined with the 1.6 dB excess span loss on one span, the accumulated OSNR deficit is causing the elevated FEC-CW count.
Root Cause: TX power regression on the Node B transponder (TX coherent laser aging) combined with marginal excess span loss on one span producing accumulated OSNR deficit in the B-to-A direction only. Note: the OSNR measurement appears normal because the OSNR estimate from the CLI is an approximation; the real OSNR penalty from reduced TX power is subtle and best detected through FEC-CW trending.
Resolution: Node B transponder card replaced (TX power regression confirms end-of-life laser). New card: OPT +3.1 dBm. FEC-CW B-to-A returned to 1,920 words/day. Q-factor: 11.1 dB. Span loss on ILA-7 to ILA-6 segment investigated — found connector degradation adding 1.3 dB on that specific span; connector cleaned, span loss returned to 22.9 dB.
Lessons Learned: (1) Asymmetric BER always points to a unidirectional element — TX at the far end, a span-specific issue, or an amplifier in one direction's chain. (2) TX power is often the last parameter operators check because RX power looks fine at the near end — but near-end RX power being within range does not mean far-end TX power is healthy. (3) "OSNR looks OK from CLI" does not mean OSNR is OK — the coherent modem's OSNR estimate has a ±1 dB uncertainty that can mask a 2–3 dBm TX power regression when combined with marginal spans. |
|---|

| 📁 Case Study 7.6 — FEC Utilization Creeping Toward Threshold (Micro-Bending)
Network Context: Regional backbone, buried cable infrastructure (HDPE duct), 8-span route, 320 km, 60 channels (200G DP-16QAM). No alarms. No customer complaints. Routine PM review (quarterly).
PM Trend Data — Channel 23 (worst-performing channel on the route):
Month

Key Observations: FEC-CW is doubling roughly every 60 days. OSNR declining monotonically — 1.4 dB over 5 months. No discrete event in OTDR (no new reflection events). Span loss increasing slowly: Span 5 has increased from 18.4 dB to 19.1 dB over 5 months. All other spans stable. Adjacent channels on same span also showing similar but less severe trends (all channels through span 5 are affected uniformly).
Diagnosis: Uniform degradation across all channels on one span (Span 5) rules out per-channel or node issues. Slow monotonic span loss increase without OTDR discrete events is the signature of distributed fiber micro-bending — the OTDR shows no point-reflection events because the loss is distributed along the cable, not concentrated at a splice or connector. Cable installation records for Span 5 reviewed: the duct section between km 158 and 173 was re-pulled in October of the prior year during a duct repair that required increased cable tension during re-installation. Micro-bending is mechanical deformation of the fiber core caused by lateral pressure from cable jacket or duct, often induced by excess installation tension.
Root Cause: Distributed micro-bending in Span 5 cable section (km 158–173) induced by excess tension during duct re-pull. Loss increasing gradually as mechanical stress redistributes along the cable. At current rate: FEC threshold will be reached in approximately 90 days.
Resolution: Replacement of the 15-km cable section (km 158–173) in a planned maintenance window. New cable installed with tension logging (maximum tension: 2.7 kN vs. the estimated 4.1 kN of the damaged pull). After replacement, Span 5 loss: 18.3 dB (within baseline). FEC-CW on all channels: returned to within 5% of January baseline within 24 hours.
Lessons Learned: (1) Uniform span-wide degradation without OTDR events is the micro-bending signature. Do not wait for an OTDR event — there will not be one. (2) Cable installation records (tension logs) are critical diagnostic inputs. If tension logs are not kept during installation, this root cause cannot be efficiently confirmed. (3) At 90 days to threshold, the 5-month PM review cadence was the only reason this was caught in time for a planned replacement. Quarterly PM reviews for all production spans are non-optional. |
|---|

### Chapter 7 Summary
These six case studies collectively demonstrate the breadth of failure modes that a production DWDM architect will encounter. The unifying theme is methodology: every case was solved by systematic data collection, layer-by-layer isolation, and disciplined hypothesis testing. Pattern recognition accelerates diagnosis — but only when built on a foundation of systematic data gathering, not shortcircuited by it.
#### Architect-Level Review Questions
- You are given the following PM snapshot: OSNR normal, received power 2.5 dBm below baseline, FEC-CW 8× baseline, FEC-UCW zero, all adjacent channels nominal, pattern only in one direction of transmission. Based on these data points alone, construct your ranked hypothesis list (at least three hypotheses), and identify which single CLI command output would most efficiently differentiate between your top two hypotheses.
- A channel shows gradual OSNR degradation over 6 weeks that affects all channels on span X uniformly, but the OTDR trace shows no new events. What is the most likely cause? What physical measurements would you commission to confirm, and what is the remediation?
- After a protection switch, the protect path shows 30% higher FEC-CW than the working path. OSNR and received power are within 0.5 dBm of the working-path values. What are the three most likely causes of the performance differential on the protect path?
- You are reviewing PM data from a 20-node network for a quarterly operational review. Describe the specific PM parameters you would export, the time range you would analyze, and the specific patterns that would trigger you to open a trouble ticket vs. flag for passive monitoring vs. dismiss as normal variation.
- A WSS failure is confirmed at a major hub node. You have a replacement card. Before beginning the hot-swap MOP, what pre-checks do you perform, and after card replacement, what is the minimum verification sequence required before closing the maintenance activity?

3.title: Chapter 8 — Optical Amplifier Operations
3.content_md:
# Chapter 8 — Optical Amplifier Operations

| Chapter Overview
Operational Objectives: Understand EDFA and Raman amplifier health monitoring, degradation prediction, and operational management. Master gain block replacement procedures and APC behavior analysis.
Field Competencies: Amplifier health parameter reading; pump laser lifetime estimation; N+1 replacement procedure; APC interaction analysis. |
|---|

## 8.1 EDFA Health Indicators
An **EDFA (Erbium-Doped Fiber Amplifier)** has five primary health parameters that must be monitored in production:

| Parameter | Normal Range | Degradation Threshold | Failure Mode Indicated |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pump Drive Current (mA) | 250–350 mA (module-specific) | >85% of rated maximum | Pump laser end-of-life (current rises to maintain constant output power as efficiency declines) |
| Pump Output Power (mW) | 100–200 mW (module-specific) | Declining vs. baseline at same current | Pump laser efficiency degradation; laser facet damage |
| Gain (dB) | Within ±0.3 dB of setpoint | Deviation >0.5 dB from setpoint | APC unable to maintain gain: pump failure, erbium depletion, hardware fault |
| Noise Figure (dB) | 5.0–7.0 dB (EDFA type-dependent) | >7.5 dB or >1 dB increase from baseline | Erbium fiber degradation; temperature effects; splice or coupler loss within amplifier |
| Module Temperature (°C) | 25–45°C (operating environment-dependent) | >50°C | Cooling failure; elevated ambient; airflow obstruction — accelerates pump aging |

The critical insight for EDFA monitoring: **APC (Automatic Power Control) hides pump degradation from output-power-based monitoring.** Because APC continuously increases pump drive current to maintain the gain setpoint as the pump laser ages, output power and gain remain stable while the pump is slowly dying. Only monitoring pump current directly reveals the degradation trend. Set pump current TCAs at 80% of rated maximum (warning) and 90% (critical).
## 8.2 Pump Laser Degradation and Lifetime Modeling
Semiconductor pump lasers (980 nm for EDFAs) degrade according to the **degradation rate model**, where threshold current increases and slope efficiency decreases over time. The primary driver of degradation rate is operating temperature — pump life roughly halves for every 10°C increase above the rated operating temperature (Arrhenius model). A pump rated for 100,000 hours (≈11.4 years) at 25°C junction temperature may have only 50,000 hours of life at 35°C junction temperature.
End-of-life prediction: Plot pump drive current over time on a quarterly basis. Fit a linear or polynomial trend to the data. Project the current trajectory to the rated maximum current value. The intersection point is the estimated remaining lifetime. This is the data presented in a proactive replacement request.
## 8.3 Gain Block Replacement and Saturation Events
In N+1 EDFA configurations, a failed amplifier module can be replaced without service impact by pre-activating the standby module before removing the failed one. The replacement procedure must be executed as a formal MOP with verification at each step.
Key steps for in-service EDFA replacement:
- Verify N+1 standby module is operational and gain-configured to match the primary
- Pre-activate standby: confirm output power matches primary within ±0.2 dBm
- Initiate hitless switch to standby: APC will converge on standby output within 10–30 seconds
- Verify all channels: power levels and FEC-CW within normal range on standby
- Remove failed primary module (hot-swap if platform supports)
- Install new primary module; configure gain setpoint identical to standby
- Verify new primary pump health (baseline current, output power, noise figure)
- Optionally revert to primary; verify during revert
**EDFA saturation events** occur when the total input power to the amplifier exceeds the saturation input power (typically –10 to –5 dBm total), causing gain compression. In practical terms, this occurs when a sudden large number of channels is added to a lightly loaded system without gain adjustment. Saturation effects: gain drops, noise figure increases, gain non-linearity across channels worsens. Detection: sudden drop in gain readout when channel count increases. APC will respond by increasing pump current — but if the saturation is severe, APC cannot recover gain to setpoint. Resolution: reduce input power (adjust VOA) or reconfigure EDFA operating point for the loaded channel count.
## 8.4 Raman Amplifier Operations
**Distributed Raman amplification** is used on ultra-long spans (>80 km) and submarine systems to reduce effective noise figure by providing gain within the transmission fiber. Raman pump wavelengths are typically 100 nm below the signal band (approximately 1450–1490 nm to amplify the C-band signal at 1530–1565 nm).
Operational considerations for Raman systems:
- Pump wavelength stability: Raman gain is wavelength-specific — pump wavelength drift affects which signal wavelengths receive gain. Monitor pump laser temperature and current stability. Raman pump lasers also have lifetime characteristics similar to EDFA pumps.
- Gain flatness: Raman gain is non-flat — it peaks at signals 13.2 THz above the pump. For flat gain across the full C-band, multiple Raman pump wavelengths are used simultaneously. Flatness must be verified with an OSA at span commissioning and after any pump replacement.
- Double Rayleigh Backscattering (DRB): A Raman-specific noise mechanism where signal photons scatter backward, re-scatter forward, and add coherent noise to the signal. DRB noise increases with Raman ON/OFF gain. Systems typically limit Raman net gain to 15–20 dB to control DRB penalty.
- Safety interlock: Raman pumps operate at >1 W — Class 4 laser hazard. Automatic safety shutoff (ALS — Automatic Laser Shutdown) must be configured and verified at commissioning. Never bypass ALS interlocks on a Raman amplifier.
## 8.5 APC (Automatic Power Control) Behavior
APC is the control loop that maintains constant EDFA output power (in constant-power mode) or constant gain (in constant-gain mode) despite varying input conditions. Understanding APC behavior is essential for predicting system response to network changes.
APC protects against: gradual span loss increase (compensates by increasing pump current), individual channel add/drop (adjusts total output to maintain per-channel target), and minor temperature-induced gain changes. APC cannot protect against: sudden complete span loss (LOS — APC response may cause transient power spike on surviving channels), very fast transients (<APC loop time, typically 100–500 ms), or pump laser failure beyond the APC compensation range.
Critical operational rule: **Any time you change channel loading state (add or remove more than ±10% of total channels), notify the amplifier engineer to verify APC response.** A sudden drop from 80 to 20 channels can cause APC to overcorrect, producing a transient power overshoot that can damage receiver components on the surviving 20 channels before APC settles.

| 📁 Case Study 8.1 — Replacing an ILA in a Live 96-Channel System
Context: ILA-4 on a 96-channel national backbone. EDFA has degraded (pump current at 94% of maximum — critical TCA active). No N+1 for the complete ILA shelf, but the EDFA amplifier module (gain block) is hot-swappable from the shelf. A spare gain block module is in regional inventory (4-hour transit). Maintenance window: 02:00–04:00 Saturday. Affected traffic: 96×100G channels, all carrying live services with 1+1 protection. All circuits have working protection paths via alternate route.
Pre-Maintenance (T–2 hours): Verify all 96 channels are protected and protection is ready. Execute test switch of all 96 circuits to protect path (force switch, verify, revert). Confirm all 96 protection paths active and error-free. Check NMS for any inhibited protections that might leave circuits unprotected. Found: 2 circuits with protection inhibited from a maintenance activity 8 days prior and never re-enabled. Re-enable both. Verify all 96 circuits protected.
MOP Execution (T+00:00 — 02:00 AM):
Step 1: Force-switch all 96 circuits to protect path (single NMS command, group switch). Verify all 96 circuits show protection active, no FEC-UCW events on any channel.
Step 2: Shutdown ILA-4 EDFA gain block output (CLI: shutdown amp output). Monitor NMS — confirm all circuits remain on protect path (no traffic impact because they were already switched to protect).
Step 3: Field tech at ILA-4 removes failed gain block (hot-swap, guided by CLI: the module confirms safe to remove after output shutdown).
Step 4: New gain block seated and powered. CLI: enable amp; wait for pump initialization (90 seconds). Verify pump current: 287 mA (new module — much lower than failed module's 380 mA). Gain setpoint: 22.0 dB. Actual gain: 22.1 dB (within spec).
Step 5: Enable ILA-4 output. CLI: no shutdown amp output. Monitor output power for all channels: within ±0.3 dBm of pre-maintenance targets. APC convergence: 45 seconds.
Step 6: Revert all 96 circuits from protect to working path (single NMS command). Monitor during revert: APS switch time average 34 ms. All 96 circuits confirmed on working path.
Step 7: Verify PM data: FEC-CW for all 96 channels, first 15-minute bin post-revert: within 5% of baseline. FEC-UCW: zero for all channels.
Total Maintenance Window Elapsed: 47 minutes (well within 2-hour window).
Lessons Learned: (1) The two circuits with inhibited protections were a near-miss — those circuits would have experienced a 10–15 minute outage during the EDFA shutdown if not discovered and re-enabled before the MOP began. Pre-MOP protection audit is not optional. (2) Group protection switch commands from NMS (all 96 at once) are faster and more consistent than per-circuit switching — verify your NMS supports group switch operations before writing the MOP. (3) New pump baseline data (287 mA) should be recorded immediately and set as the new TCA reference for this module. |
|---|

### Chapter 8 Summary
Optical amplifiers are the power plants of the DWDM network. Monitoring their health requires understanding that APC hides degradation from surface-level monitoring — pump current is the true health indicator. Proactive replacement based on pump current trending prevents emergency outages. Replacement procedures executed with pre-switched protection minimize service impact to zero seconds when properly planned.
#### Architect-Level Review Questions
