- An EDFA shows pump drive current at 88% of maximum over the past two weeks, with gain holding exactly at setpoint. No alarms are active. A manager asks: "Is there a problem with this amplifier?" Provide your complete technical answer, including what will happen if no action is taken.
- You need to increase channel loading on a span from 40 to 80 channels. Describe the APC behavior you expect during and after the channel addition event. What operational precautions do you take before the channel addition, and what do you verify immediately afterward?
- A Raman amplifier's pump current has been stable for 6 months but its pump wavelength has drifted 0.8 nm from nominal. What is the operational impact of this drift? How do you detect it, and what action is required?
- During an EDFA replacement MOP, the new module seats and initializes but shows gain of 19.2 dB against a setpoint of 22.0 dB. The APC is active. What is the most likely cause? What is your immediate action?
- Design an EDFA end-of-life prediction model for a production network with 25 EDFA modules across 8 ILA sites. Describe the data you would collect, the frequency of collection, the calculation method for remaining lifetime, and the threshold at which you would initiate a proactive replacement order.

3.title: Chapter 9 — Transponder and Coherent Modem Operations
3.content_md:
# Chapter 9 — Transponder and Coherent Modem Operations

| Chapter Overview
Operational Objectives: Master coherent modem health parameter interpretation, FEC engine analysis, and failure mode identification. Develop procedures for hot-swap, firmware upgrade, and pluggable optics management in production environments.
Field Competencies: DSP health analysis; FEC threshold management; transceiver failure classification; in-service firmware upgrade risk management. |
|---|

## 9.1 Coherent Modem Health Parameters
Modern coherent transceivers (Ciena WaveLogic 5e, Infinera ICE6, Nokia PSE-3) implement digital signal processing that both compensates for optical impairments and provides rich diagnostic data about the optical path and transceiver health. Key parameters:

| Parameter | Source | Healthy Range (100G DP-QPSK example) | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| TX Power (OPT) | Coherent TX laser monitor | 0 to +4 dBm (module-specific) | TX laser health; declining OPT = laser aging or thermal issue |
| RX Power (OPR) | Coherent receiver photodetector | –10 to –24 dBm (receiver range) | Received signal level; below minimum sensitivity = LOS or near-LOS |
| OSNR (estimated) | DSP noise estimation algorithm | ≥16 dB at receiver for BER < FEC threshold | Signal quality estimate; ±1 dB uncertainty vs. OSA measurement |
| Q-Factor | DSP post-equalization SNR estimate | ≥8.5 dB for 100G DP-QPSK | Most accurate transceiver health indicator; accounts for all impairments |
| CD Estimate | DSP blind equalization | ±50,000 ps/nm (typical DSP range) | Accumulated chromatic dispersion on path; out-of-range = route change |
| DGD Estimate | DSP polarization tracking | <30 ps (most transmission fiber) | Instantaneous PMD measurement; high DGD spikes = fiber mechanical stress |
| Carrier Freq. Offset | DSP carrier recovery | ±1 GHz from nominal | TX laser frequency accuracy; large offset = wavelength assignment mismatch or laser unlock |
| Pre-FEC BER | FEC engine (before correction) | <1×10⁻³ for SD-FEC at margin | Raw error rate before FEC correction; primary path quality metric |
| Post-FEC BER | FEC engine (after correction) | = 0 (error-free) | Service quality; any non-zero value = service impact |

## 9.2 FEC Engine Status and Soft-Decision FEC Threshold Management
Modern coherent transceivers use **Soft-Decision FEC (SD-FEC)**, which provides significantly greater coding gain than the legacy Hard-Decision FEC specified in G.709. SD-FEC achieves 11–12 dB coding gain with approximately 20% overhead, allowing operation at pre-FEC BER up to approximately 2×10⁻² (vs. HD-FEC threshold of approximately 3.8×10⁻³).
FEC threshold management in production:
- Safe operating zone: Pre-FEC BER below 1×10⁻⁴. FEC-CW count is low. System has substantial margin.
- Caution zone: Pre-FEC BER 1×10⁻⁴ to 1×10⁻². FEC-CW count elevated. System is operating with reduced margin. Any additional impairment may cause service impact. Immediate investigation required.
- Critical zone: Pre-FEC BER above 1×10⁻² (approaching SD-FEC threshold). FEC-UCW events likely. Service impact imminent or occurring. Emergency response required.
The management rule: **Any entry into the caution zone must trigger a trouble ticket within one hour, regardless of whether the customer is experiencing service impact.** Waiting for the first FEC-UCW event before opening a ticket means you are waiting for service impact before investigating. This is unacceptable professional practice.
## 9.3 Transceiver Failure Modes and Hot-Swap Procedures
Coherent transceiver failure modes can be classified into five categories:
- TX Failure (partial): TX laser power declining, carrier frequency drifting. Symptoms: declining Q-factor, increasing pre-FEC BER, declining OPT at far end. Unidirectional impairment (affects only the direction transmitted from the failing TX).
- TX Failure (complete): TX laser stops emitting. Symptoms: LOS at far-end receiver, ODUk-AIS propagation end-to-end.
- RX Failure (partial): Receiver sensitivity degradation — module requires higher received power than specified. Symptoms: BER elevated despite normal received power, Q-factor low relative to OSNR.
- RX Failure (complete): Receiver photodetector failure. Symptoms: LOS at local node, no received signal despite optical power on fiber.
- DSP Failure: Coherent DSP chip fault. Symptoms: DSP not locking, persistent carrier unlock alarm, random BER behavior that does not correlate with optical parameters, module may be thermally abnormal.
**Hot-swap procedure for coherent line card replacement:** Confirm the protection path is active and carrying traffic before pulling the card. The card replacement itself will cause a brief signal interruption during the hot-swap — if no protection is active, this will be service-affecting. Never pull a line card carrying live unprotected traffic. After card reinsertion: allow 2–3 minutes for DSP initialization and carrier lock. Verify: DSP locked, TX power at correct level, far-end RX power restored, FEC engine showing nominal pre-FEC BER.
## 9.4 Firmware Upgrades and Pluggable Optics
**In-service firmware upgrades** on coherent line cards are possible on many modern platforms but carry specific risks:
- Most coherent transceivers require a service interruption of 30–120 seconds during the firmware image activation phase, even on platforms advertising "hitless" upgrades. Verify with the vendor: is the interruption truly hitless, or does it require pre-switched protection?
- Always upgrade on protected circuits first. If the upgrade causes a longer-than-expected interruption, APS protects the service.
- Rollback capability: confirm the rollback firmware version is present on the module before initiating. If the upgrade fails mid-way, you must be able to return to the previous version.
- Batch size: do not upgrade all line cards simultaneously. Upgrade in batches of 10–20% of total capacity, with a 24-hour soak period between batches.
**Pluggable coherent optics (ZR, ZR+, QSFP-DD 400G ZR+):** These modules implement the full coherent PHY in a pluggable form factor plugged into a router or switch. Key operational differences from pizza-box transponders:
- Thermal management is critical — QSFP-DD ZR+ dissipates up to 20W. Router port airflow must be verified before deployment. Cage temperature must be monitored.
- Diagnostic data access may be through the router CLI rather than an optical NMS — operators must know which router CLI commands expose optical PM parameters (typically: show controllers CoherentDSP on IOS-XR).
- Configuration is split between the router (wavelength assignment, TX power, modulation) and the optical network (frequency assignment, routing). Coordination between IP and optical domains is essential.

| 📁 Case Study 9.1 — Coherent Modem Stuck in DSP Reconvergence Loop
Network Context: Metro core, 200 km route, Ciena WaveLogic 5e transponder, Channel 22 (400G DP-16QAM). Service: enterprise MPLS backbone.
Symptoms: Channel 22 has been cycling: DSP lock → DSP unlock → DSP lock → DSP unlock, with a cycle period of approximately 14 minutes. During unlock periods (approximately 2–3 minutes each cycle), FEC-UCW events accumulate (service-affecting). During lock periods, Q-factor is 10.4 dB (adequate, not excellent).
Initial Data: Received power: –19.1 dBm (within range, stable). OSNR: 21.3 dB (adequate). CD estimate (when locked): –340 ps/nm (within range). Pre-FEC BER when locked: 2.1×10⁻⁴ (slightly elevated, in caution zone). Adjacent channels: all stable.
Diagnosis: The cycling pattern (lock → unlock → lock) is not typical of a static optical impairment (which would either cause permanent unlock or stable degraded performance). It suggests a transient event that disrupts the DSP lock periodically. Candidates: (1) Periodic optical power fluctuation disrupting carrier lock; (2) DSP internal state machine bug; (3) Temperature-induced carrier frequency excursion beyond DSP acquisition range.
CLI monitoring of coherent modem parameters during lock cycle: At the moment of unlock, carrier frequency offset jumps from +0.3 GHz to +3.8 GHz — outside the standard acquisition range — and the DSP drops lock. After 2–3 minutes the carrier offset drifts back within range and the DSP re-acquires. TX laser temperature check at Node A (the transmit end): shows 8°C temperature swing over the 14-minute cycle, correlated exactly with the HVAC cycle in the equipment room. The TX laser temperature control loop in this specific WaveLogic 5e module has a slow response time that allows the laser frequency to drift 3.5 GHz during HVAC transients.
Root Cause: TX coherent laser frequency instability during HVAC thermal cycling, caused by a suboptimal laser temperature control response in this specific line card. A known firmware issue on this WaveLogic 5e version (firmware 3.4.1); a fix was released in version 3.4.3.
Resolution: (1) Immediate: forced DSP reset via CLI to check if a manual reset settles the lock (it does temporarily — confirms DSP state machine issue, not pure optical). (2) Firmware upgrade of the affected line card to version 3.4.3 (scheduled next maintenance window, 2 days later). Post-upgrade: no further lock cycling observed over 30-day monitoring period. HVAC temperature swing unchanged but carrier frequency offset now stable within ±0.2 GHz during HVAC transitions.
Lessons Learned: (1) Periodic cycling faults correlate with periodic environmental events (HVAC, diurnal temperature, traffic load cycles). Always check environmental logs when you see a time-periodic fault. (2) Coherent modem firmware release notes should be reviewed for any known issues affecting field-deployed versions — this firmware bug had been documented in the vendor's field service advisory for 3 months. (3) The forced DSP reset — used judiciously only after adequate data was collected — was a valid diagnostic tool (it revealed the state machine issue) but is not a resolution tool on its own. |
|---|

### Chapter 9 Summary
Coherent transceivers are the most complex and information-rich components in the DWDM network. Their DSP diagnostic data, when properly read and trended, provides unparalleled visibility into optical path health. Managing their life cycle — from commissioning baselines through FEC threshold monitoring, hot-swap replacement, and firmware upgrades — requires both technical precision and operational discipline.
#### Architect-Level Review Questions
- A 400G DP-16QAM transceiver reports Q-factor of 9.8 dB vs. a minimum threshold of 9.5 dB. Pre-FEC BER: 4×10⁻³. FEC-UCW: zero. Is this an emergency? What is your response?
- You are upgrading firmware on 48 coherent line cards across an 8-node network. Design the upgrade sequencing plan, including batch sizes, soak periods, rollback criteria, and the specific verification steps you perform after each batch before proceeding.
- A pluggable ZR+ module installed in a core router is showing elevated pre-FEC BER but the network management system shows no optical alarms (because the module is managed via the router, not the optical NMS). Describe your cross-domain diagnostic procedure to isolate whether the problem is in the router/module domain or the optical network domain.
- Explain why a TX failure on a coherent transponder always manifests as a unidirectional problem (one direction of transmission is affected, the other is not), whereas an OSNR degradation on a fiber span between two nodes manifests differently. Use this distinction to explain how you distinguish between the two fault scenarios from PM data alone.
- A coherent modem successfully locks and shows good Q-factor immediately after installation, but over the next 72 hours Q-factor declines by 1.8 dB while received power remains stable. What are the three most likely causes of post-installation Q-factor decline, and what diagnostic steps do you take to distinguish between them?

3.title: Chapter 10 — Protection Operations and Restoration
3.content_md:
# Chapter 10 — Protection Operations and Restoration

| Chapter Overview
Operational Objectives: Master the complete lifecycle of optical protection: switching mechanism, APS protocol behavior, switch type discipline, post-switch verification, GMPLS restoration signaling, and multi-failure scenario management.
Field Competencies: APS protocol interpretation; protection switch discipline; GMPLS restoration analysis; degraded network operation; restoration audit procedure. |
|---|

## 10.1 Optical 1+1 Switching and APS Protocol
**Optical 1+1 protection** provides a pre-connected protect path that carries the same signal as the working path simultaneously. At the receive end, the best-quality signal is selected. Protection switching (selecting the protect path exclusively) occurs in the following conditions:
- Signal Fail (SF): Working path signal loss (LOS or LOF) or BER above Signal Fail threshold (typically 1×10⁻³)
- Signal Degrade (SD): Working path BER above Signal Degrade threshold (typically 1×10⁻⁶) but below SF threshold
- Manual switch: Operator-initiated switch for maintenance purposes
- Forced switch: Operator-initiated switch that overrides all automatic requests
The **APS (Automatic Protection Switching) protocol** is defined in **ITU-T G.873.1** for OTN 1+1 protection. It uses the **K1 and K2 bytes** in the OTUk overhead to communicate protection state between the two end nodes. K1 carries the switch request (type and priority); K2 carries the bridge/switch state and channel information. Key K1/K2 byte values:

| K1 Byte (Hex) | Request Type | K2 Byte (Hex) | Bridge/Switch State |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0x00 | No Request — normal working path | 0x00 | Bridged to working, no switch |
| 0xA0 | Lockout of Protection | 0x10 | Bridge + Switch to protect |
| 0x60 | Forced Switch (FS) | 0x20 | Switch to protect (protection active) |
| 0x40 | Signal Fail (SF) on Working | 0x11 | Working path bridged only (pre-switch) |
| 0x20 | Signal Degrade (SD) on Working | – | – |
| 0x10 | Manual Switch | – | – |

Switching time for optical 1+1: The entire switching sequence — from signal fail detection to K1/K2 exchange to bridge/switch completion — must complete in ≤50 ms per ITU-T G.873.1. This includes: SF detection time (typically 3–10 ms), K1/K2 protocol exchange (3–5 ms propagation delay each way for long routes + protocol processing), and hardware switching (typically <5 ms for solid-state MEMS switch). The 50 ms budget is tight — any provisioning misconfiguration, excessive latency on the OSC path used for K1/K2 transport, or software processing delay can cause switching time SLA violations.
## 10.2 Switch Types and Operational Discipline
The three switch types require careful operational discipline:
- Automatic Switch: Initiated by the APS protocol in response to SF or SD. This is the normal protective function. Operators should not interfere with automatic switches in progress — let APS complete. After switch completes, verify the protect path is carrying traffic correctly.
- Manual Switch: Operator-initiated; lower priority than SF/SD. Can be overridden if automatic switch condition occurs while manual switch is active. Use for: maintenance activities that require clearing the working path without removing protection capability. Manual switch will revert when the manual switch command is cleared.
- Forced Switch: Highest operator priority; overrides automatic switch conditions. Does not revert if working path fails while forced switch is active. Use for: maintenance activities that require removing the working path from service while knowing the working path may be degraded. Critical: a forced switch removes the protection backup if the working path also fails while the force is active — double failure = service loss.
**Lockout of Protection:** Prevents any switching, including SF-triggered automatic switching. This is the most dangerous protection operation. Use only when maintenance work must be done on the protect path while the working path is carrying traffic. The working path has zero protection while lockout is active. Lockout must have an automatic expiration timer set — never apply a lockout without an end time. A forgotten lockout is one of the most common causes of preventable outages in production networks.
## 10.3 GMPLS Restoration and Manual Restoration
**GMPLS (Generalized Multiprotocol Label Switching) restoration** is a path-level recovery mechanism where the control plane dynamically computes and signals a new route for a disrupted optical path after a failure. Unlike 1+1 protection (pre-computed, pre-connected), GMPLS restoration is reactive — it computes and provisions a new path after the failure is detected.
GMPLS restoration timing is significantly longer than 1+1 protection switching (seconds to minutes vs. milliseconds) because it involves: failure detection, route computation (CSPF — Constrained Shortest Path First), RSVP-TE path setup signaling, optical cross-connect establishment at each node, and signal stabilization. GMPLS restoration is appropriate for services where 50 ms switching is not required — typically managed services with minutes-level recovery SLAs.
**Manual restoration** (fiber repair + re-commissioning) is the final fallback. Key steps after physical fiber repair:
- OTDR verification of repaired splice: loss <0.15 dB per splice
- End-to-end span loss verification: within ±0.5 dB of pre-fault value
- Amplifier re-convergence: allow APC to stabilize (2–5 minutes)
- Channel-by-channel power verification at the far end
- FEC-UCW verification: zero events across all channels for 5 minutes before closing
- APS revert from protect to working path (if applicable)
- Post-restoration PM review: confirm all channels returned to baseline performance
## 10.4 Multi-Failure Scenarios and Restoration Audit
When a network event involves multiple simultaneous or sequential failures, the restoration strategy must be prioritized by service priority and available resources. Multi-failure principles:
- Protect the protect path first: If the working path has failed and the protect path is at risk, stabilizing the protect path is Priority 1 — it is carrying live traffic.
- Service prioritization: Not all services have equal SLA value. Restore highest-priority services first. This requires maintaining a current service priority list — not created during the incident.
- Resource allocation: Field crews, spare equipment, and maintenance windows are finite. Multi-failure events require explicit resource allocation decisions, documented and approved by the incident commander.
- Degraded network operations: Operating without full protection coverage is acceptable for a defined period while repairs are underway. Document the reduced protection state, set a clear restoration deadline, and escalate if the deadline cannot be met.
After any major restoration event, a **Restoration Audit** must confirm: (1) all circuits are on their designated working paths, (2) all protection groups are in armed (ready) state, (3) all lockouts and forced switches have been cleared, (4) PM data from all affected circuits shows return to baseline, and (5) the as-built record has been updated to reflect any path changes made during restoration.

| 📁 Case Study 10.1 — Double Cable Cut: Operating a Degraded Network
Network Context: Regional backbone ring, 5 nodes (A–B–C–D–E–A). Working paths: clockwise. Protect paths: counter-clockwise. 60 channels. All services have 1+1 protection. Two simultaneous cable cuts occur during a contractor excavation incident: Span B-C (working) and Span D-E (working). Both cuts occur at T+00:00.
T+00:00 — Double Cut Occurs: APS activates for all circuits on Span B-C: counter-clockwise (A-E-D-C) protect path activated. Simultaneously, APS activates for all circuits on Span D-E: circuits that were protecting via D-E are now also disrupted.
Impact Assessment: Circuits on B-C that protect via A-E-D-C: some of these pass through the D-E span — those circuits' protect paths are also cut. Result: circuits whose working path is B-C AND whose protect path passes through D-E have BOTH paths simultaneously broken. These circuits are unprotected and experiencing outage. Calculation: 18 circuits meet this condition (their counter-clockwise protect path traverses D-E). 42 circuits successfully protected via alternate routing.
T+02:00 — Architect Assessment: 18 circuits are down (both paths broken). 42 circuits are on protect paths (exposed — no redundancy). Network is in highly degraded state. Two simultaneous fiber repairs are required.
Resource Allocation Decision: Two fiber repair crews dispatched simultaneously. Crew 1 to Span B-C (repairs restore working path for 60 circuits, including 42 currently on protect paths). Crew 2 to Span D-E (repairs restore working path and protect path for the 18 failed circuits). Priority: Crew 2's repair restores the most critical services (18 circuits completely dark). Crew 1's repair removes the protection exposure on 42 circuits. Decision documented by Incident Commander.
Operational Discipline During Degraded State: (1) Inhibit all non-essential maintenance activities immediately (no changes to the network that could affect the 42 circuits on exposed protect paths). (2) Issue a "degraded network" advisory to NOC — any alarm on the 42 circuits on protect paths is to be escalated immediately (no normal SLA timer, emergency escalation). (3) Document the 18 affected circuits with SLA implications for customer notification and contractual obligations. (4) Confirm no lockouts or forced switches are active anywhere in the network (a lockout on a protect path during this event would make a third set of circuits unprotectable).
T+04:15 — Span D-E Repair Complete: 18 circuits restored on working path. APS reverted. All 18 circuits verified error-free. 42 circuits still on protect paths (Span B-C still under repair).
T+06:50 — Span B-C Repair Complete: All 42 circuits reverted from protect to working path. All 60 circuits verified on working paths, error-free. Full protection restored.
Post-Incident Restoration Audit: All 60 circuits confirmed on working paths. All 60 protection groups armed. No lockouts or forced switches remaining. PM data reviewed — the 18 circuits that were completely dark accumulated 4 hours and 15 minutes of UAS. SLA breach documentation prepared for 18 affected customers.
Lessons Learned: (1) A ring topology with working and protect on different physical routes can still fail simultaneously if the two routes share geography at any point. This event was caused by a single contractor in a shared right-of-way. Pre-route-diversity analysis should verify no geographic proximity between working and protect paths. (2) Maintaining a current, verified service priority list before incidents occur is essential for resource allocation decisions. Creating one during the incident is too slow. (3) The degraded network advisory to NOC — issued at T+02:00 — was the correct action. It changed the operational posture for the 42 exposed circuits before any additional events occurred. |
|---|

### Chapter 10 Summary
Protection operations require both technical precision (APS protocol behavior, switching time verification) and operational discipline (switch type selection, lockout management, post-switch verification). The worst-case scenarios — double failures, protect path degradation, simultaneous multi-node events — require pre-planned decision frameworks, not improvisation under pressure. The restoration audit after every major event is the mechanism that ensures the network is truly restored, not just apparently restored.
#### Architect-Level Review Questions
- You observe that K1 byte value from Node A toward Node B is 0xA0. What protection state does this indicate? Is service protected? What is the risk associated with this state, and what would you check first?
- A protection switch was triggered by SD (Signal Degrade) on the working path 6 days ago. The working path SD condition has since cleared, but the circuit remains on the protect path. What could cause this? What is the correct resolution?
- You are designing a 1+1 protection scheme for a financial exchange customer requiring 50 ms protection switching. The route is 2,800 km (transcontinental). Calculate the one-way propagation delay and determine whether 50 ms switching is physically achievable on this route. If not, what alternatives would you propose?
- A GMPLS restoration fails to complete because the control plane RSVP-TE path setup is rejected at an intermediate node (insufficient resources). The circuit remains down. What are the possible reasons for the RSVP rejection, and what is your manual fallback procedure?
- During a restoration audit 2 hours after a major fiber repair, you discover that 3 circuits are still on their protect paths — they did not revert to working paths after the fiber repair. Describe exactly how you would verify the state of each of these 3 circuits and what actions you would take to restore them to their correct working paths.

3.title: Chapter 11 — Capacity Management in Production
3.content_md:
# Chapter 11 — Capacity Management in Production

| Chapter Overview
Operational Objectives: Master day-2 wavelength operations: provisioning, re-grooming, defragmentation, C-band exhaustion response, and traffic migration. Understand how to produce accurate capacity reports and make capacity decisions under operational constraints.
Field Competencies: Wavelength assignment discipline; flex-grid defragmentation; L-band activation procedure; traffic migration without customer impact; capacity audit methodology. |
|---|

## 11.1 Day-2 Operations: Ongoing Wavelength Provisioning and Re-Grooming
Day-2 operations encompass all wavelength-level changes on a production network after initial commissioning. These include: adding new wavelengths, removing decommissioned wavelengths, re-grooming services onto different wavelengths for capacity optimization, and upgrading wavelengths (e.g., from 100G to 400G on the same frequency slot).
Each Day-2 operation must be preceded by: path computation (power budget, OSNR calculation for the proposed wavelength on the proposed path), margin verification (sufficient headroom exists for the proposed configuration), protection path availability (confirm protect path has capacity for the new service), and impact analysis (does the new channel's power interact with existing channels in OSNR-degrading ways?).
For **OSNR-critical routes** (spans approaching minimum margin), adding a new channel can slightly increase the total input power to each amplifier, causing a minor APC adjustment that slightly reduces per-channel output power. This is a second-order effect but becomes significant on systems with <2 dB margin. Model the impact before provisioning on margin-critical routes.
## 11.2 Fragmentation Management and Defragmentation
**Spectral fragmentation** occurs in flex-grid ROADM systems over time as wavelengths of different bandwidths are provisioned and deprovisioned, leaving gaps in the optical spectrum that are too small to accommodate new services. A 37.5 GHz gap between two 50 GHz-spaced channels cannot accommodate a new 50 GHz channel — it is wasted capacity.
Fragmentation measurement: **Fragmentation Index (FI)** = 1 – (largest available contiguous spectrum block) / (total available spectrum). FI = 0 is perfectly unfragmented; FI = 1 means all available capacity is in unusable fragments. A FI above 0.4 on any route is a trigger for defragmentation planning.
**Hitless wavelength re-arrangement** in flex-grid systems allows services to be moved to a different frequency slot while maintaining continuous traffic. The procedure requires a coherent transceiver capable of in-service frequency changes (most modern WaveLogic and equivalent platforms support this), the ability to pre-compute a new frequency assignment that resolves the fragmentation, and verification that the new slot has adequate OSNR for the service. The re-arrangement is performed one circuit at a time, with verification after each move.
## 11.3 C-Band Exhaustion and L-Band Activation
When C-band capacity (approximately 4.8 THz of spectrum, supporting 96 channels at 50 GHz spacing) is approaching exhaustion, the next expansion step is L-band (1565–1625 nm, approximately 5 THz additional spectrum). L-band activation is a significant engineering undertaking:
- EDFA replacement or supplementation: C-band EDFAs do not amplify the L-band. Separate L-band EDFA modules are required at every amplification point. In some cases, existing shelf slots can accommodate L-band amplifier cards; in others, new shelves are required.
- Raman amplification at long spans: L-band signals experience higher attenuation in standard G.652 fiber than C-band signals at long spans. Raman amplification may be required to achieve adequate OSNR on spans longer than 80 km.
- ROADM WSS upgrade: C-band WSS modules typically cover only 1527–1565 nm. L-band extension requires either a second WSS covering the L-band or a wideband WSS (CL-band).
- SRS (Stimulated Raman Scattering) power transfer: In a CL-band system, C-band signals at high power act as a Raman pump for L-band signals, causing power transfer from short wavelengths to long wavelengths. This power tilt must be modeled and compensated by launch power adjustment.
- L-band OSC: A new OSC wavelength outside the L-band is required for management signaling between nodes.
## 11.4 Traffic Migration and Capacity Audit
**Traffic migration** — moving services from one wavelength or path to another without customer impact — is one of the highest-risk Day-2 operations. The procedure requires: protection activation on the current path, provisioning of the new path, verification that the new path is error-free, service switch to the new path, verification of service on new path, and deprovisioning of the old path. Never deprovision the old path until the service is verified on the new path. This sounds obvious — it is violated regularly in practice.
A **Capacity Audit** produces an accurate, current view of network capacity and utilization for planning purposes. Components:

| Audit Component | Data Source | Output |
|---|---|---|
| Wavelength inventory by span | NMS wavelength registry | Table: spans vs. loaded/available wavelengths, by frequency |
| Margin assessment by span | PM data + link budget calculations | Available OSNR margin per span; risk-ranked margin list |
| Fragmentation index by route | Flex-grid WSS slot assignment data | FI per route; identified defragmentation opportunities |
| Demand forecast | Sales pipeline + historical growth rates | Projected time-to-exhaustion per route; capacity investment triggers |
| Amplifier headroom | Amplifier PM data (pump current) | Remaining pump life estimates; proactive replacement schedule |

| 🟢 Architect Reasoning Block 11.1
Dilemma: "Your C-band is 90% loaded on a critical route. What are your next 6 decisions?"
Decisions in Order: (1) Verify actual vs. apparent capacity: Is the 90% figure based on provisioned wavelengths or actually in-service wavelengths? Deprovisioned but not cleaned-up wavelength assignments are common. An audit may reveal 5–10% recoverable capacity. (2) Assess fragmentation: On flex-grid systems, 90% loaded may still have capacity for additional services if defragmentation is performed. Check FI. (3) Evaluate modulation upgrade potential: Can existing 100G DP-QPSK wavelengths be upgraded to 200G DP-16QAM on the same frequency slot? This doubles capacity per slot on margin-adequate spans — check per-span OSNR against 200G minimum threshold. (4) Order L-band equipment: Initiate the procurement process now. L-band activation takes 6–12 months from order to in-service on a complete route. At 90% utilization with typical growth rates, you are 3–6 months from exhaustion. Procurement lead time is your enemy. (5) Establish a provisioning freeze trigger: Define the utilization level (e.g., 95%) at which new service provisioning requires architect approval — to prevent salespeople committing to capacity that doesn't exist. (6) Prioritize defragmentation + modulation upgrades for immediate capacity relief: These can be done in weeks vs. months for new equipment, and may provide 20–30% additional effective capacity within the existing infrastructure. |
|---|

### Chapter 11 Summary
Capacity management is a continuous operational discipline, not a one-time planning exercise. The production architect who understands fragmentation dynamics, modulation upgrade pathways, and L-band expansion timing can deliver significant capacity relief within existing infrastructure while the longer-term expansion is prepared. The capacity audit is the instrument of operational intelligence that makes all of these decisions data-driven.
#### Architect-Level Review Questions
- A route has C-band utilization at 92% with a Fragmentation Index of 0.28. Sales has committed a new 400G service on this route for next month. Describe the specific sequence of actions you take to assess whether this service can be accommodated without new equipment.
- You are proposing a modulation upgrade on a route (100G DP-QPSK → 200G DP-16QAM) to double capacity per slot. The route has 7 spans with measured OSNR margin ranging from +2.1 dB to +5.4 dB at 100G. For 200G DP-16QAM, the minimum required OSNR is 4 dB above the 100G minimum. Which specific spans are eligible for the upgrade, and which are not? What options exist for the ineligible spans?
- Describe the complete procedure for migrating a 100G enterprise wavelength from Channel 31 to Channel 47 without customer impact. List every step, the verification command at each step, and the rollback action if any step fails.
- An L-band expansion requires installing new EDFA modules at 14 ILA sites. Some sites can accommodate the new cards in existing shelves; others require new shelf installations. How do you sequence this work to minimize the time during which the route operates with mixed C-band-only and CL-band capabilities?
- Your capacity audit reveals a route where three 100G wavelengths have been provisioned in the NMS but no actual circuits are in service on them (they were provisioned for a customer project that was cancelled 14 months ago). The provisioned wavelengths are consuming spectrum on a near-exhausted route. What is the procedure for safely deprovisioning these wavelengths?

3.title: Chapter 12 — Software, NMS, and Controller Operations
3.content_md:
# Chapter 12 — Software, NMS, and Controller Operations

| Chapter Overview
Operational Objectives: Master NMS database management, software upgrade procedures, SDN controller operations, and the resilience discipline needed to operate effectively when management systems fail.
Field Competencies: NMS backup/restore; software upgrade MOP design; NETCONF/RESTCONF troubleshooting; controller failover behavior; automated workflow risk assessment. |
|---|

## 12.1 NMS Database Integrity and Software Upgrades
The **Network Management System (NMS)** database is the authoritative record of network configuration as understood by the management plane. NMS database integrity means the NMS record matches the actual state of every network element. Discrepancies arise from: NE configuration changes made via CLI that bypass the NMS, NE software upgrades that change the configuration schema, and NMS communication failures that cause missed configuration change events.
**NMS database maintenance discipline:**
- Daily backup: Automated backup of NMS database to off-system storage. Two backup generations minimum (current and previous day). Backup integrity verified by automated restore test monthly.
- Resync on demand: After any CLI-based NE configuration change, trigger an NMS resync of the affected NE to ensure the NMS database reflects the actual NE state. Most NMS platforms have a "resync" or "reconcile" function for this purpose.
- Quarterly inventory reconciliation: Compare NMS inventory records against physical plant: every card, every module, every port. Discrepancies are either configuration errors (NMS wrong) or undocumented hardware changes (plant wrong). Both must be resolved.
**Software upgrades on DWDM NEs:** NE software upgrades are among the highest-risk operations in the DWDM lifecycle. A failed upgrade can leave an NE in an unusable state, potentially dropping all services it carries. MOP structure for NE software upgrades must include: pre-upgrade state capture (full configuration backup), upgrade staging (image download to NE, integrity check), pre-upgrade protection switch (all circuits to protect paths), upgrade execution (activate), post-upgrade verification (all circuits restored, NE responsive, PM collection resumed), and rollback procedure (revert to previous software image if verification fails).
## 12.2 SDN Controller and NETCONF/RESTCONF Operations
Modern DWDM networks increasingly use **SDN (Software-Defined Networking) controllers** for path computation and automated provisioning. The controller interfaces with NEs using **NETCONF (RFC 6241)** over SSH (port 830) or **RESTCONF (RFC 8040)** over HTTPS (port 443). Key operational points:
- NETCONF session verification: Confirm the controller has active NETCONF sessions to all managed NEs before relying on controller-reported state. A failed NETCONF session means the controller's view of that NE is stale. CLI verification: show netconf sessions on the NE; confirm the controller's IP address appears in the active session list.
- Controller failover: During SDN controller failover (active to standby controller), the data plane continues operating — the controller only manages configuration and provisioning. Services in service remain in service. However, no new provisioning and no automated protection actions can be initiated during the failover period (typically 30–120 seconds). Ensure operations teams are aware of this limitation.
- Intent-based provisioning: SDN controllers accept high-level service intents (e.g., "provision 100G service from Node A to Node B") and compute the detailed configuration. Always verify the computed path matches expectations before accepting — controllers can compute sub-optimal or even unexpected paths if topology data is stale.
- RESTCONF troubleshooting: When a RESTCONF call fails, check: HTTP return code (400 = malformed request, 401 = authentication failure, 404 = resource not found, 409 = resource conflict, 500 = server error), authentication credentials, SSL certificate validity, and YANG model version compatibility between controller and NE.
## 12.3 Alarm Forwarding and Automated Provisioning
SNMP trap forwarding from DWDM NEs to OSS (Operations Support Systems) and BSS (Business Support Systems) is the mechanism by which optical network events are integrated into the broader operational workflow. Key operational rules:
- Configure SNMP trap destinations for at minimum two independent OSS receivers (primary and secondary). A single trap destination with no backup means OSS is blind during primary OSS maintenance.
- Verify trap forwarding quarterly: generate a known test alarm (e.g., manually trigger a TCA via CLI), confirm the trap appears at the OSS, and confirm the OSS generates the expected trouble ticket.
- For automated provisioning workflows: define clearly which operations can be automated (new wavelength provisioning on pre-validated routes, protection switch operations, PM threshold configuration) and which must remain manual (changes to amplifier gain setpoints, DCM modifications, protection lockout). Automation of high-impact operations without human review is an operational risk that is not worth the efficiency gain.

### Chapter 12 Summary
Management systems are not passive tools — they are operational infrastructure that requires the same discipline of maintenance, verification, and backup as the network elements themselves. An NMS that doesn't reflect reality, a controller with stale topology, or an SNMP forwarding chain with a silent failure point are all direct risks to production operations. The architect who treats management systems as first-class operational concerns will avoid a significant category of operational failures.
#### Architect-Level Review Questions
- The NMS shows 68 provisioned wavelengths on a route. A physical inspection of the NE shows 71 wavelengths provisioned via CLI. How do you identify the three discrepant wavelengths, determine why they exist outside the NMS, and decide what action to take for each?
- During an NE software upgrade, the upgrade fails at the image activation step. The NE is now unresponsive via NETCONF but remains reachable via SSH (CLI). The NE is carrying 40 circuits, all of which are currently on protect paths (you pre-switched before upgrade). Describe your recovery procedure step by step.
- Your SDN controller is indicating that Node C has been unreachable for 4 hours (NETCONF session down). A technician manually made 3 configuration changes at Node C during the outage. What are your priorities and actions when NETCONF connectivity is restored?
- Design an automated provisioning workflow for adding new 100G wavelengths on a production network. Specify exactly which steps in the workflow must remain human-approved vs. can be automated, and justify each decision.
- An automated PM threshold change was pushed to all 45 NEs in the network via the SDN controller. The change incorrectly tightened the OSNR TCA threshold by 3 dB, triggering 847 false TCA alarms simultaneously. Describe your immediate response and the remediation procedure.

3.title: Chapter 13 — Security in Production DWDM Networks
3.content_md:
# Chapter 13 — Security in Production DWDM Networks

| Chapter Overview
Operational Objectives: Understand the physical, optical, and logical security requirements of production DWDM networks. Master Layer 1 encryption technology, management plane security hardening, and regulatory compliance for critical infrastructure.
Field Competencies: Optical interception risk assessment; OPE configuration; management plane RBAC; NERC CIP compliance applicability; fiber tap incident response. |
|---|

## 13.1 Physical Security and Optical Interception
The most fundamental vulnerability in any fiber network is the physical plant. Optical fiber can be tapped by bending the fiber sharply enough to cause evanescent field coupling — a method that extracts a fraction of the light without interrupting the signal. A competent fiber tap using commercial equipment can extract signal with less than 0.5 dBm power reduction at the receiver — below the detection threshold of most power-monitoring-based TCA systems.
Physical security requirements for critical DWDM infrastructure:
- Splice enclosure security: Above-ground splice cases should be tamper-evident sealed. Underground manholes and handholes should have tamper-indicating seals that can be checked during routine inspections.
- Hut and POP security: Two-factor physical access control (card + PIN or biometric) at all unmanned amplifier sites. Access logs reviewed monthly. Video surveillance at key sites.
- Fiber route security: High-risk sections (government facilities, financial districts, international borders) require above-standard physical security — conduit within conduit, vibration monitoring, or intrusion detection systems on the cable itself.
- Optical Time Domain Reflectometry monitoring: Some advanced systems include continuous OTDR monitoring that can detect new insertion points (a tap requiring a bend point would appear as a new reflection event in the continuous OTDR). This is available on select vendor platforms as an add-on module.
## 13.2 Layer 1 Encryption and Management Plane Security
**OTN Payload Encryption (OPE)** is the standard approach to Layer 1 encryption in DWDM networks. OPE encrypts the ODUk payload at line rate, before OTN framing, using AES-256. Even if the optical signal is physically intercepted, the payload is encrypted and unreadable without the encryption key. OPE standards: IEEE 802.1AEbw (MACsec profile for OTN) and ETSI TS 103 523 (ETSI quantum-safe Layer 1 encryption).
Key OPE operational considerations:
- Encryption keys must be managed by a key management server (KMS) that is independent of the optical NMS. Key rotation policy (typically every 24 hours or on demand) must be configured and verified.
- OPE adds latency: encryption/decryption processing time is typically 1–5 μs per node. For latency-sensitive customers, confirm that OPE latency is within SLA before deploying.
- OPE does not protect the OTN overhead (TTI, PM bytes, APS bytes) — these remain in the clear. The TTI string should therefore not contain sensitive information (customer names, service IDs that reveal network topology).
**Management plane security hardening:**
- CLI: SSH only (no Telnet). SSH version 2. RSA key length ≥ 4096 bits. Password complexity enforced. Account lockout after 5 failed attempts.
- NETCONF: TLS mutual authentication (both controller and NE authenticate each other with certificates). Certificate authority managed by network operations team, not the NE vendor.
- RBAC (Role-Based Access Control): Four tiers minimum: Read-Only (NOC monitoring), Operations (alarm handling, service switching), Engineer (configuration changes with change authorization), Admin (account management, software upgrades). Separate credentials for each role — no shared passwords.
- NMS access: VPN mandatory for remote access. Multi-factor authentication for all NMS logins. Session timeout: 15 minutes inactive.
## 13.3 NERC CIP Compliance and Incident Response
**NERC CIP (North American Electric Reliability Corporation Critical Infrastructure Protection)** standards apply to electric utilities and their communication networks — including DWDM networks used to carry protection relay and SCADA traffic for the bulk electric system. Key applicable standards:
- CIP-005: Electronic Security Perimeter — defines the boundary of the secure network zone containing BES (Bulk Electric System) Cyber Systems. DWDM NEs that are accessible from the same network as BES Cyber Systems must be within the ESP or protected by it.
- CIP-006: Physical Security — physical access to DWDM equipment carrying BES traffic must be controlled, logged, and audited.
- CIP-007: Systems Security Management — patch management, port and service management, malicious code prevention, and security event monitoring for DWDM systems within scope.
- CIP-010: Configuration Change Management and Vulnerability Assessments — changes to in-scope systems (including DWDM NE software upgrades) require change management authorization and documentation.
**Incident Response for Suspected Fiber Tap:** If a customer reports concern about interception, or if monitoring detects an anomalous insertion event:
- Document the first indication of concern with timestamp
- Pull OTDR data from the nearest automated OTDR system (if available)
- Dispatch field crew for physical inspection of the suspected segment — check splice enclosures, manholes, and any access points for evidence of tampering
- Do NOT notify the potential attacker by any network action that would reveal you have detected the intrusion
- Escalate to security officer and legal counsel — this is a law enforcement matter, not a network operations matter
- Preserve all evidence (OTDR traces, access logs, surveillance video) without alteration
- Activate OPE on affected circuits immediately if not already enabled

| 🟢 Architect Reasoning Block 13.1
Dilemma: "A customer demands proof that their wavelength cannot be tapped. What do you show them?"
Answer: You cannot prove that a signal cannot be physically tapped — any optical signal on a fiber can be physically intercepted given physical access to the fiber. What you CAN prove is that even if the signal is intercepted, the payload cannot be read. Show the customer: (1) OPE configuration records showing AES-256 encryption is active on their ODUk path end-to-end. (2) Key management records showing key rotation policy and frequency. (3) Physical security documentation for all sites on their path: access control logs, tamper-evident seal inspection records. (4) OTDR baseline traces — demonstrating that the optical path has been characterized and any new insertion point would produce a measurable change in the OTDR signature. (5) If available: continuous OTDR monitoring system status showing no anomalous events detected on their path. The correct technical statement is: "Your wavelength uses AES-256 payload encryption. Even if someone physically accesses the fiber and extracts the optical signal, the payload is mathematically unbreakable with current technology. We can prove encryption is active and key management is secure. We cannot prove the fiber will never be accessed — no carrier can — but we can prove the data is protected if it is." |
|---|

### Chapter 13 Summary
Security in production DWDM networks spans four domains: physical plant security, optical layer interception countermeasures, Layer 1 encryption, and management plane hardening. No single measure is sufficient — defense-in-depth across all four domains is the minimum acceptable standard for networks carrying critical infrastructure or sensitive customer data. NERC CIP compliance adds a regulatory dimension that requires structured change management and documentation discipline beyond standard operational practice.
#### Architect-Level Review Questions
- You are designing a new DWDM route for a government customer who requires confirmation that their data cannot be intercepted. Describe every security measure you would implement, from physical fiber route selection through encryption configuration, and explain the reasoning for each measure.
- A NOC analyst reports that an OTDR monitoring system has flagged a new low-amplitude event (0.02 dB) at km 34.7 of a span carrying financial exchange traffic. The last OTDR baseline showed no event at this location. What is your immediate response, and how do you distinguish between a potential fiber tap and a benign cause (settlement, temperature effect)?
- Your network carries SCADA traffic for a regional electric utility. A security audit flags that you do not have MFA (multi-factor authentication) enabled for CLI access to the DWDM NEs on this utility's path. The vendor says MFA requires a software upgrade. Describe your risk mitigation approach while you prepare for the upgrade.
- OPE key rotation from the KMS server fails due to a KMS software bug. The encryption keys on an in-service circuit are now 72 hours past their scheduled rotation. What are your options? What is the security risk of extended key use? What is the operational risk of initiating a manual key change?
- Explain why the OTN overhead (TTI, PM bytes) is not encrypted by OPE, and describe the security implications of this for a production network. What operational discipline should you apply to TTI string content as a result?

3.title: Chapter 14 — Level 3 Capstone: Production Network Crisis Scenario
3.content_md:
# Chapter 14 — Level 3 Capstone: Production Network Crisis Scenario

| Chapter Overview
This is the final qualification exercise for the DWDM Architect Program Level 3 certification. Candidates must demonstrate mastery of all operational domains covered in this book through a complex, multi-failure production scenario. The capstone is open-book (this text may be referenced) but must be completed independently. All 6 deliverables are required for certification sign-off.
Assessment Criteria: Technical accuracy, operational reasoning quality, prioritization correctness, communication clarity, and systemic thinking in the post-incident improvement recommendations. |
|---|

## 14.1 Scenario Brief: NexusCarrier National Backbone Event
**Network Overview:** NexusCarrier operates a national DWDM backbone connecting 14 cities across a meshed topology. Core platform: Ciena WaveLogic 5e at terminal nodes, Nokia 1830 PSS at hub/ROADM nodes. 96-channel C-band loading, mix of 100G, 200G, and 400G services. Network carries: carrier ethernet services for enterprise customers, sub-sea landing station connectivity (international traffic), financial exchange wavelengths (latency-sensitive, highest SLA), government/utility communications (NERC CIP scope), and residential broadband backbone traffic.
**Situation Brief — Event begins at 22:47 on a Tuesday:**

| ⚠ Active Incident — Time: 22:47   SLA Timers Running
Event 1 (T+00:00, 22:47): Major fiber cut on the Chicago–Indianapolis backbone span (Span CHI-IND, 380 km). The cut affects both working and protect fiber cables (both cables routed in same trench — single point of failure). 84 circuits lose both working and protect paths simultaneously. 12 circuits that use alternate routing through Louisville remain in service on protect paths.
Event 2 (T+00:03, 22:50): EDFA module failure at Denver ILA-3 (coincident with the fiber cut — power fluctuation during cut event caused an EDFA pump to latch into fault state). The Denver amplifier failure causes OSNR degradation on 22 channels traversing that amplifier. FEC-UCW events beginning on 6 channels. 16 channels degraded but still in FEC tolerance.
Event 3 (T+00:08, 22:55): NMS server goes offline. The NMS server experienced a software exception triggered by the alarm storm (over 2,000 alarms in 8 minutes exceeded the NMS event queue capacity). The NMS is unreachable. All nodes still have CLI access. The SDN controller remains operational.
Services at Risk:
•  14 financial exchange wavelengths (SLA: 50 ms switching, currently fully affected — both paths down on most)
•  6 government NERC CIP circuits (on affected paths)
•  28 enterprise ethernet services
•  36 residential broadband backbone circuits
Available Resources: 1 ILA-3 Denver spare EDFA module (correct model, on-site). No spare cable for CHI-IND (nearest depot: 6 hours). Field crew in Chicago (available in 90 min). Field crew in Indianapolis (available in 75 min). NMS team: 2 engineers working on NMS recovery. SDN controller: online, has stale topology data (last sync was 4 minutes ago at T-1 min before NMS failure).
SLA Timers Active: Financial exchange wavelengths: 50 ms switching SLA already breached (both paths down). Enterprise SLA: 4-hour MTTR. Government circuits: 2-hour MTTR. Residential: 8-hour MTTR. |
|---|

## 14.2 Capstone Deliverables
Candidates must produce all six deliverables as formal documents. Each must be clear, structured, and technically defensible.
### Deliverable 1: Fault Triage Document
Produce a prioritized fault list with the following structure: Fault ID, Description, Affected Service Count, SLA Impact, Priority (1–5), Reasoning for Priority Assignment, First Diagnostic Action. Minimum 8 distinct fault conditions identified and prioritized.
*Guidance: Priority assignment must account for simultaneous considerations: SLA urgency, number of services affected, ability to take corrective action now (vs. waiting for field crews), and dependencies between fault resolutions.*
### Deliverable 2: Step-by-Step Restoration MOP
Produce a complete Method of Procedure for the CHI-IND fiber cut restoration and the Denver EDFA replacement. Each MOP must include: Pre-conditions, numbered execution steps with CLI commands (platform-appropriate), verification steps with expected outputs, rollback procedure, and acceptance criteria. Minimum 20 execution steps across both MOPs.
*Required CLI commands must be valid for the stated vendor platforms (Ciena WaveLogic 5e and Nokia 1830 PSS). Use CLI reference from Chapter 4.*
### Deliverable 3: Customer Communication Drafts
Produce two customer communications:
- Technical Communication (for the customer's network operations team): Detailed status update at T+30 minutes. Include: confirmed root causes, affected circuits (by service type, not customer name), current service status, restoration timeline estimate, and technical actions in progress.
- Executive Communication (for the customer's CTO/VP): High-level status at T+2 hours. Non-technical language. Include: impact summary, what NexusCarrier is doing, when service will be restored, and what NexusCarrier is doing to prevent recurrence.
### Deliverable 4: Root Cause Analysis Report
Complete a formal 5-Why RCA for each of the three concurrent events. Each RCA must: identify the root cause, identify the contributing causes, identify the systemic cause, and specify a corrective action that prevents recurrence. The CHI-IND fiber cut RCA must specifically address the single point of failure in the cable routing (both working and protect in same trench).
### Deliverable 5: Post-Incident Improvement Recommendations
Produce a minimum of 6 specific, implementable recommendations addressing: physical plant route diversity, NMS resilience, EDFA spare inventory policy, alarm storm handling, SLA design for dual-failure scenarios, and any additional systemic improvements identified during your analysis. Each recommendation must include: current state, proposed change, implementation effort estimate (Low/Medium/High), and risk reduction quantification.
### Deliverable 6: Architect Sign-Off — 15 Operational Decisions Documented
Document 15 specific operational decisions you made or would make during this event. For each decision, provide: (a) the decision, (b) the data you used to make it, (c) the alternatives you considered, (d) the reasoning for your choice, and (e) the verification step that will confirm the decision was correct. Decisions must span all phases of the event: immediate response, stabilization, restoration, and post-incident recovery.
*Example of an acceptable documented decision: "Decision 7: Prioritize Denver EDFA replacement over waiting for field crew confirmation on CHI-IND. Data: Spare EDFA is on-site at Denver (confirmed by site inventory). CHI-IND fiber repair requires minimum 6 hours (field crew arrival 75–90 min + repair 4 hours + testing 1 hour). Denver EDFA replacement can restore 22 channels within 45 minutes with no field crew required (tech at site can execute MOP). Alternatives: (a) Focus all attention on CHI-IND fiber — delays Denver circuit recovery by 6+ hours unnecessarily. (b) Do nothing at Denver until NMS is back — risks FEC-UCW escalation on the 16 borderline channels during NMS recovery (estimated 1–2 hours). Reasoning: Highest service count restored quickest with available resources. Verification: After EDFA replacement, pull FEC-UCW on all 22 affected channels — must show zero UCW events within 15 minutes of EDFA replacement completion."*

| 📋 Certification Note
All six deliverables must be submitted to the program instructor within 72 hours of receiving the capstone scenario. Evaluation is conducted by a panel of at least two Level 3-certified architects. Partial credit is awarded by deliverable. A minimum score of 80% across all deliverables is required for Level 3 certification. Candidates who do not pass may re-attempt once after a minimum 60-day remediation period. |
|---|

### Chapter 14 — Capstone Guidance Summary
The NexusCarrier crisis scenario tests everything: alarm triage under pressure, multi-failure prioritization, cross-vendor CLI competency, SLA accountability, communication under uncertainty, root-cause rigor, and systemic improvement thinking. The architect who succeeds here has demonstrated that they can own a production network under the worst conditions it will ever face. That is the standard the industry requires. That is the standard this program certifies.

3.title: Appendix A — Master Alarm Reference Table
3.content_md:
# Appendix A — Master Alarm Reference Table

Complete reference for optical and OTN alarms encountered in production DWDM networks. Derived from ITU-T G.798, G.709, and G.7710.

| Alarm Code | Full Name | Layer | Severity | Root Cause or Symptom | Probable Causes | First Response Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| LOS | Loss of Signal | Physical / OTUk | Critical | Either | Fiber cut, TX failure, connector pull, amplifier failure | Check OSC; check received power; determine scope (single channel vs. all channels) |
| LOF | Loss of Frame | OTUk | Critical | Symptom | Signal present but unreadable; severe BER; wrong FEC mode | Check pre-FEC BER; verify FEC configuration matches far end |
| OOF | Out of Frame | OTUk | Major | Symptom | Transient framing error; impulsive noise event; CD exceeding DSP range | Monitor for persistence; if >3 ms escalates to LOF; check CD estimate |
| LOM | Loss of Multiframe | ODUk | Major | Symptom | ODUk multiframe alignment lost; upstream framing issue | Check upstream OTUk alarms; verify tributary mapping configuration |
| AIS | Alarm Indication Signal | ODUk | Critical | Symptom always | Upstream LOS or LOF caused AIS insertion | Trace to upstream LOS/LOF — do not act on AIS directly |
| RDI | Remote Defect Indication | OTUk / ODUk | Major | Symptom | Far end is receiving a defective signal; confirms bidirectional impact | Confirms near-to-far direction has problem; focus troubleshooting on TX at near end |
| BDI | Backward Defect Indication | ODUk TCM | Major | Symptom | TCM-layer indication of received defect; returned in reverse direction | Identify TCM monitoring segment; locate defect source in forward direction |
| DEG | Signal Degrade | OTUk / ODUk | Major | Either | BER between DEG threshold and SF threshold; OSNR degradation; connector issue | Pull FEC-CW trend; check received power; compare adjacent channels |
| OCI | Open Connection Indication | ODUk | Critical | Root cause | Provisioning error; cross-connect to unconnected port; ODU switch failure | Verify cross-connect configuration in NMS; check ODU switch state |
| LOCK | Locked Signal | ODUk | Critical | Root cause (deliberate) | Maintenance lock-out applied; should correspond to active MOP | Verify against active MOP; if no MOP, clear immediately and investigate who applied |
| TTI-MIS | Trail Trace Identifier Mismatch | OTUk / ODUk | Major | Root cause | Far-end sending wrong TTI; provisioning error; incorrect cross-connect | Read received TTI vs. expected TTI; trace to misprovisioned NE |
| APS-FAIL | APS Protocol Failure | OTUk APS | Major | Root cause | K1/K2 byte mismatch; OSC failure (no APS signaling path); NE processing error | Read K1/K2 values at both ends; verify OSC connectivity; check APS configuration |
| PUMP-FAIL | Pump Laser Failure | EDFA | Critical | Root cause | Pump laser end-of-life; TEC failure; pump driver circuit failure | Activate N+1 spare if available; schedule emergency EDFA replacement |
| TEMP-HIGH | High Temperature Alarm | NE / Module | Major | Root cause | HVAC failure; fan failure; airflow blockage; ambient temperature rise | Immediate: verify HVAC status; clear airflow; if NE temperature critical, risk assess traffic impact of shutdown vs. thermal damage |
| WSS-FAIL | Wavelength Selective Switch Failure | ROADM | Critical | Root cause | WSS internal laser failure; MEMS actuator failure; WSS driver board failure | Identify all channels through failed WSS; initiate hot-swap replacement MOP |
| OSC-LOS | Optical Supervisory Channel Loss | OSC | Major | Root cause | Fiber span failure (most common); OSC filter failure; OSC transceiver failure | Primary span-level diagnostic — confirms which span has fiber issue |
| FEC-UCW | FEC Uncorrected Words | OTUk | Critical | Root cause | Pre-FEC BER exceeds FEC correction capability; OSNR below threshold; severe impairment | Service is experiencing post-FEC errors; immediate restoration action required |
| DSP-UNLOCK | DSP Lock Lost | Coherent modem | Critical | Root cause | OSNR below DSP acquisition threshold; CD beyond DSP range; carrier frequency excursion; firmware bug | Check OSNR and CD estimate at last lock; check carrier frequency offset history |

3.title: Appendix B — Production Troubleshooting Cheatsheet
3.content_md:
# Appendix B — Production Troubleshooting Cheatsheet

*One-page rapid reference for use during active fault events.*

| STEP 1: SCOPE THE FAULT | STEP 1: SCOPE THE FAULT |
|---|---|
| All channels on one span affected | → Check OSC status. Fiber cut or amplifier failure. Trace from first OSC-LOS. |
| All add/drop channels at one node affected | → WSS failure or ROADM node issue. Express channels unaffected. |
| Single channel only affected | → Transponder, per-channel WSS port, or per-channel connector. |
| One direction only affected (asymmetric) | → Far-end TX, per-direction amplifier chain, or per-direction span loss. |
| STEP 2: CHECK FIRST DATA POINTS | STEP 2: CHECK FIRST DATA POINTS |
| FEC-UCW = 0? | Service intact. Have time for systematic diagnosis. Do not rush. |
| FEC-UCW > 0? | Service impacted NOW. Activate protection immediately, then diagnose. |
| OPR low (>2 dBm below baseline)? | Loss event between far-end TX and this RX. Check each span via half-split. |
| OPR normal, Q-factor low? | OSNR / noise problem. Check amplifier noise figure. Check adjacent channel count. |
| STEP 3: LAYER ISOLATION QUICK GUIDE | STEP 3: LAYER ISOLATION QUICK GUIDE |
| Physical fiber suspect? | OTDR from both ends. Check span loss vs. baseline. Look for new events. |
| Amplifier suspect? | show amp status → pump current, gain, noise figure vs. baseline. |
| Transponder suspect? | show port optical → OPT, OSNR, Q-factor, CD, BER, DSP state. |
| OTN layer suspect? | show pm current → BBE, ES, SES, UAS, FEC-CW, FEC-UCW. Check TTI. |
| STEP 4: HALF-SPLIT RULE (LONG ROUTES) | STEP 4: HALF-SPLIT RULE (LONG ROUTES) |
| Measure signal quality at midpoint of route. Fault in first half = quality already bad at midpoint. Fault in second half = quality good at midpoint. Repeat for identified half. Localizes in O(log n) steps. | Measure signal quality at midpoint of route. Fault in first half = quality already bad at midpoint. Fault in second half = quality good at midpoint. Repeat for identified half. Localizes in O(log n) steps. |
| ALWAYS DOCUMENT AS YOU GO | ALWAYS DOCUMENT AS YOU GO |
| Time-stamp every action and result. If you have to hand off the fault, the next engineer needs your log — not your memory. | Time-stamp every action and result. If you have to hand off the fault, the next engineer needs your log — not your memory. |
