3.title: Appendix E — PM Threshold Recommendation Table by Service Type
3.content_md:
# Appendix E — PM Threshold Recommendation Table by Service Type

Recommended TCA threshold settings for production DWDM networks. These are starting points — adjust based on your specific system baseline using the μ + 3σ methodology described in Chapter 2. All OTN PM thresholds are per 15-minute interval unless noted.

| PM Parameter | Financial Exchange (Tier 1 SLA) | Enterprise Ethernet (Tier 2 SLA) | Broadband Backbone (Tier 3 SLA) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| FEC-CW (15-min Warning) | 5× baseline average | 10× baseline average | 20× baseline average | Tighter threshold for higher-priority services |
| FEC-CW (15-min Critical) | 50× baseline average | 100× baseline average | 200× baseline average | Critical = immediate escalation required |
| FEC-UCW (any bin) | 1 word (any non-zero) | 1 word (any non-zero) | 1 word (any non-zero) | Always critical regardless of service tier |
| ES (Errored Seconds, 15-min Warning) | 1 ES per 15 min | 5 ES per 15 min | 10 ES per 15 min | G.828 objective: ES ratio ≤ 0.4% |
| SES (Severely Errored Seconds, 15-min Warning) | 1 SES (any non-zero) | 1 SES (any non-zero) | 2 SES per 15 min | SES = near-failure; treat as critical for Tier 1/2 |
| UAS (Unavailable Seconds, any bin) | 1 UAS (any non-zero) | 1 UAS (any non-zero) | 1 UAS (any non-zero) | Always critical — direct SLA availability impact |
| OSNR TCA (Warning, dB below baseline) | –1.0 dB | –1.5 dB | –2.0 dB | Based on μ–1σ / μ–1.5σ / μ–2σ of baseline |
| OSNR TCA (Critical) | Absolute minimum +2 dB headroom | Absolute minimum +1.5 dB headroom | Absolute minimum +1 dB headroom | Must never allow OSNR to reach FEC threshold without alert |
| OPR TCA (Warning) | –1.0 dBm vs. baseline | –1.5 dBm vs. baseline | –2.0 dBm vs. baseline | Set relative to established baseline, not absolute level |
| EDFA Pump Current Warning | 80% of rated max | 80% of rated max | 85% of rated max | Apply consistently across all service tiers — pump failure affects all channels |
| EDFA Pump Current Critical | 88% of rated max | 90% of rated max | 92% of rated max | At Critical: initiate emergency replacement procurement |
| Q-Factor Warning (100G DP-QPSK) | 9.5 dB (vs. 8.5 dB min) | 9.0 dB | 8.8 dB | 1 dB above minimum for Tier 1; 0.5 dB for Tier 2/3 |
| PSC (Protection Switch Count, 24-hr Warning) | 3 switches/24 hr | 5 switches/24 hr | 5 switches/24 hr | High PSC indicates path instability — must investigate |

3.title: Appendix F — Glossary of Level 3 Operations Terms
3.content_md:
# Appendix F — Glossary of Level 3 Operations Terms

Seventy-plus terms essential for Level 3 production operations. Terms in **bold** are referenced directly in ITU-T G.709, G.798, G.872, or G.873.1.

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| AIS (Alarm Indication Signal) | An all-ones signal inserted downstream by a network element when it detects an upstream failure; always a symptom alarm, never root cause per ITU-T G.798. |
| ALS (Automatic Laser Shutdown) | Safety interlock that automatically disables a laser when fiber continuity is broken; mandatory for Class 3B/4 laser systems per IEC 60825-2. |
| APC (Automatic Power Control) | EDFA control loop that maintains constant gain or output power by adjusting pump drive current; masks pump degradation from output-power-based monitoring. |
| APS (Automatic Protection Switching) | Protocol for automatic switchover from working to protect path; uses K1/K2 bytes in OTUk overhead per ITU-T G.873.1; target switching time ≤50 ms. |
| As-Built Record | Documentation of the actual current configuration of a network element or system, updated after every change; the operational ground truth. |
| BBE (Background Block Errors) | OTN PM counter: count of errored blocks not occurring during Severely Errored Seconds; baseline quality indicator per ITU-T G.826. |
| BDI (Backward Defect Indication) | Signal sent in the reverse direction by an NE confirming it is receiving a defective signal at the TCM layer; always a symptom alarm. |
| BER (Bit Error Rate) | Ratio of errored bits to total transmitted bits; measured pre-FEC (raw) and post-FEC (after error correction); post-FEC BER = 0 is the operational target. |
| C-Band | Conventional band: 1530–1565 nm (approximately 192.1–196.1 THz); the primary transmission band for DWDM networks; supports up to 96 channels at 50 GHz spacing. |
| CD (Chromatic Dispersion) | Wavelength-dependent group velocity variation in optical fiber; measured in ps/nm; causes pulse broadening; compensated by coherent DSP in modern systems. |
| CSPF (Constrained Shortest Path First) | Path computation algorithm used by GMPLS controllers to calculate optimal routes satisfying bandwidth, latency, and diversity constraints. |
| DCM (Dispersion Compensation Module) | Passive optical component (typically a dispersion-compensating fiber spool) used to compensate accumulated chromatic dispersion; required in non-coherent DWDM systems; generally unnecessary with modern coherent DSP. |
| DGD (Differential Group Delay) | Instantaneous delay difference between the two orthogonal polarization modes of a signal; related to PMD; measured in picoseconds; monitored via coherent DSP estimate. |
| DP-16QAM | Dual-Polarization 16-level Quadrature Amplitude Modulation; 4 bits/symbol per polarization, 8 bits/symbol total; used for 200G and 400G coherent systems; requires higher OSNR than DP-QPSK. |
| DP-QPSK | Dual-Polarization Quadrature Phase Shift Keying; 2 bits/symbol per polarization; the workhorse modulation format for 100G coherent transmission; tolerant of OSNR as low as ~14–16 dB. |
| DRB (Double Rayleigh Backscattering) | A Raman amplification noise mechanism where backscattered signal photons re-scatter forward, creating coherent crosstalk noise; limits maximum Raman ON/OFF gain to ~15–20 dB. |
| DSP (Digital Signal Processor) | The ASIC core of a coherent transceiver; implements carrier recovery, polarization demultiplexing, chromatic dispersion equalization, FEC decode, and diagnostic parameter extraction. |
| DWDM (Dense Wavelength Division Multiplexing) | Optical transmission technology combining multiple wavelengths onto a single fiber; channel spacing 50 GHz or 100 GHz for ITU-T G.694.1 standard grids; flex-grid allows variable spacing. |
| EDFA (Erbium-Doped Fiber Amplifier) | The primary optical amplifier for C-band DWDM; amplifies all wavelengths simultaneously via stimulated emission in erbium-doped fiber; pumped by 980 nm or 1480 nm semiconductor lasers. |
| ES (Errored Seconds) | OTN PM counter: count of one-second intervals containing at least one block error or defect; direct SLA metric per ITU-T G.826. |
| FEC (Forward Error Correction) | Error correction coding applied to the OTN payload; HD-FEC (Hard-Decision) per G.709 provides ~6 dB coding gain; SD-FEC (Soft-Decision) provides ~11–12 dB gain with 20% overhead. |
| Flex-Grid | Variable channel spacing for DWDM systems per ITU-T G.694.1 (2012 amendment); allows channel widths in multiples of 12.5 GHz; enables higher-order modulation formats and spectral efficiency optimization. |
| Forced Switch | Operator-initiated protection switch at highest priority; overrides automatic requests; does not revert automatically if working path fails; highest-risk protection operation. |
| Fragmentation Index (FI) | Measure of spectral fragmentation in flex-grid systems: FI = 1 – (largest contiguous available spectrum block / total available spectrum); FI >0.4 triggers defragmentation planning. |
| G.694.1 | ITU-T standard defining the DWDM frequency grid; specifies channel frequencies for fixed-grid (50 GHz, 100 GHz spacing) and flex-grid (12.5 GHz granularity) operation. |
| G.709 | ITU-T standard for OTN (Optical Transport Network) interfaces; defines OTUk/ODUk/OPUk frame structure, FEC, overhead bytes, and PM parameter definitions. |
| G.798 | ITU-T standard for OTN equipment functional specifications; defines alarm conditions, defect detection criteria, and PM parameter behaviors at each OTN layer termination point. |
| G.826 | ITU-T standard defining error performance parameters and objectives for international constant bit rate digital paths; defines BBE, ES, SES, and UAS performance metrics. |
| G.872 | ITU-T standard for optical transport network architecture; defines the layered OTN model (OTS, OMS, OCh, OTUk, ODUk, OPUk) and network topology concepts. |
| G.873.1 | ITU-T standard for OTN 1+1 linear protection; defines the APS protocol using K1/K2 bytes, switching criteria, and the ≤50 ms switching time objective. |
| GMPLS (Generalized MPLS) | Extension of MPLS control plane to manage optical switching resources; uses OSPF-TE for topology distribution and RSVP-TE for path setup; enables dynamic optical path provisioning and restoration. |
| HD-FEC (Hard-Decision FEC) | The standard FEC specified in ITU-T G.709; provides approximately 6.2 dB coding gain at 7% overhead; pre-FEC BER threshold approximately 3.8×10⁻³. |
| ILA (In-Line Amplifier) | An EDFA amplifier site placed along a fiber route between terminal nodes to compensate for fiber span loss; typically placed every 60–100 km. |
| KMS (Key Management Server) | Infrastructure component managing encryption key generation, distribution, storage, and rotation for Layer 1 OPE encryption; must be independent of the optical NMS. |
| L-Band | Long band: 1565–1625 nm (approximately 184.5–191.5 THz); the spectral expansion band for DWDM networks when C-band capacity is exhausted; requires separate L-band EDFA amplifiers. |
| Lockout of Protection | Highest-priority protection operation that prevents any switching (including automatic SF-triggered switching); removes all protection from the working path; must have automatic expiration timer. |
| LOF (Loss of Frame) | OTN alarm: OTUk framing cannot be recovered from the received signal; typically a symptom of LOS or severe BER degradation. |
| LOM (Loss of Multiframe) | OTN alarm: ODUk multiframe alignment cannot be maintained; symptom of upstream framing failure or severe signal quality degradation. |
| LOS (Loss of Signal) | Optical alarm: received optical power below the detector threshold (typically –30 dBm); can be root cause (fiber cut, TX failure) or symptom of upstream failure. |
| Manual Switch | Operator-initiated protection switch at lower priority than SF/SD; can be overridden by automatic switching; reverts when the manual switch command is cleared. |
| MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) | The switching mechanism used in most WSS modules; microscale mirrors that redirect individual wavelengths; failure of MEMS internal laser or actuator causes WSS failure affecting all switched wavelengths. |
| Micro-Bending | Distributed fiber loss mechanism caused by lateral pressure deforming the fiber core; produces no discrete OTDR event (loss is distributed); typical cause: cable over-tension during installation, duct settlement, ice/snow loading on aerial cable. |
| MOP (Method of Procedure) | Written, approved, step-by-step procedure for a network change; includes purpose, risk assessment, pre-conditions, numbered execution steps with verification, rollback procedure, and acceptance criteria. |
| MTTR (Mean Time to Restore) | Average time from service failure detection to service restoration; a key SLA metric; includes fault detection, isolation, field dispatch, repair, and verification time. |
| NERC CIP | North American Electric Reliability Corporation Critical Infrastructure Protection standards; regulatory requirements for cybersecurity and physical security of bulk electric system cyber systems, including DWDM networks carrying grid protection relay traffic. |
| NETCONF | Network Configuration Protocol (RFC 6241); XML-based protocol for NE configuration management using YANG data models; operates over SSH on port 830. |
| NMS (Network Management System) | Software platform providing fault, configuration, accounting, performance, and security (FCAPS) management for DWDM network elements; receives SNMP traps and/or NETCONF notifications. |
| NOC (Network Operations Center) | Centralized facility and team responsible for 24/7 monitoring and first-response for production network events; interfaces with Tier 2 optical specialists and architect-level escalation. |
| Noise Figure | EDFA parameter: ratio of input SNR to output SNR in dB; typical range 5–7 dB for standard EDFAs; elevated noise figure increases ASE noise accumulation and reduces OSNR on long routes. |
| OCh (Optical Channel) | OTN network layer carrying a single wavelength; the OCh layer carries the OTUk signal and includes the optical channel overhead for wavelength-level monitoring. |
| OCI (Open Connection Indication) | OTN alarm: the ODUk connection at an NE is to an unconnected port; indicates provisioning error or cross-connect failure; always a root-cause alarm. |
| ODUk (Optical Data Unit) | OTN layer providing end-to-end path monitoring and tandem connection monitoring; carries PM bytes (BIP-8, BIAE) and overhead (TTI, GCC) per ITU-T G.709. |
| OMS (Optical Multiplex Section) | OTN network layer covering a fiber span between optical amplifiers; the OMS layer is monitored by the EDFA supervisory channel and OMS overhead. |
| OOF (Out of Frame) | OTN transient alarm: OTUk frame alignment momentarily lost; if persistent >3 ms, escalates to LOF; typically symptomatic of impulsive noise events or intermittent signal quality issues. |
| OPE (OTN Payload Encryption) | Layer 1 encryption standard encrypting the OPUk payload at line rate using AES-256; protects against physical fiber interception; does not encrypt OTN overhead fields. |
| OPR (Optical Power Received) | Total optical power measured at a receiver input port; measured in dBm; primary physical-layer health indicator; declining OPR indicates span loss increase, connector degradation, or TX power regression. |
| OPT (Optical Power Transmitted) | Total optical power at a transmitter output port; measured in dBm; declining OPT indicates TX laser degradation or EDFA pump failure. |
| OSA (Optical Spectrum Analyzer) | Test instrument measuring optical power as a function of wavelength; primary tool for OSNR measurement, power spectral profile verification, and amplifier tilt characterization. |
| OSC (Optical Supervisory Channel) | Out-of-band management wavelength (typically 1510 nm or 1625 nm) carrying NE management traffic between adjacent nodes; OSC LOS is the primary indicator of span-level failure. |
| OSNR (Optical Signal-to-Noise Ratio) | Ratio of signal optical power to ASE noise power in 0.1 nm reference bandwidth; measured in dB; the fundamental DWDM system health metric; minimum value at receiver determines maximum achievable BER. |
| OTDR (Optical Time Domain Reflectometer) | Test instrument launching optical pulses into fiber and measuring backscattered light vs. time; used for fiber fault location, splice loss measurement, and span characterization. |
| OTN (Optical Transport Network) | The digital wrapper standard for DWDM signals per ITU-T G.709; provides structured frame, overhead, FEC, PM, and multiplexing capabilities; defines OTUk, ODUk, OPUk container hierarchy. |
| OTS (Optical Transport Section) | OTN network layer covering the physical fiber between two adjacent NEs (amplifiers or terminal nodes); provides physical-layer monitoring. |
| OTUk (Optical Transport Unit) | OTN section layer termination point; carries FEC and section-level overhead (BIP-8, BDI, IAE); k=1 (2.5G), k=2 (10G), k=3 (40G), k=4 (100G), k=5 (400G+) per G.709. |
| PDL (Polarization-Dependent Loss) | Variation in optical insertion loss as a function of polarization state; measured in dB; excessive PDL degrades OSNR and Q-factor; typically caused by degraded passive components. |
| PM (Performance Monitoring) | Systematic collection and analysis of operational parameters at the optical and OTN layers; collected in 15-minute and 24-hour bins; the primary tool for proactive network health management. |
| PMD (Polarization Mode Dispersion) | Statistical fiber impairment causing differential propagation speed between polarization states; characterized by DGD; relevant for systems without full coherent DSP polarization tracking or very long routes with older fiber. |
| PSC (Protection Switch Count) | OTN PM counter: number of protection switching events in the measurement period; PSC >5/hour indicates network instability requiring investigation. |
| PSD (Protection Switch Duration) | OTN PM counter: total elapsed time traffic spent on the protect path; long PSD indicates working path restoration is delayed or incomplete. |
| Q-Factor | Signal quality metric from coherent DSP; relates to pre-FEC BER via Q = 20×log₁₀(√2 × erfc⁻¹(2×BER)); most comprehensive single-parameter health indicator; accounts for all linear and some nonlinear impairments. |
| RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) | Access control model assigning permissions based on organizational role; required for NMS and CLI access management; minimum four tiers: Read-Only, Operations, Engineer, Admin. |
| RDI (Remote Defect Indication) | OTN signal inserted in the return direction overhead when the far-end NE detects a defect; confirms bidirectional impact; identifies that root cause is in the near-to-far direction. |
| RESTCONF | REST-based configuration protocol (RFC 8040); HTTP/HTTPS interface for NE configuration using YANG data models; operates on port 443; used by SDN controllers for NE configuration. |
| ROADM (Reconfigurable Optical Add-Drop Multiplexer) | Network element capable of adding, dropping, and routing individual wavelengths at network nodes without optical-to-electrical conversion; core component of agile optical networks; uses WSS for wavelength switching. |
| SD-FEC (Soft-Decision FEC) | Advanced FEC using analog signal quality values (soft bits) rather than binary decisions; provides ~11–12 dB coding gain at 20% overhead; pre-FEC BER threshold ~1.2×10⁻²; standard for all modern coherent systems. |
| SES (Severely Errored Seconds) | OTN PM counter: count of one-second intervals with ≥30% errored blocks or a defect condition; near-failure indicator; 10 consecutive SES triggers transition to Unavailable state per G.826. |
| SLA (Service Level Agreement) | Contractual commitment to service availability, performance, and restoration times; optical layer parameters (OSNR, BER, UAS) must be mapped to SLA metrics for accountability. |
| SNMP (Simple Network Management Protocol) | Protocol for NMS-to-NE communication; v3 required for security (authentication + encryption); traps carry alarm notifications from NE to NMS; polling collects PM data. |
| SRS (Stimulated Raman Scattering) | Nonlinear optical effect where short-wavelength (C-band) photons transfer energy to long-wavelength (L-band) photons; produces power tilt in wideband CL systems; must be modeled for power control in C+L systems. |
| TCA (Threshold Crossing Alert) | Automated alarm generated when a PM parameter crosses a configured threshold within a measurement bin; must be engineered from baseline data to minimize false positives while capturing genuine degradation. |
| TCM (Tandem Connection Monitoring) | OTN sublayer (up to 6 levels, TCM1–TCM6) enabling performance monitoring of sub-path segments; allows intermediate operators to monitor path performance without full end-to-end access. |
| TTI (Trail Trace Identifier) | OTN overhead field carrying a 64-byte ASCII string identifying the signal source; used to detect misconnections; TTI mismatch alarm (TTI-MIS) indicates cross-connect or provisioning error. |
| UAS (Unavailable Seconds) | OTN PM counter: seconds counted during an unavailable period (entered after 10 consecutive SES, exited after 10 consecutive non-SES); direct SLA availability metric with contractual/legal significance. |
| VOA (Variable Optical Attenuator) | Programmable optical attenuator used to adjust power levels within amplifier modules and between system components; used for gain tilt correction and per-channel power equalization. |
| WSS (Wavelength Selective Switch) | Core ROADM component using MEMS or liquid crystal technology to route individual wavelengths to specific output ports; failure causes simultaneous loss of all wavelengths through the failed WSS; field-replaceable module. |
| ZR+ | OpenROADM/OIF standard for pluggable coherent optics in QSFP-DD form factor; supports 400G DP-16QAM; designed for metro and regional distances; managed via the host router CLI; requires careful thermal management (up to 20W dissipation). |

3.title: Appendix G — Recommended Tool Set for DWDM Operations
3.content_md:
# Appendix G — Recommended Tool Set for DWDM Operations

Field and lab instruments required for professional production DWDM operations. Every Level 3 architect should ensure these tools are available in their operations environment and that they are proficient in their use.

| Tool | Primary Use Case | Key Specifications Required | Recommended Vendors/Models (examples) |
|---|---|---|---|
| OTDR (Optical Time Domain Reflectometer) | Fiber fault location; splice loss measurement; span characterization; pre-commissioning baseline | C-band range (1550 nm); dynamic range ≥40 dB; distance resolution <1 m; dead zone <5 m; two-wavelength capability (1310+1550 nm) | JDSU/Viavi MTS-6000; EXFO FTB-700; Anritsu MT9083 |
| OSA (Optical Spectrum Analyzer) | OSNR measurement; power spectral profile; amplifier tilt characterization; channel power verification; CD measurement | Wavelength range 1520–1620 nm (C+L coverage); resolution ≤0.02 nm; dynamic range ≥60 dB; polarization-scrambled OSNR measurement capability for coherent systems | JDSU/Viavi MTS-8000 OSA; EXFO FTB-5240; Yokogawa AQ6370D |
| Optical Power Meter | Span loss verification; connector loss measurement; insertion loss budget verification | Wavelength range 1260–1650 nm; accuracy ±0.2 dB; dynamic range –80 to +10 dBm; calibrated to NIST traceable standards | JDSU OLP-87; EXFO PPM-352C; Fluke Networks PM100 |
| Tunable Laser Source (TLS) | Per-channel power measurement; connector insertion loss at specific wavelengths; WDM component characterization | C-band tuning range; output power ≥0 dBm; wavelength accuracy ±0.001 nm; low linewidth (<1 MHz) | Keysight 81600B; EXFO IQS-2600B; Santec TSL-550 |
| Fiber Inspection Probe (Video Microscope) | Connector end-face inspection per IEC 61300-3-35; contamination identification; pre-clean/post-clean verification | 400× magnification minimum; IEC 61300-3-35 auto-grading capability (Grade A/B/C/D); compatible with LC, SC, MPO connector types | JDSU/Viavi P5000i; EXFO FIP-400B; Fluke Networks FI-7000 |
| Connector Cleaning Kit | Cleaning contaminated fiber connectors per IPC/WHMA-A-620; maintenance of all patch panel connectors | Dry-then-wet cleaning method; IPA-free dry cleaners for initial pass; lint-free wipes; push-type cleaners for adapter cleaning; field kit for ILA sites | Cletop-S; Sticklers Fiber Optic Splice & Connector Cleaner; Fujikura CTB-10 |
| OTN Protocol Analyzer / BER Tester | End-to-end OTN framing verification; BER measurement at OTUk layer; TTI verification; protection switch timing measurement | OTU1–OTU4 (and OTU5 for 400G); OTN overhead insertion/analysis; bit error insertion; loopback capability; protection switch timer (<1 ms resolution) | Spirent Xcellon-Ultra; IXIA/Keysight OTN test modules; EXFO FTB-88000NGE |
| CD / PMD Measurement System | Chromatic dispersion measurement for fiber qualification; PMD measurement for 100G+ deployment on legacy fiber | CD range: ±50,000 ps/nm; PMD method: Jones Matrix Eigenanalysis or interferometric; single-end measurement capability | JDSU/Viavi T-BERD 8000 with CD/PMD modules; EXFO FTB-5800; Anritsu MP9800A |
| Visual Fault Locator (VFL) | Quick identification of fiber breaks, tight bends, and misconnections in short-distance applications; not a substitute for OTDR at DWDM wavelengths | Output wavelength: 650 nm (visible red); range: ≥5 km; output power ≥0 dBm; CW and blink modes | EXFO FVL-SC; Fluke Networks VisiFault; JDSU FHS-300 |
| Laptop with SSH/CLI access and NMS VPN client | Remote CLI access to all NEs; NMS access; script execution for multi-node PM audits | SSH client (OpenSSH or equivalent); NETCONF client (optional); Python environment for scripting; VPN client for secure management access; offline CLI reference documentation | Any enterprise-grade laptop; PuTTY or native SSH; Cisco AnyConnect or equivalent VPN |
| Optical Loopback Plugs | NE self-test and loopback commissioning; transceiver receive sensitivity testing | LC/APC loopbacks with appropriate attenuation (5 dB, 10 dB, 15 dB); covers all transceiver types in use on your network | Maintain site stock by connector type and attenuation value |

| 📋 Tool Calibration Discipline
All optical measurement instruments must be calibrated on a defined schedule (typically annually for reference-grade instruments; every 2 years for field-grade instruments) against NIST-traceable standards. Uncalibrated instruments produce measurements that cannot be relied upon for SLA defense, regulatory compliance, or legal proceedings. Maintain calibration certificates for all instruments and verify calibration currency before any measurement used for acceptance testing or fault evidence. |
|---|

3.title: Program Completion and Certification
3.content_md:
# Program Completion and Certification

## Level 3 Certification Requirements
To earn the **DWDM Architect Program Level 3 Certification**, candidates must satisfy all of the following requirements:
- Prerequisites verified: Valid Level 1 (Foundations) and Level 2 (Architecture) certifications on file with the program administrator
- Book completion: All chapter review question sets submitted and reviewed by a certified instructor
- Chapter practical exercises: Minimum 8 of 13 chapter practical exercises completed with satisfactory scores (≥75% per exercise)
- Capstone scenario: All 6 capstone deliverables submitted and evaluated at ≥80% aggregate score by a two-architect evaluation panel
- Oral defense (optional for distinction): Candidates seeking Distinction designation must defend their capstone deliverables in a 60-minute oral examination with the evaluation panel
## Continuing Professional Development
DWDM technology and operational practice evolve continuously. Level 3 certified architects are expected to maintain currency through:
- Annual review of applicable ITU-T standard updates (G.709, G.694.1, G.872, G.698.x series)
- Vendor release notes review for all platforms in their operational environment
- Participation in post-incident reviews that produce institutional knowledge improvements
- Mentoring of Level 1 and Level 2 candidates as a structured knowledge transfer discipline
Recertification is recommended every three years to account for technology evolution, particularly in coherent DSP capabilities, SD-FEC advances, and SDN/automation integration that have materially changed operational best practices over each three-year period since the introduction of coherent DWDM.
## A Final Word from the Program
The DWDM Architect Program exists because the optical networking industry has a shortage of engineers who can do all of it: design, operate, troubleshoot, and improve production networks with the same depth of understanding. The three-level curriculum was designed to produce exactly that engineer — someone who can look at an OSNR margin calculation at the design stage and immediately understand how it will manifest as a FEC-CW count in operation, and how that FEC-CW count will look in a PM trending chart six months later when something begins to fail.
The engineers who complete this program carry a responsibility: to use the knowledge actively, to document what they learn in production, to mentor those behind them, and to contribute to the institutional knowledge that makes networks more reliable for the customers and communities that depend on them.
You have done the work. Own the network.

**DWDM Architect Program — Level 3: Production & Troubleshooting Book**
Version 1.0 | 2026 Edition
This document is provided for educational use within the DWDM Architect Program.
All CLI examples, case study data, and network parameters are representative of field practice and are provided for instructional purposes.
ITU-T standard references: G.709 (2020), G.872 (2017), G.798 (2017), G.873.1 (2017), G.826 (2002), G.694.1 (2020)
*End of Document*

3.title: How to Use This Workbook
3.content_md:
# How to Use This Workbook

| ⚑ Operational Mandate
Every exercise in this workbook is conducted as a live production event. There is no practice mode. Treat every alarm, every CLI output, and every PM dataset as if customer SLAs are counting down right now. That mindset is what separates a Level 3 architect from everyone else. |
|---|

### Production Simulation Philosophy
This workbook does not present textbook problems. It presents operational crises — the kind that arrive at 2:17 AM with 112 alarms on screen, a customer on the phone, and three simultaneous faults competing for your attention. Each drill is designed to stress your ability to triage under pressure, sequence actions correctly, communicate clearly, and document accurately. Speed matters. Accuracy matters more. Documentation matters most, because the next engineer inherits your incident log.
You will be graded not only on whether you found the right answer, but on whether you found it within the architect benchmark time, documented it correctly, and selected the right escalation path. Partial credit is not awarded for identifying a symptom when you missed the root cause.
### Lab Environment for Level 3
- Preferred: Real hardware — Ciena 6500, Nokia 1830 PSS, Cisco NCS 1004 with live optical connections
- Simulation option: Vendor NMS sandboxes (Ciena OneControl sandbox, Nokia NetAct lab, Cisco Crosswork emulation)
- GNS3 optical emulation: Use the Level 3 GNS3 topology package (6-node DWDM ring, 4 vendors, pre-loaded fault injection scripts)
- Minimum requirement: Access to one vendor's NMS and CLI environment; all PM data and CLI outputs are reproduced verbatim in this workbook for offline analysis
- OSA access: Highly recommended for Modules 4 and 5; at minimum, use OSA trace screenshots provided in the companion digital archive
### Timing Discipline
Every drill carries an **Architect Benchmark** — the target resolution time at which a Level 3 engineer is expected to reach the correct root cause, recommend action, and document the finding. Benchmarks are not aspirational; they are the operational standard. Log your actual time for every exercise. If you consistently exceed benchmarks by more than 50%, revisit the corresponding chapter in the Level 3 Production & Troubleshooting Book before proceeding.
### Portfolio and Certification Note
Completion of this workbook — with all deliverables submitted and all benchmarks documented — constitutes the operational readiness component of the DWDM Architect Program Level 3 Certification. Your completed workbook, including all RCA reports, MOP documents, and capstone simulation logs, becomes your operational portfolio. Retain every page. The certification panel will reference it during Part C of the Final Capstone exam.

| Portfolio Requirement
All deliverables marked [DELIVERABLE] throughout this workbook must be retained in your personal operational portfolio. The Level 3 Architect Certification panel reviews a minimum of 8 deliverables from your portfolio during the Architecture Defense (Part C of the Final Capstone). |
|---|

## Table of Contents

| Module 6 | Vertical Capstone Scenarios |

| Final Capstone | Level 3 Architect Certification Exam |
| Appendices | A through F |

| MODULE 1 — PM Monitoring and Trending Drills |
|---|

Performance Monitoring is not passive data collection. It is the early warning system for every fault that will eventually become an alarm. A Level 3 architect reads PM data the way an aircraft engineer reads engine telemetry — continuously, comparatively, and with an instinct for what is about to go wrong. These drills train that instinct.

| Lab 1.1: PM Data Interpretation Drill
Type: Analysis Drill     Target Time: 25 minutes     Deliverable: Anomaly Classification Report |
|---|

**SCENARIO BRIEF**
You are the on-call optical architect for a carrier operating an 8-channel DWDM system on a 640 km route. The NMS has flagged no critical alarms. However, you have been provided with a complete 24-hour PM snapshot (midnight to midnight) and asked to validate system health before the morning operations briefing at 08:00. You have 25 minutes.

#### Given Data — 24-Hour PM Snapshot (End-of-Period Values, 00:00 to 23:59)
**System:** 8-channel C-band DWDM, 640 km, 8 spans × 80 km, 4 in-line amplifiers (ILA). Modulation: 100G QPSK. EFEC pre-FEC BER threshold: 2.4×10⁻³.

| Channel | OPT (dBm) | OPR (dBm) | OSNR (dB) | Q-Factor (dB) | Pre-FEC BER | Post-FEC BER |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Ch 1 | 0.0 | -18.2 | 27.4 | 13.8 | 4.1E-4 | 0 |
| Ch 2 | 0.0 | -18.5 | 27.1 | 13.6 | 4.8E-4 | 0 |
| Ch 3 | 0.0 | -18.3 | 26.8 | 12.9 | 2.1E-3 | 0 |
| Ch 4 | 0.0 | -18.4 | 27.2 | 13.7 | 5.2E-4 | 0 |
| Ch 5 | 0.0 | -18.6 | 21.0 | 10.4 | 1.9E-3 | 0 |
| Ch 6 | 0.0 | -18.1 | 27.0 | 13.5 | 5.5E-4 | 0 |
| Ch 7 | 0.0 | -16.2 | 27.3 | 13.7 | 5.0E-4 | 0 |
| Ch 8 | 0.0 | -18.4 | 27.1 | 13.6 | 4.9E-4 | 0 |

#### Channel 5 — OSNR 24-Hour Trend (Hourly Readings)

| Hour | 00 | 02 | 04 | 06 | 08 | 10 | 12 | 14 | 16 | 18 | 20 | 22 | 23:59 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| OSNR (dB) | 28.0 | 27.8 | 27.5 | 27.1 | 26.6 | 25.9 | 25.0 | 24.1 | 23.2 | 22.4 | 21.9 | 21.3 | 21.0 |

#### Worksheet Steps
- Anomaly Identification: Review all 8 channels across all 6 PM parameters. List every value that is outside normal or that stands out relative to peers. State precisely why each is anomalous.
- Channel Classification: Classify each channel using one of four states: NORMAL | OUTLIER | DEGRADING | CRITICAL. Justify each classification with specific data references.
- Root Cause Hypothesis: For Channel 5, the OSNR has declined 7 dB in 24 hours with no associated span loss increase. What is the most likely physical cause? List three possibilities in order of probability.
- Channel 3 Margin Analysis: The pre-FEC BER threshold is 2.4×10⁻³. Channel 3 is at 2.1×10⁻³. Calculate the margin as a percentage of threshold. Is this safe? Justify your answer.
- Channel 7 Power Outlier: Channel 7 OPR is 2.0 dB higher than the average of all other channels (-18.3 dBm). Identify four possible causes. Which requires immediate action and which requires only a note in the shift log?
- Architect Decision: Of all 8 channels, which one do you escalate immediately to a senior engineer? Which one do you place on 4-hour enhanced monitoring with 15-minute PM collection? Which requires a maintenance dispatch within 72 hours? Which is normal?
- Trend Projection: If Channel 5 OSNR continues declining at the same rate observed between hour 00 and hour 23:59 (approximately 0.29 dB/hour), at what UTC time tomorrow does it cross the minimum acceptable OSNR of 14.0 dB for 100G QPSK?
- Documentation: Write the three-sentence shift-handover note for Channel 5 that you pass to the incoming engineer. Include: current status, trend rate, projected crossing time, and recommended action.

| ▶ Architect Benchmark
Target time: 25 minutes. All 8 channels correctly classified. Root cause hypothesis for Ch 5 includes EDFA pump degradation as primary candidate. Channel 7 outlier identified as likely per-channel power optimization artifact (WSS passband calibration) — not a fault. Escalation decision: Ch 5 escalate immediately; Ch 3 on 4-hour monitoring; Ch 7 log and verify WSS calibration. Trend projection: Ch 5 crosses 14.0 dB in approximately 24 additional hours (≈ 08:00 next day UTC). |
|---|

| Answer Key — Lab 1.1
Channel Classifications:
•  Ch 1, 2, 4, 6, 8: NORMAL — all PM parameters within healthy ranges, post-FEC BER = 0, OSNR > 25 dB
•  Ch 3: DEGRADING — pre-FEC BER 2.1×10⁻³ represents 87.5% of EFEC threshold; only 12.5% margin remains; requires 4-hour enhanced monitoring
•  Ch 5: CRITICAL — OSNR in active monotonic decline (7 dB in 24 h); most likely cause: EDFA pump degradation at ILA 2 or ILA 3 (single pump failure characteristically produces per-channel OSNR decline on wavelengths in the affected span); secondary candidates: connector contamination with progressive IL increase, fiber splice partial failure; requires immediate escalation
•  Ch 7: OUTLIER (non-fault) — OPR 2.0 dB elevated; most likely WSS passband offset calibration difference or per-channel power optimization setting; verify WSS port calibration on the add node; this is a documentation item, not a fault dispatch
Channel 3 Margin: (2.4×10⁻³ − 2.1×10⁻³) / 2.4×10⁻³ = 12.5% margin. This is dangerously low. Any additional OSNR degradation of ~0.5 dB could push the channel above FEC threshold. Enhanced monitoring is mandatory.
Ch 5 Crossing Time Calculation: Current OSNR = 21.0 dB; target = 14.0 dB; remaining delta = 7.0 dB; rate = 0.292 dB/hr; hours to crossing = 7.0 / 0.292 = ~24 hours from 23:59 = approximately 00:00 the following morning. The system will begin taking uncorrectable errors well before that if the Q-factor crosses below ~8.5 dB.
Sample Shift Handover Note (Ch 5): "Channel 5 OSNR has declined from 28.0 dB to 21.0 dB over the past 24 hours at a rate of ~0.29 dB/hr; no increase in span loss has been observed, suggesting EDFA pump degradation at ILA 2 or ILA 3. Current Q-factor is 10.4 dB with 4.4 dB margin to minimum; at current rate, OSNR will cross 14 dB threshold in approximately 24 hours. Escalate to optical lead immediately; dispatch vendor TAC and arrange maintenance window for ILA pump inspection within 8 hours." |
|---|

| Lab 1.2: Trending Analysis Exercise
Type: Trend Interpretation     Target Time: 20 minutes     Deliverable: 4-Wavelength Trend Report with Forecast |
|---|

**SCENARIO BRIEF**
You are reviewing the monthly performance report for a 32-channel metro DWDM backbone. The following 30-day Q-factor dataset has been extracted from the NMS historian for four wavelengths of interest. System minimum Q-factor alarm threshold is 10.0 dB. Engineering minimum (with margin) is 11.5 dB. The FEC hard limit is 8.5 dB. Characterize each trend and forecast risk.

#### Given Data — 30-Day Q-Factor Trend (dB), Daily Readings

| Day | WL-A (dB) | WL-B (dB) | WL-C (dB) | WL-D (dB) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 13.2 | 14.1 | 13.5 | 12.8 |
| 5 | 13.0 | 13.8 | 13.4 | 14.4 |
| 10 | 13.3 | 13.4 | 13.6 | 12.3 |
| 15 | 13.1 | 13.0 | 13.5 | 14.1 |
| 20 | 13.4 | 12.5 | 13.4 | 13.9 |
| 22 | 13.2 | 12.2 | 10.1 | 14.6 |
| 25 | 13.0 | 12.0 | 10.2 | 12.0 |
| 30 | 13.1 | 11.8 | 10.0 | 14.8 |

#### Worksheet Steps
- Pattern Classification: For each of the four wavelengths, identify the degradation pattern: stable / monotonic decline / sudden step change / oscillation. Cite specific data points to support your classification.
- Root Cause Hypothesis — WL-B: Monotonic decline over 30 days. List the four most likely causes in descending probability order. What distinguishes pump degradation from connector degradation in trending data?
- Root Cause Hypothesis — WL-C: Sudden 3.4 dB step drop at day 22, then stable. What are three possible causes of a sudden permanent step change? What PM data would you request to narrow it down?
- Root Cause Hypothesis — WL-D: Oscillation between 12.0 and 14.8 dB with ~7-day period. What is the most common cause of periodic Q-factor oscillation? Hint: consider diurnal and weekly thermal cycles, traffic loading changes, and protection switching patterns.
- Forecast — WL-B: Using a linear regression approximation, estimate the rate of Q-factor decline for WL-B. At that rate, on what day does WL-B cross the engineering minimum (11.5 dB)? When does it cross the alarm threshold (10.0 dB)? Show your calculation.
- Prioritization: Rank the four wavelengths by urgency of action required. Assign each a recommended action: Monitor / 4-hour enhanced monitoring / Maintenance ticket within 7 days / Emergency dispatch.

| ▶ Architect Benchmark
Target time: 20 minutes. All four patterns correctly classified. WL-B linear rate correctly calculated as approximately −0.077 dB/day. Engineering minimum crossing identified at approximately day 37. WL-C step change at day 22 correctly identified as candidate for physical event (splice failure, connector re-termination, or protection switch to a higher-loss path). WL-D oscillation linked to thermal cycling or periodic protection switch events. |
|---|

| Answer Key — Lab 1.2
Pattern Classification: WL-A = Stable (13.2 ± 0.3 dB, no trend); WL-B = Monotonic decline (14.1 to 11.8 dB over 30 days, consistent slope); WL-C = Sudden step change (13.5 → 10.1 dB at day 22, then stable at ~10.1 dB); WL-D = Oscillation (~7-day period, amplitude ≈ 2.8 dB peak-to-peak).
WL-B Forecast: Total decline = 14.1 − 11.8 = 2.3 dB over 30 days. Rate = 2.3/30 = 0.0767 dB/day. From day 30 value of 11.8 dB to engineering minimum 11.5 dB: Δ = 0.3 dB / 0.0767 = ~4 more days → crosses engineering minimum at approximately day 34. To alarm threshold 10.0 dB: Δ = 1.8 dB / 0.0767 = ~23 more days → day 53. Maintenance window should be scheduled within the next 7 days to prevent service degradation.
WL-C Step Change: A sudden, permanent 3.4 dB drop suggests: (1) fiber splice failure or degraded connector (physical IL increase on the optical path); (2) protection switch to a higher-loss secondary path; (3) WSS attenuation setting erroneously changed during a provisioning or maintenance activity. Request: span loss OTDR re-measurement from day 22, protection switch event log, and provisioning change log for that date.
WL-D Oscillation: 7-day period is characteristic of weekly traffic loading changes (some systems use power leveling that responds to aggregate load), weekly maintenance window activities that trigger protection switches, or thermal cycling driven by HVAC shutdown cycles in a specific equipment room. Obtain rack temperature logs and protection switch event log; correlate to the oscillation cycle.
Priority Ranking: WL-C first (at engineering minimum, one fault from threshold — emergency dispatch within 24 h); WL-B second (maintenance ticket within 7 days, 4-hour enhanced monitoring immediately); WL-D third (root cause investigation, monitor for another 7-day cycle); WL-A fourth (log as healthy, standard 15-min monitoring). |

| MODULE 2 — Alarm Triage Drills |
|---|

Alarm triage is the highest-pressure skill in optical operations. When 112 alarms arrive simultaneously, the wrong move is to start resolving them from the top of the list. The right move is to find the one root cause that explains all of them — and then act on that one. Every drill in this module is designed to build the pattern recognition required to do that within the architect benchmark time.

| Drill 2.1: Single Fiber Cut — Alarm Correlation
Type: Alarm Triage     Target Time: 90 seconds (root cause); 5 minutes (full correlation tree)     Deliverable: Root Cause + Alarm Tree |
|---|

| 2 | 03:47:12.043 | N4 | Span-N3-N4-RX | LOS | CRITICAL |
| 3 | 03:47:12.050 | N4 | Pre-Amp-N4 | INPUT-POWER-FAIL | MAJOR |
| 4–99 | 03:47:12.055–03:47:24 | N4, N3, N1, N2, N5, N6, N7, N8 | WL-001 to WL-096 (all channels), OTN AIS, Client AIS, APS switch events, NMS topology | Various MAJOR/MINOR | — |

| Drill 2.2: EDFA Failure — Alarm Storm
Type: Root Cause Analysis     Target Time: 3 minutes     Deliverable: Root Cause + Impact Scope + First Action |

**SCENARIO BRIEF**
Seven-span 100G DWDM system, 32 channels. In-line amplifier at Span 4 (ILA-4, located at an unmanned regeneration site) has failed. 31 alarms generated. Service is degraded — some channels have elevated BER, some are fine. You receive the alarm list at 02:33:18.

#### Abbreviated Alarm List (31 Alarms)

| Alarm | Object | Type | Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| A1 | ILA-4 | AMPLIFIER-FAIL | Root event — pump laser failure |
| A2–A17 | WL-01 to WL-16 (lower band) | OSNR-DEGRADED, Q-BELOW-THRESHOLD | 16 channels heavily affected — significant gain reduction |
| A18–A25 | WL-17 to WL-24 (mid-band) | OSNR-WARN, BER-ELEVATED | 8 channels partially affected — gain tilt causing mid-band rise |
| A26–A29 | WL-25 to WL-28 (upper band) | OPWR-HIGH | 4 channels showing POWER HIGH — anomalous |
| A30 | N5 (downstream node) | OTN-AIS | AIS propagated for 16 heavily-affected channels |
| A31 | NMS | ILA-4-UNREACHABLE | OSC dropped after EDFA failure |

#### Worksheet Steps
- Root Cause: Identify root cause alarm. In one sentence, state what happened physically at ILA-4.
- Explain the Upper Band Power Anomaly: Alarms A26–A29 show four upper-band channels with OPWR-HIGH. Why would an amplifier failure cause some channels to show high power? (Answer: gain tilt — a partial pump failure causes the EDFA gain spectrum to tilt; lower-band channels lose gain while upper-band channels temporarily experience higher relative gain before total saturation response kicks in.)
- Impact Scope: Which channels are at risk of data loss right now? Which are degraded but still within FEC? Which are anomalously high but not at data risk?
- First Action Decision: At 02:33:18, you have three options: (a) call the NOC and notify all customers; (b) log into the NE remotely to verify ILA-4 status; (c) call the field team for ILA-4 dispatch immediately. Which do you do first, second, and third? Justify the sequencing.
- OSC Failure Implication: Alarm A31 shows ILA-4 is unreachable. What operational capability have you lost because the OSC is down? What is the workaround for remote monitoring of ILA-4 optical parameters?

| ▶ Architect Benchmark

First action: log into ILA-4 via out-of-band management (if OSC is down, use DCN or secondary management path) to confirm pump status before dispatching. Commissioning is where architecture meets reality. Every design assumption gets tested against actual fiber loss, real amplifier behavior, and physical connector quality. **SCENARIO BRIEF**
A new 120 km span has been installed between Node 7 and Node 8, with a single ILA at the 60 km midpoint (ILA-5). This is a brownfield extension of an existing 80-channel C-band DWDM network. The span will carry 32 initial wavelengths, with capacity for full 80-channel loading. You are the lead commissioning architect.

#### Commissioning Sequence
**Step 1** **OTDR Measurement Verification**
Measure span loss on both fiber pairs (East and West) using OTDR at 1,550 nm. Compare to design loss budget. Design: 60 km × 0.22 dB/km + connectors/splices = 13.2 dB + 1.5 dB = 14.7 dB per fiber section (total 29.4 dB for full 120 km, 14.7 dB per ILA section). Acceptance criterion: measured loss ≤ design + 0.5 dB tolerance.
Provided OTDR measurements for the Lab:

| Section | Direction | Design Loss (dB) | Measured Loss (dB) | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| N7 to ILA-5 (60 km) | East | 14.7 | 14.4 | PASS |
| N7 to ILA-5 (60 km) | West | 14.7 | 14.9 | PASS |
| ILA-5 to N8 (60 km) | East | 14.7 | 17.1 | FAIL — 2.4 dB excess |
| ILA-5 to N8 (60 km) | West | 14.7 | 14.6 | PASS |

**Step 2** **OSC Bring-Up and Link Verification**
Provision the Optical Supervisory Channel (1,510 nm or 1,625 nm per system design) on both spans. Verify OSC power within −20 to −30 dBm at each receive end. Verify NE-to-NE communication via OSC (ping across OSC management VLAN). Expected: OSC UP on both N7↔ILA-5 and ILA-5↔N8 links within 60 seconds of provisioning.
**Step 3** **Booster Amplifier Gain Optimization at N7**
Set booster gain to achieve target launch power for the 120 km span. With ILA-5 at 60 km, target launch power from booster: +3 dBm per channel (for initial 32 channels). Set gain = target launch − input power. Verify gain tilt ≤ ±0.3 dB/THz.
**Step 4** **ILA-5 Gain and Tilt Optimization**
ILA-5 must compensate for the N7→ILA-5 span loss (14.4 dB East, 14.9 dB West). Set ILA-5 gain to 14.5 dB (average) with automatic tilt correction. Verify output power per channel at ILA-5 within ±0.5 dBm of design. **Issue: The East fiber ILA-5→N8 is 2.4 dB worse than design (Step 1 finding). How does this affect ILA-5 gain setting?**
**Step 5** **Pre-Amp Optimization at N8**
Set pre-amp gain at N8 to equalize input power at the receiver. Target receive power at N8 coherent modem input: −15.0 ± 1.0 dBm. With the East fiber 2.4 dB excess loss (from Step 1), verify that pre-amp can compensate without exceeding its maximum gain limit (typically 30 dB for metro pre-amps).
**Step 6** **OSC PM Verification**
Collect OSC PM for 15 minutes. Verify OSC packet loss < 0.01% and OSC latency < 1 ms per hop. This confirms the supervisory plane is stable before loading traffic wavelengths.
**Step 7** **First Wavelength Provisioning and Power Verification**
Provision Channel 33 (1,550.12 nm) as the first test wavelength. Verify: TX power at N7 = +1.0 dBm; RX power at N8 = −14.0 to −22.0 dBm (within modem input range); OSNR at N8 ≥ design minimum + 3 dB margin. Accept this wavelength before loading additional channels.
**Step 8** **Full Channel Loading and OSNR Verification**
Load all 32 initial wavelengths. Verify OSNR on all 32 channels ≥ system minimum. Verify gain tilt on ILA-5 remains ≤ ±0.3 dB/THz under full 32-channel load. Collect 15-minute PM interval for all channels before proceeding.
**Step 9** **Protection Commissioning and Switch Test**
Configure 1+1 OLP protection on all 32 wavelengths. Execute manual protection switch on each circuit and verify: switch time ≤ 50 ms; OSNR on protect path ≥ system minimum; revert-to-working successful.
**Step 10** **Acceptance Criteria Sign-Off**
All channels: OSNR ≥ design − 0.5 dB; pre-FEC BER ≤ 1.0×10⁻³; protection switch ≤ 50 ms; span loss within budget. Sign commissioning log; obtain customer witness signature if required by contract.

| MODULE 5 — Troubleshooting Drills |
|---|

Troubleshooting at Level 3 is hypothesis-driven, evidence-confirmed, and resolution-documented. You form a hypothesis from the first three data points, test it with the next two commands, and either confirm or discard it. You do not chase alarms. You chase root causes. These drills are structured to build exactly that discipline.

| Troubleshooting Drill 5.1: Slow OSNR Degradation
Type: Trending Fault Investigation     Target Time: 20 minutes     Deliverable: Completed Troubleshooting Worksheet |
|---|

**SCENARIO BRIEF**
Channel 34 on a 640 km, 8-span DWDM route has been quietly degrading. Six weeks ago, OSNR was 26.0 dB. Today it is 20.1 dB — a 5.9 dB decline. No critical alarms have fired (minimum threshold is 15.0 dB). The PM trending function flagged this on the weekly performance review. No customer impact yet. You have the data below.

#### Given Data

| Week | OSNR Ch34 (dB) | Span Loss (all spans, avg dB) | ILA-3 Pump A Current (mA) | ILA-3 Pump B Current (mA) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Week 0 (baseline) | 26.0 | 17.8 | 480 | 478 |
| Week 1 | 25.4 | 17.8 | 488 | 479 |
| Week 2 | 24.8 | 17.9 | 502 | 480 |
| Week 3 | 24.1 | 17.8 | 518 | 481 |
| Week 4 | 23.2 | 17.8 | 539 | 479 |
| Week 5 | 22.1 | 17.9 | 558 | 480 |
| Week 6 | 20.1 | 17.8 | 582 | 480 |

#### Worksheet Steps
- Span Loss vs. OSNR Relationship: Span loss is stable at 17.8–17.9 dB across all 6 weeks. OSNR is declining steadily. What does stable span loss combined with declining OSNR indicate? (Answer: The optical fiber is intact. The degradation is originating within an active component — almost certainly an amplifier.)
- EDFA Pump Degradation Indicator: ILA-3 Pump A current has risen from 480 mA (Week 0) to 582 mA (Week 6) — a 21% increase. Pump B remains stable at ~480 mA. What does rising pump current with declining OSNR confirm? Explain the physical mechanism. (Answer: Pump A laser efficiency is declining — it requires more current to maintain the same output power. The AGC (Automatic Gain Control) is increasing the drive current to compensate. This is a classic sign of pump laser end-of-life degradation.)
- Span Identification: You have per-span OSNR data from an OSA sweep. Describe how you would use per-span OSNR measurements to confirm that ILA-3 (Span 3) is the source of the degradation rather than ILA-5 or ILA-7.
- OSA Confirmation: You connect an OSA at the ILA-3 output. What specific OSA measurement confirms pump degradation versus fiber connector contamination? (Answer: OSNR measured immediately at ILA-3 output — if OSNR is low at the amplifier output itself, the fault is the amplifier NF, not downstream fiber.)
- Remediation: The remediation is pump module replacement at ILA-3. This is an unmanned ILA site. List the steps to arrange the maintenance: spare assessment, access coordination, service protection verification, maintenance window scheduling, and post-replacement acceptance testing.

| Answer Key — Drill 5.1
Pump A current progression: 480 → 582 mA (+21% in 6 weeks). In a typical 976 nm pump laser, current efficiency declines as the semiconductor active region ages. The AGC compensates by increasing drive current. When pump current reaches ~95% of rated maximum (typically 600–650 mA), efficiency drops rapidly and the pump enters terminal degradation. At the current rate (approximately +17 mA/week), ILA-3 Pump A will reach 600 mA in approximately 1 week. Schedule emergency replacement within 48–72 hours. Current OSNR margin: 20.1 − 15.0 = 5.1 dB above threshold. At current degradation rate (~1.0 dB/week), threshold will be crossed in approximately 5 weeks — but pump terminal failure will likely cause a rapid collapse well before that. |
|---|

| Answer Key — Drill 5.2 (Hypothesis Ranking)

The DSP's coherent engine has thermal sensitivity thresholds; above a certain junction temperature, phase tracking stability degrades, leading to BER elevation without full LOS. **SCENARIO BRIEF**
A 400G coherent modem on a 2,200 km transnational route intermittently loses carrier lock, dropping traffic for 200–400 ms before re-acquiring. Events have occurred on 3 occasions in the past 30 days, each coinciding with a weather event (high wind storm) in the central section of the fiber route. The customer is a Tier 1 bank. Every 200 ms drop is a reportable incident.

#### Given Data

| Parameter | Value | Specification / Limit |
|---|---|---|
| Route length | 2,200 km | — |
| Fiber type | G.652.A (legacy, installed 1998) | PMD coefficient: ≤0.5 ps/√km (G.652.A limit) |
| Modem DGD tolerance | 30 ps max (400G 16QAM specification) | — |
| Measured DGD (calm weather) | 12.4 ps (average), 18.1 ps (max) | OK — within spec |
| Measured DGD (during storm events) | 31.8 ps, 34.2 ps, 29.7 ps | Exceeds 30 ps limit on 2 of 3 events |
| CD estimate (all conditions) | Stable, within ±50 ps/nm | OK |
| Lock loss events correlated with DGD >30 ps | 2 of 3 events | — |

#### Worksheet Steps
- PMD Coefficient Calculation: Using the maximum observed DGD during storm conditions (34.2 ps) as a proxy for the fiber's statistical DGD extreme, estimate the PMD coefficient for this route. Is it within G.652.A specification? Show your calculation. (PMD coefficient = DGD / √L; L = 2,200 km.)
- DGD vs. Modem Tolerance: The modem's stated DGD tolerance is 30 ps. The measured DGD during storms reached 34.2 ps. By how many ps does the maximum observed DGD exceed the modem's tolerance? Is this a fiber problem, a modem problem, or both?
- Architect Options — Evaluate Three:
        
        For each option, evaluate: customer service impact, cost category, implementation time, and risk accepted.
- Option A: Accept the risk and monitor — document the 3 events as weather-correlation exceptions, implement alert at DGD = 25 ps (90-second advance warning), and notify the customer of weather-related service risk.
- Option B: Reroute the 400G circuit to an alternate fiber pair with lower PMD — requires network survey of available alternatives; may require new equipment at both ends.
- Option C: Downgrade modulation from 16QAM to QPSK on this route — QPSK has higher DGD tolerance (~50 ps for typical 100G QPSK DSP); however, this halves the 400G capacity to 200G, requiring either a second circuit or capacity reduction.
- DSP Firmware Upgrade: A newer DSP firmware (v3.2.1) claims improved PMD tolerance of up to 35 ps DGD. This would keep the modem within spec for 2 of the 3 observed events (34.2 ps and 29.7 ps would be within new limit; 31.8 ps would also be OK). What are the risks of upgrading firmware on a live financial circuit? How do you manage the upgrade safely?
- [DELIVERABLE] Customer Incident Report: Write a professional incident report for the bank customer covering all three lock-loss events. The report must include: incident summary (non-technical), technical root cause determination, service impact quantification (seconds of outage per event), investigation steps taken, options being evaluated, and recommended next steps. Maximum 400 words. No internal jargon — this goes to the bank's CTO.

| ▶ Architect Benchmark

Recommended option: pursue firmware upgrade (v3.2.1) as immediate action with proper maintenance window management, combined with alternative fiber survey as a 90-day parallel workstream. **SCENARIO BRIEF**
The Chicago–New York ultra-low latency DWDM circuit (circuit ID: CHI-NYC-ULL-001) serves an HFT (High-Frequency Trading) customer with a contractual RTT guarantee of <4.0 ms. At 09:31:14 EST this morning, the customer's latency monitoring system detected 4.6 ms RTT — 0.6 ms above the SLA. No optical alarms. Service is up. OSNR is healthy. But the SLA is breached and the financial penalty clock started at 09:31:14.

#### Given Data — Route Analysis

| Segment | Normal Path | Current Path | Equipment Delay (ms) | Fiber Delay (ms) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Chicago hub → Mid-1 | ROADM direct express | ROADM direct express | 0.08 | 0.62 |
| Mid-1 → Mid-2 | Express pass-through | Add/drop + re-add via ROADM-HUB-B | 0.31 (extra ROADM) | 0.82 |
| Mid-2 → New York | ROADM direct express | ROADM direct express | 0.08 | 0.62 |
| OSC routing change | Normal OSC path | Alternate OSC path (longer) | — | 0.30 added |
| Total RTT (one-way × 2) | 3.96 ms | 4.62 ms | — | — |

#### Worksheet Steps
- Latency Accounting: The extra 0.66 ms RTT breaks down as: extra ROADM switching delay (0.31 ms × 2 = 0.62 ms) + OSC path change effect (0.04 ms × 2 = 0.08 ms) ≈ 0.70 ms additional one-way, but RTT shows 0.6 ms. Reconcile this discrepancy. (Answer: The OSC path change does not directly add to the client signal latency — it only affects management plane timing. The 0.6 ms RTT increase is entirely attributable to the extra ROADM add/drop at Hub B.)
- Root Cause: How did traffic end up routing through the extra ROADM at Hub B? What NMS or provisioning event would cause a previously direct-express wavelength to be re-routed through an add/drop node? (Hint: check the NMS routing change log and any recent maintenance activities at Hub B.)
- Restoration: Write the specific NMS operation and CLI commands required to restore the circuit to the original direct-express path through Hub B. Is a traffic interruption required? If yes, how long? If no, explain why hitless restoration is possible on this platform.
- SLA Breach Calculation: The breach duration is from 09:31:14 to the time of restoration. For every 15 minutes of SLA breach, the customer contract specifies a 2% monthly bill credit, capped at 25%. Calculate the financial exposure if restoration takes 45 minutes.
- [DELIVERABLE] RCA Report: Write a complete Root Cause Analysis report for this event in customer-facing format. Sections: Event Summary, Timeline, Root Cause, Impact Quantification, Corrective Actions Taken, Preventive Measures. Maximum 350 words. No internal acronyms without definitions.

| Vertical Capstone 6.2: 5G Fronthaul DWDM — Synchronization Fault
Type: Synchronization Fault Analysis     Target Time: 45 minutes     Deliverable: Fix Design + Configuration Change |
|---|

**SCENARIO BRIEF**
5G small cells served by an eCPRI fronthaul DWDM ring are showing intermittent connectivity loss every 6–8 hours. The mobile operator's NOC has escalated to you. The fronthaul ring has 4 ROADM nodes (RN-1 through RN-4). eCPRI Class C synchronization requires latency asymmetry <1 µs between the east and west ring directions. Investigation reveals a latency asymmetry of 3 µs at RN-3.

#### Given Data — Latency Measurements Per Hop

| Node-to-Node | East Direction (µs) | West Direction (µs) | Asymmetry (µs) | Limit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| RN-1 → RN-2 | 82 | 82 | 0 | <1 µs OK |
| RN-2 → RN-3 | 79 | 82 | 3 | FAIL — 3 µs |
| RN-3 → RN-4 | 81 | 81 | 0 | <1 µs OK |
| RN-4 → RN-1 | 80 | 80 | 0 | <1 µs OK |

#### Worksheet Steps
- Asymmetry Source: The 3 µs asymmetry is between RN-2 and RN-3 in the east direction (79 µs) vs. west direction (82 µs). A 3 µs asymmetry at the speed of light in fiber (approximately 5 µs/km) corresponds to approximately 600 m of additional fiber path in one direction. What are three possible causes of this 600 m equivalent path length difference?
- PTP Synchronization Impact: Explain why a 3 µs latency asymmetry causes IEEE 1588v2 PTP synchronization to fail for 5G eCPRI Class C applications. What is the timing error introduced by a 3 µs asymmetry into the PTP slave clock estimate?
- ROADM Optical Path Equalization: One fix is to add an optical delay coil (fiber spool) in the faster direction to equalize latency to within 1 µs. Calculate the required delay coil length: target is to reduce asymmetry from 3 µs to <0.5 µs; 3 µs − 0.5 µs = 2.5 µs of delay needed; at 5 µs/km propagation delay, required coil = 2.5 µs / (5 µs/km) = 500 m of fiber. Is this a practical solution?
- PTP Configuration Fix: An alternative is to configure the PTP master at RN-1 to apply a static latency correction (asymmetry compensation) in the PTP dataset. Write the configuration concept for the PTP asymmetry correction parameter. What is the risk of using software compensation rather than physical equalization?
- Design Fix Recommendation: Compare both solutions (physical fiber delay coil vs. PTP software correction) and recommend one. Justify your recommendation in terms of operational risk, future maintainability, and standards compliance for ITU-T G.8271.1.

**Lessons Learned:**
- PM trending data is only useful if it is reviewed on a cadence that allows action before failure. A 6-week warning existed in the data; a weekly review cycle was too slow to act on it.
- MTBF is a guideline, not a guarantee. Equipment must be tracked against actual hours of operation, not assumed to be within spec because it was installed within a certain year.
- Protect path performance must be continuously monitored, not assumed to be healthy because it is the "standby" path. When working path fails, the protect path becomes the only service path — it must be ready.
- Operational procedures must be living documents. A PM schedule that is 7 years old without revision is a liability, not a safeguard.
- The difference between a service outage and a service degradation event in this case was optical protection. Every circuit that lacked protection would have experienced a full outage for 4+ hours. Protection coverage must be audited and maintained as a first-order operational priority.

| Lab 7.3: Architect Design Decision Log
Type: Design Documentation Exercise     Target Time: 60 minutes     Deliverable: 12-Entry Decision Log |
|---|

**SCENARIO BRIEF**
You are the lead architect for a major metro DWDM ring upgrade. The existing ring is a 4-node, 40G DWDM system. The upgrade adds 400G capacity, two new ROADM nodes, and extends the ring by 80 km. Every significant design decision must be logged with full traceability. This log will be reviewed by the customer's technical committee.

#### [DELIVERABLE] Architect Decision Log — Metro Ring 400G Upgrade

| # | Decision Made | Options Considered | Chosen Option | Reasoning | Risk Accepted |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Modulation format for 400G circuits | 16QAM vs. QPSK vs. 8QAM | 16QAM (primary); QPSK fallback enabled | 16QAM provides 400G on single carrier within spectral plan; QPSK fallback retained for worst-span circuits exceeding OSNR budget | Reduced OSNR margin vs. QPSK; mitigated by requiring OSNR >24 dB for all 16QAM circuits |
| 2 | ROADM node architecture for 2 new nodes | Colorless/Directionless/Contentionless (CDC-F) vs. colorless-only vs. fixed-grid | CDC-F architecture | CDC-F provides full routing flexibility for future wavelength reassignment without physical fiber changes; ring expansion planned in 3 years | Higher initial cost (~40% premium over colorless-only); justified by 3-year NPV of reduced operational intervention |
| 3 | Protection scheme | 1+1 OLP vs. OADM ring protection vs. unprotected with restoration | 1+1 OLP for Tier 1 circuits; OADM ring protection for Tier 2 | Customer SLA requires 50 ms for Tier 1; ring protection meets 50 ms but requires all-node coordination; OLP simpler and more reliable for metro ring | OLP consumes 2× fiber capacity; accepted given ring capacity surplus |
| 4 | Amplifier type for 80 km ring extension | Standard EDFA vs. Raman hybrid vs. extended-reach EDFA | Extended-reach EDFA (low-NF, high-gain) | 80 km single span exceeds standard EDFA design range of 80 km with margin; low-NF EDFA provides 1.5 dB NF improvement, maintaining OSNR budget | Higher power consumption; EDFA module cost 30% higher than standard |
| 5 | Channel plan — 50 GHz vs. 100 GHz grid | 100 GHz (fixed grid, 96 channels) vs. 50 GHz (flex grid, up to 192 channels) | 50 GHz flex-grid | 400G coherent requires ~75 GHz of optical bandwidth; 50 GHz super-channel approach enables efficient 400G packing; future 800G planned | Requires all ROADMs to support flex-grid; confirmed at procurement phase |
| 6 | OSC wavelength | 1,510 nm vs. 1,625 nm vs. in-band pilot tone | 1,510 nm OSC | Existing ring uses 1,510 nm; inter-node compatibility maintained; OSC filters already installed at all existing nodes | None — continuity decision |
| 7 | NMS platform for new nodes | Extend existing multi-vendor NMS vs. deploy vendor-native NMS for new nodes vs. migrate all nodes to new platform | Extend existing multi-vendor NMS | Migration risk during active production ring operation is unacceptable; existing NMS has API integration with customer's OSS; incremental NMS license cost <10% of migration project cost | Multi-vendor NMS limitations in 400G coherent PM depth; workaround via vendor CLI audit scripts |
| 8 | Dispersion compensation strategy | DCF modules vs. all-electronic DSP compensation | All-electronic (DSP only) — no DCF | 400G coherent DSP handles CD compensation up to ±80,000 ps/nm; metro ring maximum CD is <20,000 ps/nm; DCF adds unnecessary IL and complexity | None — DSP compensation is now standard for coherent metro systems |
| 9 | Fiber type for 80 km extension | G.652.D (standard SMF) vs. G.654.E (ultra-low-loss) vs. G.655 (NZ-DSF) | G.654.E (ultra-low-loss fiber) | 80 km single span with high-capacity 400G requires minimum loss; G.654.E at 0.17 dB/km provides 4.2 dB improvement over G.652.D (0.20 dB/km) over 80 km — critical for OSNR budget | Higher fiber cost (≈2× vs. G.652.D); accepted as essential for span budget |
| 10 | TCA threshold philosophy | Aggressive (close to limit) vs. conservative (wide margin) vs. tiered (warning + alarm) | Tiered: Warning at 20% margin; Alarm at 10% margin above FEC limit | Aggressive TCAs on a 400G production ring cause alarm fatigue and risk of missed real events; tiered approach provides advance warning without excessive noise | Some degradation events may not alarm until 10% margin — mitigated by daily PM trending review |
| 11 | Power equalization strategy for full 80-channel load | Per-channel VOA equalization vs. gain tilt control only vs. hybrid | Hybrid: gain tilt control + per-channel VOA at ROADM add ports | Gain tilt control alone insufficient for ±1 dBm uniformity across 80 channels on non-uniform fiber loss spans; per-channel VOA at add ports provides ±0.3 dBm equalization without NF penalty | VOA calibration required at commissioning; documented in commissioning procedure |
| 12 | Latency budget for 5G eCPRI circuits on the ring | Standard ROADM latency (~300 µs per node) vs. low-latency ROADM mode (~150 µs per node) vs. dedicated express path | Low-latency ROADM mode for eCPRI circuits; dedicated express path bypassing 2 nodes for <1 ms circuits | 5G eCPRI Class C requires <100 µs one-way latency; standard ROADM mode at 4 nodes = 1.2 ms — unacceptable; low-latency mode at 4 nodes = 0.6 ms; express path for <100 µs requirement | Express path consumes dedicated fiber pair; accepted given 5G contract value |

| FINAL CAPSTONE — Level 3 Architect Certification Exam |
|---|

| ⚑ Certification Exam Notice
The Final Capstone is conducted under examination conditions. Part A is closed-book. Parts B and C are open to your workbook and portfolio only — no internet access, no peer consultation. Time limits are strictly enforced. The certification panel includes a minimum of two Level 3-certified architects. Results are pass/fail — there is no partial credit for incomplete answers. |
|---|

## Part A: Written Exam — Scenario-Based Questions
**Format:** 10 questions. Each question presents an operational scenario with PM data, CLI output, or an alarm list. Student must: diagnose the condition, recommend a specific action, and justify in 3–5 sentences. Closed-book. 60 minutes. Each question is worth 10 points; minimum passing score: 70/100.
### Sample Exam Questions (Full Exam Delivered by Certification Panel)
**Q1.** A 100G QPSK wavelength on a 1,200 km route shows OSNR = 16.2 dB (system minimum = 14.0 dB) and pre-FEC BER = 3.9×10⁻³ (GFEC limit = 4.5×10⁻³). Span loss on all 15 spans is within ±0.3 dB of design. ILA-8 pump current has risen from 480 mA to 561 mA over the past 4 weeks. Diagnose the situation, state the root cause, and recommend action with urgency classification.
**Q2.** During a routine CLI audit of a Nokia 1830 PSS node, you observe: EDFA East gain deviation = −2.1 dB; Pump A current = 488 mA (nominal); Pump B current = 8 mA (nominal: 490 mA); OSNR on all channels 1.8–2.2 dB below design. No active alarms in NMS (alarm threshold = 3.0 dB below design). What is happening? Why hasn't the NMS alarmed? What is your immediate action?
**Q3.** You receive 62 simultaneous alarms at 03:22 AM. The first three alarms are: (1) LOS on Span-N6-N7 at 03:22:01.011; (2) Pre-amp input-power-fail at N7 at 03:22:01.018; (3) 58 wavelength OPWR-LOW alarms across N7 at 03:22:01.022–03:22:02.140. Identify the root cause. Classify all other 61 alarms. State your first action in the next 90 seconds.
**Q4.** A financial trading customer reports their Chicago–New York RTT has increased from 3.92 ms to 4.48 ms. No optical alarms are active. OSNR is healthy. The NMS shows the circuit route unchanged. How do you investigate this? What specific CLI command or NMS query reveals a routing change that the NMS route display is not showing?
**Q5.** You are commissioning a new span and the OTDR shows a discrete reflection event (return loss = 45 dB, insertion loss = 0.8 dB) at 34.2 km from the Node-A end of a 70 km span. The design shows no splice or connector at that location. Identify three possible causes. How do you proceed with commissioning — accept, investigate, or reject? Justify.
## Part B: Live Simulation — 4-Hour Production Exercise
### Exercise Overview
The candidate is given access to either real lab equipment or a high-fidelity simulation environment running a 6-node DWDM network with 96 channels. The certification panel injects 6 faults over the 4-hour window. The candidate must triage, diagnose, resolve, and document each fault.
#### Fault Injection Schedule (Panel Reference — Not Revealed to Candidate)

| Fault # | Injection Time | Fault Type | Target Resolution Time | Scoring Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| F-1 | T+00:15 | Fiber cut with successful APS (verify within 3 min) | 3 minutes | 15% |
| F-2 | T+00:45 | EDFA pump degradation (slow — in trending data) | 15 minutes | 20% |
| F-3 | T+01:30 | False alarm — NMS stale state on a high-priority circuit | 10 minutes | 15% |
| F-4 | T+02:00 | Wavelength blocked at ROADM — NMS/NE sync failure | 20 minutes | 20% |
| F-5 | T+02:45 | Intermittent BER spike — thermal correlation | 25 minutes | 15% |
| F-6 | T+03:15 | Multi-failure: fiber cut + EDFA alarm simultaneously | 30 minutes | 15% |

#### Scoring Criteria — Part B
- Root cause accuracy (40%): Did the candidate identify the correct root cause for each fault?
- Resolution within benchmark time (30%): Was the root cause identified within the target resolution time?
- Correct action sequence (20%): Did the candidate execute the right actions in the right order? (No unnecessary commands; no alarm suppression before root cause confirmed.)
- Documentation (10%): Is the incident log complete, accurate, and usable by the next shift? No credit for undocumented resolutions.

| ▶ Architect Certification Standard — Part B
All 6 faults resolved within 4 hours. Customer SLA maintained for all circuits with optical protection. Minimum score: 75%. A candidate who correctly diagnoses 5 of 6 faults but exceeds the benchmark time on 2 may still pass if documentation is exemplary and the incorrect fault is the lowest-weight item (F-1 or F-3). |
|---|

## Part C: Architecture Defense
### Format
The candidate presents their Level 2 capstone design and their Level 3 operational plan (from Capstone 6.4 and Module 7 deliverables) to a panel of two Level 3-certified architects. The presentation is 20 minutes. The panel then asks 10 challenge questions testing operational readiness. Total time: 60 minutes.
### Challenge Question Categories
- Technical Accuracy (4 questions): "In your design, you chose 16QAM for all circuits. What is the Q-factor required for error-free 16QAM after SD-FEC? How did you verify your OSNR budget provides this?"
- Operational Judgment (3 questions): "If ILA-6 fails at 02:00 AM and your protect path OSNR is only 1.5 dB above minimum, at what point do you escalate from 'monitor' to 'emergency dispatch' — and how do you make that call with no additional data?"
- Communication Clarity (2 questions): "Read your executive summary from the Capstone 6.4 RCA report. If the bank's CTO asked you: 'Was my data at risk?' — what do you say? In plain English. In 30 seconds."
- Network Hardening (1 question): "Of your 5 hardening recommendations, which one would have prevented tonight's incident most definitively — and how do you prioritize it given a capital budget that only covers 2 of your 5 recommendations?"
### Scoring Rubric — Part C

| Dimension | Excellent (90–100%) | Acceptable (70–89%) | Fails (<70%) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Technical Accuracy | All values cited from memory; no corrections required from panel | Minor errors corrected when challenged; fundamentals solid | Fundamental misunderstanding of OSNR, FEC, or protection; cannot be corrected by prompting |
| Operational Judgment | Decisions are immediate, correctly sequenced, and demonstrably experience-driven | Correct decisions but hesitant; requires prompting on sequencing | Wrong decisions, wrong sequence, or inability to make a decision under uncertainty |
| Communication Clarity | Panel could hand this RCA to a customer CFO without editing | Clear but contains minor jargon; one revision needed | Technical language used throughout; customer would not understand |
| Portfolio Completeness | All 8+ required deliverables present, complete, and timestamped | 6–7 deliverables; minor gaps | Fewer than 6 deliverables; or critical deliverables missing |
